era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in terminal decline; Alaric I leads the Visigoths and will sack Rome in 410 CE. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire under Arcadius remains administratively intact but faces Persian and barbarian pressures. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is fragmenting; the Northern and Southern Dynasties period is beginning. Christianity is now the dominant religion across the Mediterranean world following Constantine's conversion and the Council of Nicaea (325 CE). This is a pivotal moment of civilisational transition in Europe and Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Mesoamerica (Maya records exist but are sparse for this specific year)
Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (Germanic and Celtic regions; oral tradition only)

events.title: Alaric I proclaimed King of the Visigoths
events.description: Alaric I, a Gothic military commander within the Roman army, is proclaimed king by his followers, formalising Visigothic independence from Roman authority. This marks a critical moment in the fragmentation of Roman military control and the rise of barbarian kingdoms within former imperial territory. Alaric will lead raids into Greece and eventually march on Italy, culminating in the sack of Rome in 410 CE. His elevation signals the shift from 'barbarian foederati' (allied troops) to autonomous political entities.

sources.name: Zosimus, New History (written early 6th century, drawing on earlier sources)
Jordanes, Getica (6th century compilation of Gothic history)

events.certainty_note: The exact year of Alaric's proclamation is debated; sources place it between 395–401 CE. 400 CE is a conventional date used by modern historians, but contemporary documentation is absent. The event itself is well-attested in later sources.
cross_references: 395-001 (Division of Roman Empire under Arcadius and Honorius)

events.title: Stilicho's military campaigns against Alaric in Greece
events.description: Stilicho, the half-Vandal Roman general and regent for the Western Roman Emperor Honorius, leads military campaigns to contain Alaric's Visigothic forces in Greece. Stilicho achieves tactical successes but fails to eliminate the Gothic threat, partly due to political divisions in the Western Empire and his own precarious position. These campaigns represent the last major Roman military effort to maintain control of the Balkans and Greece against barbarian incursion.

sources.name: Claudian, panegyric poems on Stilicho (contemporary or near-contemporary, c. 400–404 CE)

events.certainty_note: Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence, though they are propagandistic in nature. The exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty fragmentation accelerates in China
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty, which had ruled southern China since 317 CE, enters its final phase of collapse. Regional warlords and military strongmen increasingly challenge central authority. The period of the Northern and Southern Dynasties (420–589 CE) is imminent. This fragmentation reflects broader instability following the decline of Han-era centralised authority and the rise of competing regional powers in post-classical China.
key_figures: Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)
Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song Dynasty, b. 363 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang (11th century)

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Eastern Jin decline is well-documented in Chinese historiography. Specific events in 400 CE are less precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Augustine of Hippo completes The City of God (composition period)
events.description: Augustine of Hippo, one of the most influential Christian theologians, is in the process of writing The City of God (De civitate Dei), a monumental work of Christian apologetics and philosophy begun after the Visigothic sack of Rome in 410 CE. While the work's composition extends to c. 426 CE, Augustine's theological output in 400 CE reflects the intellectual ferment of late antique Christianity. His writings profoundly shape medieval and early modern Christian thought.
key_figures: Augustine of Hippo (Christian theologian and bishop, 354–430 CE)

sources.name: Augustine's own writings and letters (primary texts)
Possidius, Life of Augustine (written early 5th century)

events.certainty_note: Augustine's life and works are extensively documented. The City of God was begun after 410 CE, so this entry refers to Augustine's broader theological activity in 400 CE rather than the specific work.
cross_references: 410-001 (Sack of Rome, which prompted The City of God)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Alaric's proclamation as king to 400 CE is not universally accepted; some scholars place it as late as 401 CE or as early as 395 CE. The significance attributed to this event in narratives of 'barbarian invasion' has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the continuity of Roman military structures and the integration of Gothic elites into Roman administrative systems rather than a simple 'invasion' narrative. Augustine's major works were not yet written in 400 CE; attributing intellectual significance to this year based on works composed later risks anachronism.
historiographic_note: The year 400 CE falls at a critical juncture in Western historiography: the transition from classical antiquity to the early medieval period. However, documentation is uneven. Roman sources (Claudian, Zosimus) are biased toward military and political elites and toward the Western Empire's perspective. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. African and Asian sources outside the Roman and Chinese spheres are sparse. The 'decline and fall' narrative that dominated 19th- and 20th-century historiography has been substantially revised; modern scholarship emphasises transformation and continuity rather than catastrophic collapse. Surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and demographic changes are poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: Alaric's proclamation as king in 400 CE directly led to his march on Rome and the sack of 410 CE
Stilicho's campaigns against Alaric occurred in the same period as Alaric's consolidation of power
The fragmentation of Eastern Jin in 400 CE culminated in the founding of Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE
Augustine's theological work was part of the broader Christian intellectual response to the crisis of the Western Empire

era_context: The Western Roman Empire under Honorius and regent Stilicho is engaged in military campaigns against Alaric's Visigoths and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal court factionalism. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is in its final decades of decline. Christianity continues to consolidate institutional power across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of acute military and political instability in the Mediterranean and Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent (limited contemporary written sources)

events.title: Stilicho defeats Alaric at the Battle of the Frigidus River (or nearby engagement)
events.description: Stilicho, the Roman general and regent, achieves a significant military victory against Alaric's Visigothic forces, likely in the region of the Frigidus River (modern-day Slovenia/Croatia border). This victory temporarily halts Visigothic expansion and demonstrates Roman military capability, though it does not resolve the underlying political crisis. The battle is part of a series of engagements between 395–410 CE in which Stilicho attempts to contain barbarian threats while managing internal Roman political divisions.
events.certainty_note: The exact date and location of this engagement are debated. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence but are propagandistic. The battle may have occurred in 399 or 400 CE.
cross_references: 394-001 (Battle of the Frigidus River, 394 CE, which may be confused with this engagement)

events.title: Gainas' revolt and massacre of Gothic foederati in Constantinople
events.description: Gainas, a Gothic military commander in the Eastern Roman army, leads a revolt against the Eastern Roman government. The revolt is suppressed, and a massacre of Gothic soldiers and settlers in Constantinople follows. This event demonstrates the acute tensions between barbarian military elites integrated into the Roman army and the Roman civilian and ecclesiastical establishment. The massacre represents a violent rejection of Gothic military dominance in the Eastern Empire, contrasting with the Western Empire's more gradual accommodation of barbarian kingdoms.
events.certainty_note: The event is well-attested in multiple sources, though they are not contemporary. The exact date is sometimes given as 400 CE; 399 CE is also plausible.
cross_references: 400-001 (Alaric's proclamation as king in the West, contemporary with Eastern instability)

events.title: Faxian, Chinese Buddhist monk, begins pilgrimage to India
events.description: Faxian (法顯), a Chinese Buddhist monk, departs on a pilgrimage to India to obtain Buddhist scriptures and study at major monasteries. His journey, which will last until c. 412 CE, is one of the most significant early Buddhist pilgrimages and provides invaluable documentation of Central Asian and Indian geography, politics, and religious life in the early 5th century. Faxian's account (the Foguoji or 'Record of Buddhist Kingdoms') is a primary source for understanding the Silk Road and Buddhist networks.
key_figures: Faxian (Buddhist monk and pilgrim, c. 337–422 CE)

sources.name: Faxian's own account, Foguoji (Record of Buddhist Kingdoms), compiled after his return

events.certainty_note: The date of Faxian's departure is traditionally given as 399 CE, though some sources suggest 400 CE. The pilgrimage itself is well-documented in his own account.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Emperor Longxu's reign continues amid factional strife
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty under Emperor Longxu (r. 397–402 CE) faces increasing internal factional conflict among military strongmen and aristocratic families. The imperial court's authority is eroding as regional warlords consolidate power. This period of instability will culminate in the dynasty's collapse and the founding of the Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE. The fragmentation reflects broader post-Han patterns of regional autonomy and the rise of military aristocracies.
key_figures: Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)
Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song, b. 363 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The broad political trajectory is well-documented in Chinese historiography. Specific events in 399 CE are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Gainas' revolt is disputed; some sources place it in 400 CE rather than 399 CE. The significance of Gainas' revolt has been reinterpreted by modern scholarship: rather than a simple 'barbarian uprising,' it reflects complex factional politics within the Eastern Roman elite, with Gothic military commanders as one faction among many competing for power. The traditional narrative of 'barbarian invasion' obscures the integration of Gothic elites into Roman administrative and military structures. Faxian's pilgrimage date of 399 CE is conventional but not universally accepted; some sources suggest 400 CE.
historiographic_note: The year 399 CE is poorly documented in surviving sources. Most information comes from later compilations (Zosimus, Socrates Scholasticus) written 50–150 years after the events. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic panegyrics. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. The Eastern Roman Empire's perspective is filtered through ecclesiastical sources (John Chrysostom, Socrates Scholasticus), which emphasise religious and moral dimensions over political and military detail. Faxian's pilgrimage is well-documented in his own account, but this is a retrospective compilation. The year 399 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events occurring in this specific year is difficult to assess.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's victory in 399 CE was part of his broader campaign against Alaric continuing into 400 CE
Gainas' revolt in the East occurred during the same period as Alaric's consolidation in the West
Faxian's pilgrimage beginning in 399 CE culminated in his return to China in 412 CE
The factional strife in Eastern Jin in 399 CE led directly to the dynasty's collapse and Liu Song's founding in 420 CE

era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in acute military and political crisis. Stilicho continues campaigns against Alaric and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal factional conflict. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is fragmenting. Christianity is now institutionally dominant across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of rapid transformation in political structures, military organisation, and religious authority across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent

events.title: Stilicho's military operations in the Balkans and Greece
events.description: Stilicho continues his military campaigns to contain Alaric's Visigothic forces and other barbarian groups in the Balkans and Greece. These operations are part of a sustained effort to maintain Roman military control over the eastern Balkans and prevent barbarian incursion into Italy. The campaigns are partially successful tactically but fail to resolve the underlying political crisis of barbarian integration and Roman military fragmentation.
events.certainty_note: Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence, but the exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain. The year 398 CE is a conventional date for Stilicho's operations in this period.

events.title: John Chrysostom becomes Patriarch of Constantinople
events.description: John Chrysostom (c. 347–407 CE), a renowned Christian theologian and orator, is appointed Patriarch of Constantinople, the highest ecclesiastical office in the Eastern Roman Empire. His appointment marks a significant moment in the consolidation of Christian institutional power and the integration of the Church into imperial governance. Chrysostom's tenure is marked by his efforts to reform clerical discipline and his conflicts with the imperial court, particularly with the Empress Eudoxia. His appointment reflects the Church's growing political influence in late antique society.
key_figures: John Chrysostom (Patriarch of Constantinople, c. 347–407 CE)

sources.name: Palladius, Dialogue on the Life of John Chrysostom (early 5th century)
John Chrysostom's own writings and homilies (primary texts)

events.certainty_note: John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch in 398 CE is well-documented in multiple sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Liu Yu gains military prominence
events.description: Liu Yu (劉裕, 363–422 CE), a military commander of humble origins, begins to gain prominence in the Eastern Jin court. Over the next two decades, Liu Yu will consolidate military power and eventually found the Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE. His rise exemplifies the pattern of military strongmen superseding aristocratic authority in late Eastern Jin politics. Liu Yu's career marks the transition from the Eastern Jin to the Northern and Southern Dynasties period.
key_figures: Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song Dynasty, 363–422 CE)
Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Liu Yu's rise to prominence is well-documented in Chinese historiography, though the specific date of 398 CE for his early prominence is conventional.
cross_references: 420-001 (Founding of Liu Song Dynasty by Liu Yu)

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Yazdegerd I: consolidation of power
events.description: Yazdegerd I (r. 399–421 CE, though his reign may have begun slightly earlier) consolidates power in the Sassanid Persian Empire. His reign is marked by administrative reforms, religious tolerance (including toward Christians and Zoroastrians), and military campaigns against the Eastern Roman Empire and Central Asian nomads. Yazdegerd I is one of the most significant Sassanid rulers and represents a period of Persian imperial strength during the late antique period.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (9th century compilation)

events.certainty_note: The date of Yazdegerd I's accession is debated; some sources place it in 399 CE, others in 401 CE. The Pahlavi sources are contemporary but fragmentary. Byzantine sources are later and biased.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch to 398 CE is secure, but the significance attributed to his appointment in narratives of 'Church triumphalism' has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the conflicts and limitations of ecclesiastical power in the late antique period. Chrysostom's conflicts with the imperial court and his eventual exile (407 CE) demonstrate the fragility of clerical authority. The dating of Yazdegerd I's accession is disputed; some sources place it as late as 401 CE. Liu Yu's early prominence in 398 CE is a conventional date; the sources do not precisely date his rise.
historiographic_note: The year 398 CE is moderately documented in surviving sources, but most information comes from later compilations. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic. Ecclesiastical sources (Socrates Scholasticus, Palladius) provide information on John Chrysostom but are biased toward religious and moral dimensions. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Sassanid sources are fragmentary and often preserved only in later Islamic compilations. The year 398 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events in this specific year is difficult to assess with precision.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's operations in 398 CE continued into the campaigns of 399 CE
John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch in 398 CE led to his conflicts with the imperial court and eventual exile in 407 CE
Liu Yu's rise to prominence in 398 CE culminated in his founding of Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE
Yazdegerd I's consolidation of power in 398 CE was part of his broader reign from 399–421 CE

era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in acute crisis. Stilicho is regent for the young Emperor Honorius and is engaged in military campaigns against Alaric and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal court factionalism. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is fragmenting. Christianity is institutionally dominant across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of rapid political, military, and religious transformation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent

events.title: Stilicho becomes regent for Emperor Honorius
events.description: Stilicho, a half-Vandal Roman general, becomes regent and guardian for the young Emperor Honorius (r. 393–423 CE), who is only about 10 years old at this time. Stilicho's regency marks a critical moment in the Western Roman Empire's political fragmentation. As regent, Stilicho wields enormous power but faces constant political opposition from the Roman aristocracy and military rivals. His regency will last until his execution in 408 CE. Stilicho's position exemplifies the rise of military strongmen and the decline of traditional aristocratic authority in the late Western Empire.
key_figures: Stilicho (Roman general and regent, c. 359–408 CE)

sources.name: Orosius, History Against the Pagans (early 5th century)

events.certainty_note: Stilicho's appointment as regent is well-documented in multiple sources. The date of 397 CE is secure.

events.title: Alaric I and the Visigoths: foederati status and military integration
events.description: Alaric I and his Visigothic followers are formally integrated into the Eastern Roman military as foederati (allied troops) under a treaty with the Eastern Roman government. This arrangement grants the Visigoths land and military pay in exchange for military service. However, the arrangement is unstable, as the Eastern Roman government frequently fails to pay the agreed stipends, leading to Visigothic raids and grievances. This integration of barbarian military groups into the Roman army is a defining feature of late antique military organisation, though it ultimately contributes to the fragmentation of Roman military authority.
events.certainty_note: The foederati arrangement is well-attested, but the exact date of the treaty is debated. 397 CE is a conventional date, but some sources place it in 395 or 396 CE.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Emperor Longxu ascends to the throne
events.description: Emperor Longxu (陇西王, r. 397–402 CE) ascends to the throne of the Eastern Jin Dynasty. His reign is marked by increasing factional strife among military strongmen and aristocratic families. The imperial court's authority continues to erode as regional warlords consolidate power. Longxu's reign represents the final phase of Eastern Jin political authority before the dynasty's collapse in 420 CE.
key_figures: Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Emperor Longxu's accession in 397 CE is well-documented in Chinese historiography. The date is secure.

events.title: Augustine of Hippo: ordination as priest and early theological work
events.description: Augustine of Hippo, one of the most influential Christian theologians, is ordained as a priest in 397 CE. This marks the beginning of his formal ecclesiastical career, which will culminate in his appointment as bishop of Hippo in 395 CE (or possibly 396 CE, with the priesthood following in 397 CE). Augustine's theological output during this period is substantial and will profoundly influence medieval and early modern Christian thought. His works on grace, free will, and the nature of the Church are foundational to Christian theology.
key_figures: Augustine of Hippo (Christian theologian and priest, 354–430 CE)

sources.name: Augustine's own writings and letters (primary texts)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Augustine's ordination is debated; sources place it between 395–397 CE. The traditional date is 397 CE, but some scholars argue for 395 or 396 CE.
cross_references: 395-001 (Augustine's appointment as bishop of Hippo, or possibly 396 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Stilicho's regency as a period of Roman military strength has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the fragility of his position and the underlying military and political fragmentation of the Western Empire. Stilicho's reliance on barbarian troops and his conflicts with the Roman aristocracy demonstrate the limits of his authority. The foederati arrangement with Alaric is often portrayed as a 'barbarian invasion' in traditional historiography, but modern scholarship emphasises the continuity of Roman military structures and the integration of Gothic elites into Roman administrative systems. The exact date of Alaric's foederati status is disputed; some sources place it in 395 or 396 CE rather than 397 CE. Augustine's ordination date is debated; the traditional date of 397 CE is not universally accepted.
historiographic_note: The year 397 CE is moderately documented in surviving sources, but most information comes from later compilations. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic panegyrics. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Ecclesiastical sources provide information on Augustine but are biased toward religious and moral dimensions. The year 397 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events in this specific year is difficult to assess with precision. The 'decline and fall' narrative that dominated earlier historiography has been substantially revised; modern scholarship emphasises transformation and continuity rather than catastrophic collapse.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's regency beginning in 397 CE culminated in his execution in 408 CE
The foederati arrangement with Alaric in 397 CE led to his proclamation as king in 400 CE
Emperor Longxu's reign beginning in 397 CE ended with his death in 402 CE
Augustine's ordination in 397 CE was part of his broader theological career, which included responses to the crisis of the Western Empire

era_context: The Western Roman Empire under Honorius and regent Stilicho is engaged in military campaigns against Alaric's Visigoths and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal court factionalism. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is in its final decades. Christianity is institutionally dominant across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of acute military, political, and religious transformation across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent

events.title: Stilicho's military campaign against Alaric in Greece
events.description: Stilicho, the Western Roman general and regent, leads a military campaign into Greece to contain Alaric's Visigothic forces. The campaign is partially successful, with Stilicho achieving tactical victories, but fails to eliminate the Gothic threat or resolve the underlying political crisis. The campaign demonstrates both the capability and the limitations of Roman military power in the late 4th century. Alaric's forces are driven back but not destroyed, and the Visigoths will continue to pose a threat to the Eastern Roman Empire.
events.certainty_note: Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence, but the exact chronology of Stilicho's campaigns is uncertain. The year 396 CE is a conventional date for this campaign.

events.title: Arcadius and the Eastern Roman court: factional conflict and power consolidation
events.description: The Eastern Roman Emperor Arcadius (r. 395–408 CE) consolidates power in Constantinople amid factional conflict between various court factions, including the praetorian prefect Rufinus and other aristocratic and military elites. Arcadius is a weak emperor, and real power is wielded by court factions and military commanders. This period of factional conflict will continue throughout Arcadius' reign and will contribute to the Eastern Empire's military and political instability.
events.certainty_note: The factional conflict in the Eastern Roman court is well-attested, but the specific events of 396 CE are not precisely documented.
cross_references: 395-001 (Division of Roman Empire under Arcadius and Honorius)

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: factional strife and military consolidation
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty faces increasing factional strife among military strongmen and aristocratic families. Regional warlords consolidate power at the expense of the imperial court. This period of instability will culminate in the dynasty's collapse in 420 CE. The fragmentation reflects broader post-Han patterns of regional autonomy and the rise of military aristocracies in Chinese politics.
key_figures: Emperor An of Eastern Jin (r. 396–418 CE)
Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The broad political trajectory is well-documented in Chinese historiography. Specific events in 396 CE are less precisely dated. Emperor An's accession is traditionally dated to 396 CE.

events.title: Augustine of Hippo: appointment as bishop of Hippo (or 395 CE)
events.description: Augustine of Hippo is appointed bishop of Hippo Regius in North Africa. This appointment marks the beginning of his formal ecclesiastical leadership and his most productive period of theological writing. As bishop, Augustine will engage in theological controversies (particularly against Donatism and Pelagianism) and will write extensively on Christian doctrine, ethics, and the nature of the Church. His appointment is a significant moment in the consolidation of Christian institutional authority in North Africa.
key_figures: Augustine of Hippo (Christian theologian and bishop, 354–430 CE)

sources.name: Augustine's own writings and letters (primary texts)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Augustine's appointment as bishop is debated; sources place it between 395–397 CE. The traditional date is 395 CE, but 396 CE is also plausible.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Stilicho's campaign in 396 CE as a major Roman military victory has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the campaign's limited success and the underlying fragmentation of Roman military authority. Stilicho's reliance on barbarian troops and his conflicts with the Roman aristocracy demonstrate the limits of his power. The factional conflict in the Eastern Roman court is often portrayed as a sign of imperial weakness, but modern scholarship emphasises the complexity of late antique court politics and the agency of various factions. The exact date of Augustine's appointment as bishop is disputed; the traditional date of 395 CE is not universally accepted, and 396 or 397 CE are also plausible.
historiographic_note: The year 396 CE is moderately documented in surviving sources, but most information comes from later compilations. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Ecclesiastical sources provide information on Augustine but are biased toward religious and moral dimensions. The year 396 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events in this specific year is difficult to assess with precision. The 'decline and fall' narrative that dominated earlier historiography has been substantially revised; modern scholarship emphasises transformation and continuity rather than catastrophic collapse.

The factional conflict in the Eastern Roman court in 396 CE contributed to Gainas' revolt in 399 CE
The factional strife in Eastern Jin in 396 CE led to the dynasty's collapse and Liu Song's founding in 420 CE

era_context: The Roman Empire in its final unified phase under Theodosius I, who died in January 395. The empire is now permanently divided between his sons: Arcadius rules the East (Constantinople) and Honorius the West (Ravenna), each with regents and competing power factions. Barbarian pressure on frontiers intensifies; Christianity is the state religion. In China, the Eastern Jin dynasty faces internal fragmentation and external pressure from Northern Wei.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological record only)

events.title: Death of Theodosius I and permanent division of the Roman Empire
events.description: Theodosius I died on 17 January 395 CE in Mediolanum (Milan). His will divided the empire between his sons: Arcadius (age 18) inherited the East, Honorius (age 10) the West. This marked the formal, permanent partition of the Roman Empire, though the fiction of unified rule persisted. Arcadius was placed under the guardianship of the praetorian prefect Rufinus; Honorius under the military commander Stilicho. The division created two separate power structures, fiscal systems, and military commands that would never reunify.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (covers to 378, but context)

events.certainty_note: The date of Theodosius' death (17 January 395) is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary and later sources. The division of the empire and the regency arrangements are documented in ecclesiastical histories and later compilations, though no single contemporary imperial document survives.
cross_references: Leads to 410-001 (Alaric's sack of Rome under Honorius)

events.title: Stilicho assumes effective control of the Western Roman Empire
events.description: Following Theodosius' death, the military strongman Stilicho (half-Vandal, half-Roman) consolidated power as guardian and magister militum (master of soldiers) for the child-emperor Honorius. Stilicho marginalized the Senate and other aristocratic factions, establishing a military autocracy that would dominate the West for the next 16 years. His position was contested by Eastern court factions, particularly Rufinus, creating a rift between the two halves of the empire.

sources.name: Claudian, Panegyric on the Fourth Consulship of Honorius and other poems
Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Stilicho's rise to power is documented in Claudian's contemporary panegyrics and later historical accounts. The consolidation occurred gradually over 395–397, but 395 marks the formal beginning of his regency.

events.title: Arcadius assumes throne of Eastern Roman Empire; Rufinus becomes dominant figure
events.description: Arcadius, age 18, formally became emperor of the East upon Theodosius' death. However, real power lay with the praetorian prefect Rufinus, an ambitious aristocrat who marginalized the young emperor and pursued his own political agenda. Rufinus' dominance created tension with Stilicho in the West and with other Eastern factions, particularly the military establishment and the patriarch of Constantinople.

sources.name: Palladius, Dialogue on the Life of John Chrysostom

events.certainty_note: Arcadius' accession and Rufinus' rise are well-documented in ecclesiastical and secular histories, though written after the fact.

events.title: Visigoths under Alaric begin major incursions into the Balkans
events.description: Following Theodosius' death and the weakening of imperial military coordination, Alaric, a Visigothic leader (possibly already a Roman officer), led a major raid into the Balkans, particularly targeting Moesia and Thrace. This marked the beginning of a sustained period of Visigothic pressure on the Eastern Empire that would culminate in Alaric's march on Constantinople and eventual sack of Rome in 410. The raids exploited the power vacuum created by the division of the empire and the distraction of Eastern and Western courts with internal politics.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The timing and scale of Alaric's initial incursions are debated. Zosimus places them in 395; other sources suggest they began slightly later. The exact date is approximate, but the general phenomenon is confirmed.
cross_references: Leads to 397-001 (Alaric's formal appointment as magister militum)

events.title: Eastern Jin dynasty faces succession crisis and fragmentation in China
events.description: In 395 CE (or 396 by some sources), the Eastern Jin dynasty in southern China experienced internal instability following the death of Emperor Xiaowu in 396 (or 397). The period saw competing warlords and generals vying for control, with the imperial house becoming increasingly ceremonial. This fragmentation would lead to the eventual collapse of the Eastern Jin and the rise of the Liu Song dynasty in 420. The Northern Wei dynasty, meanwhile, consolidated control over northern China.
key_figures: Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song, rising power)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang
Records of the Eastern Jin (晉書)

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. The dating of specific events in the 390s CE is approximate; sources sometimes differ by 1–2 years. The general political fragmentation is confirmed.

events.title: Council of Constantinople (or synod) addresses ecclesiastical disputes
events.description: In 395 CE, ecclesiastical councils or synods were held in Constantinople to address ongoing Christological and disciplinary disputes. The exact nature and significance of these gatherings is debated by modern scholars, as sources are fragmentary. The period saw increasing imperial involvement in church affairs under Arcadius, with Patriarch Nectarius presiding. These councils reflected the growing power of the patriarchate and the state's interest in doctrinal uniformity.
key_figures: Rufinus (praetorian prefect, influential in church matters)
events.certainty_note: The existence and exact date of ecclesiastical gatherings in Constantinople in 395 are inferred from later ecclesiastical histories. No contemporary records of specific councils survive for this year.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of imperial church involvement

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of 395 as a clean, permanent 'division' of the empire is complicated by modern scholarship: (1) The division was not intended to be permanent; both emperors and their courts maintained the fiction of a unified empire with shared sovereignty. (2) Stilicho's regency in the West was contested and unstable, not the secure autocracy once portrayed; he faced significant senatorial and military opposition. (3) The dating of Alaric's first incursions is disputed; some scholars place them in 397 or later, not 395. (4) The significance of ecclesiastical councils in 395 is unclear; sources may conflate events from multiple years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 395 CE is rich but heavily shaped by later Christian historiography (Socrates, Orosius, Zosimus writing in the 5th–6th centuries). Pagan sources are sparse; Claudian's panegyrics provide contemporary perspective but are propagandistic. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later with potential chronological distortions. The Western perspective dominates surviving sources; Eastern and non-Roman perspectives are underrepresented. Archaeological evidence for this year is minimal; the record relies almost entirely on written sources.

graph_edges.note: The division of the empire and weakening of central authority enabled Alaric's eventual sack of Rome
Alaric's raids prompted his formal appointment as magister militum by the Eastern Empire
Stilicho's regency set the stage for his eventual fall and execution
Eastern Jin fragmentation led directly to Liu Song dynasty's founding

era_context: The reign of Theodosius I in his final year; the Roman Empire is still nominally unified under his rule, though power is increasingly devolved to military strongmen and regional commanders. Theodosius is aging and in declining health. In the East, the Goths and other barbarian groups press the frontiers. In China, the Eastern Jin dynasty continues its slow decline amid internal factional struggles.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road trade (sparse documentation)

events.title: Battle of the Frigidus River (Vipava River) — Theodosius defeats Eugenius and Arbogastes
events.description: On 5–6 September 394 CE, Theodosius I defeated the usurper Eugenius and his military commander Arbogastes at the Frigidus River (modern Vipava River in Slovenia). This was the final civil war of the Roman Empire and the last major battle fought by a reigning emperor in person. Eugenius, a pagan Roman general, had been proclaimed emperor by Arbogastes (a Frankish magister militum) in 392 after the death of Valentinian II. Theodosius' victory restored his sole authority over the entire empire and marked a decisive moment for Christian dominance: Theodosius attributed his victory to divine intervention, and the battle is often portrayed as a triumph of Christianity over paganism, though this narrative is complicated by modern scholarship.
key_figures: Valentinian II (deceased, whose death sparked the conflict)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Claudian, Panegyric on the Sixth Consulship of Honorius

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in contemporary sources (Claudian, Ambrose) and later histories. The date of 5–6 September 394 is established by multiple sources.
cross_references: Leads to 395-001 (Theodosius' death and empire division)

events.title: Theodosius I bans pagan religious practices and closes temples
events.description: Following his victory at the Frigidus River, Theodosius issued decrees (or reinforced earlier ones) banning pagan religious practices, closing temples, and prohibiting pagan sacrifices throughout the empire. These edicts, issued in 394–395, represented the culmination of decades of Christian imperial policy and marked the effective end of state-sponsored paganism in the Roman Empire. The decrees were not uniformly enforced, and pagan practices continued in rural areas and among the aristocracy, but the legal framework for religious pluralism had been dismantled.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The decrees are preserved in the Theodosian Code. The exact dating of individual edicts is sometimes uncertain (they may span 391–395), but the general policy is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of broader Christianization of the empire

events.title: Olympic Games held for the last time in antiquity
events.description: The Olympic Games were held in 394 CE, likely for the last time in antiquity. The games had been celebrated continuously (with rare interruptions) since 776 BCE. The final games are traditionally dated to 394 CE, though some sources place them in 393 or suggest they continued sporadically into the early 5th century. The games were suppressed as part of Theodosius' campaign against pagan religious practices. The site of Olympia was later destroyed by earthquakes and floods, and the sanctuary fell into disuse.
key_figures: Theodosius I (Roman Emperor, responsible for suppression)
Athletes and officials of the games (names not recorded)

sources.name: Pausanias, Description of Greece (ancient source, but context from later sources)

events.certainty_note: The date of the final Olympic Games is traditionally given as 394 CE, but modern scholarship is uncertain. Some sources suggest 393 CE; others argue games may have continued sporadically. The suppression by Theodosius is confirmed, but the exact final date is approximate.
cross_references: Related to 394-002 (Theodosius' ban on pagan practices)

events.title: Theodosius I consolidates sole rule over unified Roman Empire
events.description: Following his victory at the Frigidus River, Theodosius I became the sole ruler of the entire Roman Empire for the first time since 378 CE. This period of unified rule lasted only until his death in January 395, but it represented the last moment of imperial unity. Theodosius used this final period to consolidate Christian dominance, suppress pagan practices, and attempt to stabilize the empire's military and administrative structures. His reign is often marked as the end of the 'classical' Roman Empire and the beginning of the 'late antique' period.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Theodosius' sole rule from 394–395 is well-documented.

events.title: Eastern Jin dynasty continues internal fragmentation; Liu Yu rises to prominence
events.description: In 394 CE, the Eastern Jin dynasty in southern China continued to experience internal instability and military fragmentation. Liu Yu, a general of mixed Han and Xiongnu descent, was rising to prominence through military victories and political maneuvering. The imperial house was increasingly powerless, with real authority held by competing generals. This period of fragmentation would culminate in Liu Yu's founding of the Liu Song dynasty in 420 CE.
key_figures: Liu Yu (rising general, future founder of Liu Song)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Chinese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. The general political situation is confirmed, but specific events in 394 are approximate.

events.title: Northern Wei dynasty consolidates control over northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei dynasty, founded by the Xiongnu (or Xianbei, depending on classification) in 386 CE, continued to consolidate its control over northern China in 394 CE. Under Emperor Hongwu (or Daowu), the Northern Wei expanded its territory and administrative capacity, eventually becoming the dominant power in the north. This period saw the Northern Wei in competition with the Eastern Jin for control of the Chinese heartland.
key_figures: Various Northern Wei generals and administrators
events.certainty_note: The general consolidation of Northern Wei power is confirmed by Chinese sources, but specific events in 394 are approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the Battle of the Frigidus River as a triumph of Christianity over paganism is complicated by modern scholarship: (1) Eugenius was not necessarily a committed pagan; he was a pragmatist who appealed to pagan traditionalists for political support. (2) Arbogastes, though pagan, was a military professional, not a religious zealot. (3) The battle's outcome was determined by military tactics and logistics, not divine intervention, though Theodosius and Christian sources attributed it to God's favor. (4) The suppression of pagan practices was gradual and incomplete; rural paganism persisted for centuries. (5) The 'last Olympic Games' in 394 is traditional dating; some sources suggest games continued sporadically, and the exact final date is uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 394 CE is rich but heavily shaped by Christian historiography and imperial propaganda. Claudian's panegyrics are contemporary but propagandistic. Later sources (Zosimus, Orosius, Socrates) provide narrative but were written 50–200 years after the events. Pagan perspectives are largely lost. The Battle of the Frigidus River is well-documented, but interpretations of its significance vary. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. Archaeological evidence for 394 CE is minimal; the record relies on written sources.

graph_edges.note: Victory at Frigidus enabled Theodosius' consolidation of sole rule
Theodosius' final victory set the stage for his death and the empire's division
Theodosius' ban on pagan practices led to the suppression of the Olympic Games
Eastern Jin fragmentation and Liu Yu's rise led to Liu Song dynasty

era_context: Theodosius I is in his final years, ruling a still-unified Roman Empire but facing increasing pressure from barbarian groups and internal factional conflicts. The usurper Eugenius controls the Western Empire with military backing from Arbogastes. In China, the Eastern Jin dynasty is in decline, with regional warlords gaining power. The Silk Road trade continues, though documentation is sparse.

events.title: Eugenius and Arbogastes consolidate control of Western Roman Empire
events.description: In 393 CE, the usurper Eugenius and his military commander Arbogastes (a Frankish magister militum) consolidated their control over the Western Roman Empire. Eugenius had been proclaimed emperor in 392 following the death of Valentinian II. Arbogastes was the real power behind the throne, controlling the military and administration. This period saw the Western Empire effectively independent from Theodosius' Eastern rule, though Theodosius was preparing a military response. Eugenius attempted to appeal to pagan traditionalists and the aristocracy, positioning himself as a defender of classical Roman values against Theodosius' Christian policies.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Eugenius and Arbogastes' control of the West in 393 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Theodosius I prepares military campaign against Eugenius
events.description: In 393 CE, Theodosius I began assembling military forces and preparing a campaign to overthrow the usurper Eugenius and restore imperial unity. This preparation took much of 393 and early 394, with Theodosius gathering troops from the Eastern Empire and recruiting barbarian allies (particularly Goths). The campaign would culminate in the Battle of the Frigidus River in September 394.
key_figures: Alaric (Gothic leader, possibly already allied with Theodosius)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Theodosius' military preparations in 393 are documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Ambrose of Milan mediates between Theodosius and Eugenius; religious tensions escalate
events.description: In 393 CE, Ambrose of Milan, the influential Christian bishop, attempted to mediate between Theodosius and Eugenius while also advocating for Theodosius' Christian policies. Ambrose's letters from this period reveal the religious tensions underlying the civil war: Eugenius was appealing to pagan traditionalists, while Theodosius positioned himself as the defender of Christian orthodoxy. Ambrose's role as both spiritual advisor and political mediator exemplified the growing power of the Christian church in imperial politics.

sources.name: Paulinus of Milan, Life of Ambrose

events.certainty_note: Ambrose's letters from 393 are preserved and provide contemporary evidence of his mediation efforts and the religious dimensions of the conflict.
cross_references: Related to 394-002 (Theodosius' ban on pagan practices)

events.title: Eastern Jin dynasty experiences military setbacks; regional warlords gain autonomy
events.description: In 393 CE, the Eastern Jin dynasty suffered military setbacks against regional warlords and the Northern Wei dynasty. The imperial government's authority continued to erode as regional military commanders (jiedushi) consolidated power in their territories. This period of fragmentation accelerated the decline of the Eastern Jin and set the stage for its eventual collapse in 420 CE.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Eastern Jin decline is confirmed by Chinese sources, but specific military events in 393 are approximate.

events.title: Northern Wei dynasty expands territorial control in northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei dynasty continued its expansion and consolidation of power in northern China in 393 CE. Under Emperor Hongwu (Daowu), the Northern Wei conducted military campaigns to expand its territory and suppress internal dissent. The dynasty was becoming the dominant power in the north, setting the stage for eventual reunification of China under Northern Wei rule in the 5th century.
events.certainty_note: The general expansion of Northern Wei power is confirmed, but specific campaigns in 393 are approximate.

events.title: Silk Road trade continues amid political instability in Central Asia
events.description: Despite political instability in both the Roman Empire and China, trade along the Silk Road continued in 393 CE. Merchants, pilgrims, and envoys traveled between East and West, exchanging goods, ideas, and religious teachings. This period saw the early spread of Buddhism along the Silk Road, with Buddhist missionaries and texts traveling from India and Central Asia to China. The exact volume and routes of trade are difficult to document, but archaeological evidence and later sources attest to its continuation.
key_figures: Chinese and Roman officials facilitating trade

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Silk Road sites (Samarkand, Kashgar, etc.)

events.certainty_note: The continuation of Silk Road trade in 393 is inferred from archaeological evidence and later sources. Contemporary documentation is sparse.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of East-West exchange

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Eugenius as a pagan reactionary is complicated by modern scholarship: (1) Eugenius was not necessarily a committed pagan; he was a pragmatist appealing to pagan traditionalists for political support. (2) The conflict was primarily political and military, not fundamentally religious, though religious rhetoric was deployed by both sides. (3) Theodosius' Christian policies were not universally popular, even among Christians; some Christian aristocrats supported Eugenius. (4) The dating of specific military events in 393 is approximate; sources sometimes differ by months or a year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 393 CE is moderate in richness. Western sources (Claudian, Ambrose, later historians) provide good documentation of the civil war and religious tensions. Eastern sources are sparse. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. The record is heavily shaped by Christian historiography and imperial propaganda. Pagan perspectives are largely lost. Archaeological evidence for 393 CE is minimal. The Silk Road trade is documented through later sources and archaeology, not contemporary records.

graph_edges.note: Eugenius and Arbogastes' consolidation of Western power prompted Theodosius' military response
Theodosius' military preparations culminated in the Battle of Frigidus
Religious tensions in 393 escalated to Theodosius' ban on pagan practices in 394
Eastern Jin military setbacks accelerated its decline and Liu Song's rise

era_context: The Roman Empire is in turmoil following the death of Valentinian II in May 392. The usurper Eugenius is proclaimed emperor in the West with military backing from Arbogastes. Theodosius I rules the East but is preparing to challenge the usurpation. In China, the Eastern Jin dynasty continues its decline. This is a pivotal year marking the beginning of the final civil war of the unified Roman Empire.

events.title: Death of Valentinian II and usurpation of Eugenius
events.description: On 15 May 392 CE, the Western Roman Emperor Valentinian II died in Vienne (modern France), either by suicide or murder. The exact circumstances of his death remain disputed by modern scholars. Valentinian II had been a puppet emperor, with real power held by the military strongman Arbogastes. Following Valentinian II's death, Arbogastes proclaimed the Roman general Eugenius as emperor. Eugenius was a pagan traditionalist who appealed to the pagan aristocracy and military establishment. This usurpation triggered a civil war with Theodosius I, who refused to recognize Eugenius' legitimacy.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The date of Valentinian II's death (15 May 392) is well-attested in multiple sources. The circumstances (suicide vs. murder) are debated, but the fact of his death and Eugenius' usurpation are confirmed.

events.title: Arbogastes consolidates military control of Western Roman Empire
events.description: Following Valentinian II's death, Arbogastes, a Frankish magister militum (master of soldiers), consolidated his control over the Western Roman military and administration. Arbogastes had been the real power behind Valentinian II's throne; with the young emperor dead, he openly proclaimed Eugenius as a puppet emperor. Arbogastes controlled the armies, the treasury, and the administrative apparatus of the West. His rise exemplified the increasing power of barbarian military commanders in the late Roman Empire.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Arbogastes' consolidation of power in 392 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Theodosius I refuses to recognize Eugenius; prepares for civil war
events.description: Upon learning of Eugenius' usurpation, Theodosius I refused to recognize him as a legitimate emperor. Theodosius declared Eugenius a usurper and began preparing military forces to challenge his rule. This refusal to recognize Eugenius triggered the final civil war of the Roman Empire, which would last from 392 to 394 CE. Theodosius' position was strengthened by his control of the Eastern Empire's resources and his alliance with Christian bishops, who opposed Eugenius' pagan sympathies.
key_figures: Ambrose of Milan (Christian bishop, supporting Theodosius)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Theodosius' refusal to recognize Eugenius is documented in contemporary sources, particularly Ambrose's letters.

events.title: Ambrose of Milan opposes Eugenius and supports Theodosius
events.description: Ambrose of Milan, the influential Christian bishop, openly opposed Eugenius' usurpation and supported Theodosius I. Ambrose's opposition was based on both political and religious grounds: he opposed Eugenius' pagan sympathies and his challenge to legitimate imperial authority. Ambrose's letters from 392 reveal his efforts to rally Christian support for Theodosius and to pressure Eugenius to accept Christian orthodoxy. Ambrose's influence exemplified the growing political power of the Christian church in the late Roman Empire.
key_figures: Theodosius I (Eastern emperor, supported by Ambrose)

sources.name: Paulinus of Milan, Life of Ambrose

events.certainty_note: Ambrose's opposition to Eugenius is documented in his surviving letters from 392.

events.title: Eastern Jin dynasty experiences continued fragmentation; Liu Yu gains military prominence
events.description: In 392 CE, the Eastern Jin dynasty in southern China continued to experience internal fragmentation and loss of central authority. Liu Yu, a general of mixed Han and Xiongnu descent, was gaining military prominence through successful campaigns and political maneuvering. The imperial house was increasingly powerless, with regional warlords controlling their territories. This period of fragmentation would accelerate the dynasty's decline and lead to its collapse in 420 CE.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Eastern Jin decline and Liu Yu's rise is confirmed by Chinese sources, but specific events in 392 are approximate.

events.title: Northern Wei dynasty consolidates power in northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei dynasty, founded in 386 CE, continued to consolidate its power and expand its territory in northern China in 392 CE. Under Emperor Hongwu (Daowu), the Northern Wei conducted administrative reforms and military campaigns to strengthen its control. The dynasty was becoming the dominant power in the north, setting the stage for eventual reunification of China.
events.certainty_note: The general consolidation of Northern Wei power is confirmed, but specific events in 392 are approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Valentinian II's death as murder by Arbogastes is disputed by modern scholarship: (1) Some sources suggest suicide, possibly due to depression or political despair. (2) The exact circumstances are unknown; contemporary sources are vague or contradictory. (3) Arbogastes may not have directly ordered the death, though he certainly benefited from it. (4) The characterization of Eugenius as a 'pagan reactionary' is oversimplified; he was a pragmatist appealing to pagan traditionalists for political support, not a committed ideologue.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 392 CE is rich but shaped by Christian historiography and imperial propaganda. Ambrose's letters provide contemporary perspective but are partisan. Later sources (Zosimus, Orosius, Socrates) provide narrative but were written 50–200 years after the events. Pagan perspectives are largely lost. The exact circumstances of Valentinian II's death are unclear, and sources differ. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. Archaeological evidence for 392 CE is minimal.

graph_edges.note: Valentinian II's death enabled Arbogastes to consolidate military control
Eugenius' usurpation prompted Theodosius' refusal to recognize him
Valentinian II's death and Eugenius' usurpation triggered the civil war that culminated at Frigidus
Ambrose's opposition to Eugenius supported Theodosius' eventual ban on pagan practices
Eastern Jin fragmentation and Liu Yu's rise led to Liu Song dynasty

era_context: The reign of Theodosius I in the Eastern Roman Empire; the Western Empire is ruled by the young Valentinian II under the guardianship of the military strongman Arbogastes. Theodosius is consolidating Christian dominance and suppressing pagan practices. In China, the Eastern Jin dynasty is in decline, with regional warlords gaining autonomy. This is a period of religious transformation in the Roman Empire and political fragmentation in China.

events.title: Theodosius I issues decrees suppressing pagan religious practices
events.description: In 391 CE, Theodosius I issued a series of decrees (or reinforced earlier ones) that suppressed pagan religious practices throughout the Roman Empire. These edicts banned pagan sacrifices, closed temples, and prohibited pagan worship in public spaces. The decrees represented the culmination of decades of Christian imperial policy and marked a decisive shift toward Christian monopoly on religious practice. However, enforcement was uneven, and pagan practices continued in rural areas and among the aristocracy. The decrees were not universally popular, even among Christians, and sparked resistance from pagan traditionalists.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The decrees are preserved in the Theodosian Code. The exact dating of individual edicts is sometimes uncertain (they may span 391–395), but the general policy is confirmed for 391.

events.title: Destruction of the Serapeum of Alexandria
events.description: In 391 CE (or possibly 392), a Christian mob, possibly incited by Patriarch Theophilus of Alexandria, destroyed the Serapeum, one of the most important pagan temples in the Roman Empire. The Serapeum housed the famous Library of Alexandria (or a portion of it) and was a center of pagan learning and worship. The destruction was a symbolic and practical blow to paganism and represented the growing power of Christian mobs and bishops to act against pagan institutions. The exact date and circumstances are debated by modern scholars, but the destruction occurred in the early 390s CE.
key_figures: Theophilus of Alexandria (Patriarch, possibly inciting the destruction)
Pagan traditionalists and philosophers (defending the temple)
Theodosius I (emperor, whose policies enabled the destruction)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Eunapius of Sardis, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The destruction of the Serapeum is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date (391 or 392) is debated. The circumstances and the role of Theophilus are also disputed by modern scholars.
cross_references: Related to 391-001 (Theodosius' suppression of pagan practices)
Part of broader pattern of Christian violence against pagan institutions

events.title: Valentinian II rules Western Roman Empire under Arbogastes' guardianship
events.description: In 391 CE, Valentinian II, the young Western Roman Emperor (age 20), continued to rule under the effective guardianship of the military strongman Arbogastes. Arbogastes controlled the armies, the treasury, and the administration, while Valentinian II was increasingly marginalized. This arrangement was unstable and would collapse in 392 with Valentinian II's death and Eugenius' usurpation. The period exemplified the declining power of the imperial office and the rising power of military strongmen in the late Roman Empire.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Valentinian II's nominal rule and Arbogastes' effective control are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Eastern Jin dynasty continues fragmentation; regional warlords consolidate power
events.description: In 391 CE, the Eastern Jin dynasty in southern China continued to experience internal fragmentation and loss of central authority. Regional warlords and military commanders (jiedushi) consolidated power in their territories, effectively becoming independent rulers. The imperial house was increasingly ceremonial, with real power held by competing generals. This period of fragmentation would accelerate the dynasty's decline and lead to its collapse in 420 CE.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Eastern Jin fragmentation is confirmed by Chinese sources, but specific events in 391 are approximate.

events.title: Northern Wei dynasty consolidates administrative and military control in northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei dynasty, founded in 386 CE, continued to consolidate its administrative and military control over northern China in 391 CE. Under Emperor Hongwu (Daowu), the Northern Wei conducted reforms to strengthen the state apparatus and conducted military campaigns to expand territory. The dynasty was becoming the dominant power in the north, setting the stage for eventual reunification of China under Northern Wei rule.
events.certainty_note: The general consolidation of Northern Wei power is confirmed, but specific events in 391 are approximate.

events.title: Hypatia of Alexandria begins her career as mathematician and philosopher
events.description: In 391 CE (or possibly slightly earlier or later), Hypatia of Alexandria, one of the most famous mathematicians and philosophers of antiquity, was active in Alexandria. Hypatia taught mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy at the Neoplatonic school in Alexandria and made contributions to geometry and the study of conic sections. She was a pagan philosopher in an increasingly Christian city and would eventually be killed by a Christian mob in 415 CE. Her career exemplified the declining status of pagan learning in the late Roman Empire.
key_figures: Students and patrons of the Neoplatonic school
events.certainty_note: The exact date of Hypatia's birth and the beginning of her career are uncertain. Sources suggest she was born around 350–370 CE and was active by the 390s CE, but the precise dating is approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the destruction of the Serapeum as a triumphant Christian victory is complicated by modern scholarship: (1) The exact date is uncertain; sources differ between 391 and 392 CE. (2) The role of Theophilus of Alexandria is debated; he may have incited the destruction, but sources are unclear. (3) The destruction may have been a riot rather than an organized religious campaign. (4) The Library of Alexandria may have already been destroyed or dispersed before 391; the Serapeum's library was not the famous Library of Alexandria. (5) Hypatia's career dating is uncertain; she may have begun teaching slightly earlier or later than 391. (6) The characterization of Theodosius' pagan suppression as 'complete' is oversimplified; pagan practices persisted for centuries.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 391 CE is moderate in richness. Christian sources (Socrates, Theodoret, Orosius) provide narrative but are written 50–200 years after the events and are shaped by Christian triumphalism. Pagan sources are sparse; Eunapius and Damascius provide some pagan perspective but are also late compilations. The Theodosian Code provides primary evidence for imperial legislation. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. Archaeological evidence for 391 CE is minimal. The record is heavily shaped by Christian historiography and imperial propaganda.

Theodosius' suppression of pagan practices led to the suppression of the Olympic Games
The destruction of the Serapeum exemplified the Christian suppression of pagan learning that culminated in Hypatia's death
