era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, following the end of the Peloponnesian War (404 BCE). Athens is recovering politically and culturally under democratic restoration; Sparta dominates mainland Greece but faces challenges to its hegemony. In the Near East, the Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II remains the dominant regional power. In Italy, Rome is consolidating control over the Latin League and beginning expansion into central Italy.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones

events.title: Sack of Rome by Gallic forces under Brennus
events.description: A coalition of Celtic (Gallic) tribes, led by the chieftain Brennus, invaded central Italy and defeated a Roman army at the Battle of the Allia River. The Gauls subsequently sacked the city of Rome itself, burning much of it and forcing the Roman population to retreat to the Capitoline Hill. The city was ransomed with gold, and the Gauls eventually withdrew. This event shocked the Mediterranean world and demonstrated Rome's vulnerability, though it ultimately prompted military and defensive reforms that strengthened Roman institutions.
key_figures: Camillus (Roman military leader, though his role in this year is debated)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita), Books 5–6

events.certainty_note: The sack of Rome is attested in multiple later sources (Livy, Diodorus, Plutarch) but no contemporary written records survive. Archaeological evidence for destruction in Rome around this period is debated; some scholars place the event slightly later (c. 387 BCE) or question its scale. The date 390 BCE follows the traditional chronology but is not definitively confirmed by archaeology.
cross_references: Follows from broader Celtic migrations into Italy in the 5th–4th centuries BCE
Led to Roman military reforms and the construction of the Servian Wall

events.title: Artaxerxes II's consolidation of Persian control in Egypt
events.description: Following the Egyptian revolt under Amyrtaeus (404–399 BCE), Persian forces under Artaxerxes II reasserted direct control over Egypt, ending the brief period of Egyptian independence. This reassertion of Persian hegemony was part of Artaxerxes II's broader consolidation of imperial authority after the Anabasis of Cyrus the Younger (401 BCE) and internal instability. Egypt remained under Persian rule until the Macedonian conquest under Alexander the Great.
key_figures: Phanes of Halicarnassus (Greek mercenary commander, possibly involved in reconquest)

sources.name: Egyptian demotic papyri and administrative records

events.certainty_note: The reconquest of Egypt by Artaxerxes II is attested in Greek sources and Egyptian administrative records. The precise date is uncertain; some sources place it around 390 BCE, others around 387 BCE. The process may have taken several years.
cross_references: Follows from the Egyptian revolt of 404–399 BCE
Part of Artaxerxes II's reign (404–358 BCE)

events.title: Corinthian War: ongoing conflict between Sparta and anti-Spartan coalition
events.description: The Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) was in its middle phase, with Thebes, Corinth, Athens, and Argos allied against Spartan hegemony. Naval and land campaigns continued across Greece. This conflict was partly fueled by Persian gold supporting anti-Spartan factions, as Artaxerxes II sought to weaken Spartan power in the Aegean. The war would eventually be resolved by the King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas) in 387 BCE.
key_figures: Conon (Athenian admiral, supported by Persian gold)
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Hellenica is a near-contemporary account by a participant in Greek affairs. The Corinthian War is well-attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Part of the broader Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)
Led to the King's Peace of 387 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and scale of the Gallic sack of Rome (390-001) is disputed by modern scholarship. Some scholars, based on archaeological evidence and analysis of later sources, argue the sack occurred around 387 BCE rather than 390 BCE, or that it was less catastrophic than later Roman sources (Livy, Plutarch) suggest. The absence of contemporary written records and the reliance on sources written centuries later (Livy wrote in the Augustan age) introduces significant uncertainty. Additionally, some scholars question whether the sack was as complete or destructive as tradition claims, noting that Rome recovered remarkably quickly if the destruction was truly total.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 390 BCE is heavily shaped by later Greek and Roman historiography. For Rome, we rely almost entirely on Livy and other sources written 300+ years after the events, creating substantial risk of legendary embellishment or distortion. For Greece, Xenophon's Hellenica provides near-contemporary narrative, but it reflects an Athenian/pro-Spartan perspective and omits much of the broader Mediterranean world. Persian sources are sparse; Egyptian administrative records exist but are fragmentary. The record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and Near Eastern events; sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Asia beyond Persian reach are essentially absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: The Gallic sack prompted Roman military reforms and defensive construction
Reassertion of Persian control followed the Egyptian revolt of 404–399 BCE
The Corinthian War was resolved by the King's Peace of 387 BCE

era_context: Continuation of the Classical Greek period and early 4th century BCE. The Corinthian War remains active; Sparta continues to assert hegemony over Greece despite opposition from Thebes, Athens, and Corinth. Rome is recovering from the Gallic sack and consolidating its position in Latium. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II is stabilizing after internal conflicts and the Anabasis of Cyrus the Younger.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from most of the Mediterranean beyond Greece and Rome

events.title: Corinthian War: continued military campaigns
events.description: The Corinthian War continued with ongoing campaigns between Spartan forces and the anti-Spartan coalition (Thebes, Corinth, Athens, Argos). Naval operations in the Aegean and land campaigns in central Greece characterized this year. Persian financial support for anti-Spartan factions remained a key factor in sustaining the conflict. The war was approaching its resolution through diplomatic negotiation.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's near-contemporary account confirms the continuation of the Corinthian War through this period.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Aegean
events.description: Athens, supported by Persian gold, conducted naval operations in the Aegean to challenge Spartan naval dominance and support allied cities. These operations were part of the broader Corinthian War strategy to weaken Spartan hegemony. Athenian naval power, though diminished from its 5th-century peak, remained significant in regional politics.
key_figures: Artaxerxes II (Persian Great King, financial supporter)
events.certainty_note: Athenian naval activity during the Corinthian War is attested in sources, though specific operations in 389 BCE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)
Related to Persian support for anti-Spartan factions

events.title: Roman consolidation in Latium and conflict with neighboring tribes
events.description: Following the Gallic sack of 390 BCE, Rome was consolidating its position within the Latin League and managing relations with neighboring Italic tribes. This period saw Roman military reorganization and the beginning of Rome's gradual expansion beyond Latium. Specific military campaigns in 389 BCE are poorly documented, but the general trajectory of Roman consolidation and expansion is clear from later sources.
key_figures: Camillus (Roman military leader and statesman, though his exact activities in 389 BCE are unclear)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita)

events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman consolidation is clear, but specific events in 389 BCE are not well-documented. Livy's account is written centuries after the fact and may conflate or misdate events.
cross_references: Follows from the Gallic sack of 390 BCE
Part of Rome's gradual expansion in the 4th century BCE

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for Roman activities in 389 BCE reflects a broader historiographic problem: Roman history of the 4th century BCE is poorly attested, and much of what Livy reports may be legendary or misplaced chronologically. The Corinthian War is well-attested, but specific campaigns in individual years are sometimes difficult to distinguish in the sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 389 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides a near-contemporary but Athenian-centric perspective. Roman history of this period is almost entirely dependent on Livy, written centuries later, and is therefore inherently unreliable for specific events and dates. Persian sources are minimal. The record is heavily weighted toward Greece and Rome; the broader Mediterranean, Near East, and all other regions are underrepresented or absent.

graph_edges.note: The Corinthian War continued toward its resolution in the King's Peace of 387 BCE
Roman consolidation followed the Gallic sack of 390 BCE

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the final phase of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE). Diplomatic negotiations were intensifying, leading toward the King's Peace of 387 BCE. Rome was consolidating power in Latium and beginning its expansion into central Italy. The Persian Achaemenid Empire remained the dominant regional power in the Near East and eastern Mediterranean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Mediterranean beyond Greece and Rome

events.title: Corinthian War: diplomatic negotiations intensify
events.description: As the Corinthian War entered its final phase, diplomatic negotiations intensified between the warring Greek factions and the Persian Great King Artaxerxes II. The conflict had become economically exhausting for all parties, and Persian mediation offered a path to resolution. These negotiations would culminate in the King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas) in 387 BCE, which would establish a new framework for Greek interstate relations under Persian hegemony.
events.certainty_note: The intensification of diplomatic negotiations in the final years of the Corinthian War is attested in sources, though the precise timing of specific diplomatic moves in 388 BCE versus 387 BCE is not always clear.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)
Led to the King's Peace of 387 BCE

events.title: Plato's Academy continues intellectual activity in Athens
events.description: Plato's Academy, founded around 387 BCE (or possibly 388 BCE), was beginning its long history as a center of philosophical inquiry and education in Athens. The Academy would become one of the most influential intellectual institutions of the ancient world, shaping Greek and later Western philosophy for nearly 900 years. In this period, Plato was developing his philosophical system and attracting students from across the Greek world.
key_figures: Aristotle (not yet a student, but would join in 367 BCE)

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The founding date of Plato's Academy is traditionally placed around 387 BCE, but some sources suggest 388 BCE. The exact date is not definitively established. Diogenes Laërtius, writing in the 3rd century CE, is the primary source for biographical information about Plato.
cross_references: Part of the broader intellectual flowering of Classical Athens
Led to the development of Western philosophy over subsequent centuries

events.title: Roman military campaigns in Latium and central Italy
events.description: Rome continued its consolidation of power in Latium and began expanding into central Italy. Specific military campaigns in 388 BCE are poorly documented in surviving sources, but the general trajectory of Roman expansion is clear. Rome was gradually subjugating or allying with neighboring Italic tribes and city-states, laying the groundwork for its eventual dominance of the Italian peninsula.
key_figures: Camillus (Roman military leader, though his specific role in 388 BCE is unclear)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita)

events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman expansion is clear from later sources, but specific events in 388 BCE are not well-documented. Livy's account is written centuries after the fact.
cross_references: Part of Rome's gradual expansion in the 4th century BCE
Preceded by consolidation following the Gallic sack of 390 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: The founding date of Plato's Academy is uncertain. While 387 BCE is the traditional date, some ancient sources suggest 388 BCE, and modern scholars debate whether the Academy was formally founded at a specific moment or gradually developed over time. The distinction between 388 and 387 BCE may be artificial. Additionally, the sparse documentation for Roman activities in 388 BCE reflects the broader historiographic problem of unreliable sources for 4th-century BCE Rome.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 388 BCE is again dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica for political and military events. The founding of Plato's Academy is attested in later biographical sources (Diogenes Laërtius) and internal evidence from Plato's dialogues, but the precise date is uncertain. Roman history remains poorly documented and dependent on Livy's much later account. The record is heavily weighted toward Greece and Rome; other regions are absent or minimally represented.

graph_edges.note: Diplomatic negotiations in 388 BCE culminated in the King's Peace of 387 BCE
Plato's Academy continued to develop and attract students, including Aristotle in 367 BCE

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, marking the end of the Corinthian War and the establishment of a new framework for Greek interstate relations under Persian hegemony. Rome was consolidating its position in central Italy. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II was at the height of its power and influence in the Mediterranean and Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Mediterranean beyond Greece and Rome

events.title: King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas) ends the Corinthian War
events.description: The King's Peace, also known as the Peace of Antalcidas, was negotiated by the Persian Great King Artaxerxes II and imposed on the warring Greek city-states. The peace established that all Greek cities would be autonomous and independent, but that the Persian Great King would be the guarantor and enforcer of this arrangement. Sparta, despite its military dominance, accepted Persian mediation to end the costly Corinthian War. The peace effectively subordinated Greek interstate relations to Persian hegemony and marked a shift in the balance of power in the Mediterranean world. The peace would remain the framework for Greek politics until the rise of Macedon under Philip II.
key_figures: Artaxerxes II (Persian Great King, mediator and enforcer)
events.certainty_note: The King's Peace of 387 BCE is one of the best-attested events of the Classical Greek world. Xenophon's near-contemporary account provides detailed information about the negotiations and terms. The date is firmly established.
cross_references: Established Persian hegemony over Greece until the Macedonian conquest
Led to Spartan dominance of Greece in the following decades

events.title: Plato's Academy formally established in Athens
events.description: Plato's Academy was formally established in Athens, likely in 387 BCE (though some sources suggest 388 BCE). The Academy was founded in a gymnasium dedicated to the hero Academus in the suburbs of Athens. It became one of the most influential intellectual institutions of the ancient world, serving as a center for philosophical inquiry, mathematical study, and education for nearly 900 years. The Academy attracted students from across the Greek world and shaped the development of Western philosophy, mathematics, and science.
key_figures: Xenocrates (early student and later head of the Academy)

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers, Book 3

events.certainty_note: The founding of Plato's Academy is traditionally dated to 387 BCE, but the exact date is not definitively established. Diogenes Laërtius, writing in the 3rd century CE, is the primary source for biographical information. The Academy's existence and influence are well-attested in later sources.
cross_references: Part of the intellectual flowering of Classical Athens
Led to the development of Western philosophy and science
Aristotle joined the Academy in 367 BCE

events.title: Roman consolidation in Latium and expansion into central Italy
events.description: Rome continued its consolidation of power in Latium and gradual expansion into central Italy. Following the Gallic sack of 390 BCE and the subsequent period of recovery, Rome was now establishing itself as a major power in the Italian peninsula. Specific military campaigns in 387 BCE are poorly documented, but the general trajectory of Roman expansion is clear from later sources. Rome was subjugating or allying with neighboring Italic tribes and city-states.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita)

events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman expansion is clear, but specific events in 387 BCE are not well-documented. Livy's account is written centuries after the fact and may conflate or misdate events.
cross_references: Part of Rome's gradual expansion in the 4th century BCE
Followed the Gallic sack of 390 BCE and Roman recovery

events.title: Persian consolidation of control in Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Following the reassertion of Persian control over Egypt in the early 390s BCE, Artaxerxes II consolidated Persian hegemony over Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean. The King's Peace of 387 BCE further solidified Persian influence over Greek affairs. Egypt remained under Persian rule, administered through Persian governors and local Egyptian officials. The Persian Achaemenid Empire was at the height of its territorial extent and influence.

sources.name: Egyptian demotic papyri and administrative records

events.certainty_note: Persian control over Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean is attested in Egyptian administrative records and Greek sources. The consolidation of this control is part of the broader context of Artaxerxes II's reign.
cross_references: Part of Artaxerxes II's reign (404–358 BCE)
Related to the King's Peace of 387 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: The King's Peace of 387 BCE is well-attested and its significance is not seriously disputed. However, some modern scholars have questioned the extent to which the peace actually established Persian hegemony over Greece, arguing that Persian influence was more limited and that Greek city-states retained more autonomy than the traditional interpretation suggests. Additionally, the founding date of Plato's Academy remains uncertain; while 387 BCE is the traditional date, some sources suggest 388 BCE, and the distinction may be artificial. The sparse documentation for Roman activities in 387 BCE reflects the broader historiographic problem of unreliable sources for 4th-century BCE Rome.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 387 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides a near-contemporary and detailed account of the King's Peace. This is one of the best-documented years of the Classical Greek world. The founding of Plato's Academy is attested in later biographical sources and internal evidence from Plato's dialogues. Roman history remains poorly documented and dependent on Livy's much later account. Persian sources are minimal, though Egyptian administrative records provide some evidence of Persian control. The record is heavily weighted toward Greece and Rome; other regions are absent or minimally represented.

graph_edges.note: The King's Peace ended the Corinthian War that began in 395 BCE
The King's Peace established a framework for Greek politics that lasted until the Battle of Mantinea in 362 BCE
Plato's Academy continued to develop and attract students, including Aristotle in 367 BCE

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the immediate aftermath of the King's Peace of 387 BCE. The new framework for Greek interstate relations under Persian hegemony was being implemented. Sparta was consolidating its dominance of mainland Greece. Rome was continuing its expansion in central Italy. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II was at the height of its power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Mediterranean beyond Greece and Rome

events.title: Implementation of the King's Peace in Greece
events.description: In the year following the King's Peace of 387 BCE, the terms of the peace were being implemented across Greece. Greek city-states were adjusting to the new framework of Persian-guaranteed autonomy. Sparta, as the dominant military power, was positioned to enforce the peace and maintain order in Greece. The peace established a period of relative stability in Greek interstate relations, though tensions and rivalries remained beneath the surface. This period would see Sparta's hegemony over Greece solidified, though it would eventually be challenged by Thebes under Epaminondas.
key_figures: Artaxerxes II (Persian Great King, guarantor of the peace)
events.certainty_note: The implementation of the King's Peace in the years following 387 BCE is attested in sources, though specific events in 386 BCE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.
cross_references: Followed the King's Peace of 387 BCE
Led to Spartan dominance of Greece in the following decades

events.title: Plato's Academy develops philosophical curriculum
events.description: Plato's Academy, recently established (387 BCE), was developing its philosophical curriculum and attracting students from across the Greek world. The Academy focused on mathematics, dialectic, and philosophical inquiry, with the goal of training students in the pursuit of truth and virtue. Plato was developing his theory of Forms and other philosophical doctrines that would profoundly influence Western thought. The Academy was becoming a center of intellectual activity in Athens.
key_figures: Xenocrates (early student and later head of the Academy)

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The development of Plato's Academy in the years following its founding is attested in Plato's dialogues and later biographical sources. Specific activities in 386 BCE are not always clearly documented.
cross_references: Followed the founding of the Academy in 387 BCE
Part of the intellectual flowering of Classical Athens

events.title: Roman military campaigns in central Italy
events.description: Rome continued its military expansion in central Italy, subjugating or allying with neighboring Italic tribes and city-states. Specific campaigns in 386 BCE are poorly documented in surviving sources, but the general trajectory of Roman expansion is clear. Rome was gradually consolidating its position as the dominant power in the Italian peninsula, though this process would take several more centuries.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita)

events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman expansion is clear, but specific events in 386 BCE are not well-documented. Livy's account is written centuries after the fact and may conflate or misdate events.
cross_references: Part of Rome's gradual expansion in the 4th century BCE
Followed the consolidation of Roman power in Latium

events.title: Persian administration of Egypt under Artaxerxes II
events.description: Egypt remained under Persian rule, administered through Persian governors and local Egyptian officials. The Persian Achaemenid Empire maintained tight control over Egypt as a key source of grain and revenue. Egyptian administrative records and demotic papyri document the continuation of Persian rule and the functioning of the Egyptian bureaucracy under Persian oversight.

sources.name: Egyptian demotic papyri and administrative records

events.certainty_note: Persian control over Egypt is attested in Egyptian administrative records and Greek sources. The continuation of Persian rule in 386 BCE is part of the broader context of Artaxerxes II's reign.
cross_references: Part of Artaxerxes II's reign (404–358 BCE)
Related to the King's Peace of 387 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for specific events in 386 BCE reflects the broader historiographic problem of ancient history: years that are not marked by major political upheavals or military campaigns often leave minimal traces in surviving sources. The implementation of the King's Peace is attested in sources, but the specific details of how it was implemented in 386 BCE versus 387 BCE or 385 BCE are not always clear.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 386 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica. The year is less well-documented than 387 BCE (the year of the King's Peace) because it lacks a major political or military event that captured the attention of ancient historians. Roman history remains poorly documented and dependent on Livy's much later account. Persian sources are minimal, though Egyptian administrative records provide evidence of Persian control. The record is heavily weighted toward Greece and Rome; other regions are absent or minimally represented. This year illustrates a broader historiographic challenge: the survival of historical records is often determined by the occurrence of dramatic events rather than by the actual historical importance of a year.

graph_edges.note: The implementation of the King's Peace followed its negotiation in 387 BCE
The development of Plato's Academy followed its founding in 387 BCE

era_context: The Late Roman Empire in its final century of unified rule. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire under Theodosius I was consolidating Christian orthodoxy and military control. The Western Empire faced increasing pressure from Germanic migrations and internal instability. In East Asia, the Northern Wei Dynasty (Tuoba) was expanding control over northern China. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization was in decline, with major centres experiencing abandonment.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological record sparse)

events.title: Theodosius I issues edict against pagan temples and sacrifices
events.description: Emperor Theodosius I issued a series of edicts (part of the broader Theodosian Code) prohibiting pagan religious practices, including temple sacrifices and worship. This represented a major escalation in Christian imperial policy, moving beyond earlier restrictions to active suppression. The edicts were enforced unevenly across the empire but marked a decisive shift toward Christian monopoly on state religion. This policy had profound consequences for the survival of classical pagan institutions and knowledge.

sources.name: Theodosian Code (Codex Theodosianus), compiled 438 CE but preserving earlier edicts
Ambrose of Milan, Letters and sermons
Zosimus, New History (written early 6th century, hostile to Christianity)

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Theodosius's anti-pagan edicts is debated. Some scholars place key legislation in 391–392 CE rather than 385. The Theodosian Code preserves the text but was compiled decades later. Ambrose's letters confirm pressure on pagan practices in this period.

events.title: Execution of Priscillian and followers in Hispania
events.description: Priscillian, a Christian bishop and ascetic reformer from Hispania, was executed by Roman authorities in Trier on charges of heresy and sorcery. Priscillian had advocated strict asceticism and challenged episcopal authority, attracting followers but also fierce opposition from mainstream bishops. His execution marked the first use of imperial power to execute a Christian for doctrinal reasons—a precedent that would shape religious persecution for centuries. The case revealed deep divisions within the Christian church over authority and practice.

sources.name: Sulpicius Severus, Chronicle (written early 5th century, near-contemporary account)
Augustine, Against the Priscillianists (later polemical work)

events.certainty_note: The execution is well-attested in near-contemporary sources. The exact charges and theological details are debated, but the fact and approximate date (385 CE) are secure.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty consolidates control over northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei (Tuoba) Dynasty, founded by the Xiongnu-descended Tuoba people, continued its consolidation of power over northern China. Under Emperor Xiaowen's predecessors, the Wei expanded territorial control and began the process of sinicization—adopting Chinese administrative practices, language, and culture. By 385, the Wei controlled much of the Yellow River basin and was emerging as a major power that would eventually unify northern China. This period saw significant cultural synthesis between nomadic and Chinese traditions.
key_figures: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei (r. 471–499, but dynasty established earlier)
Tuoba Hong (regent, active in this period)

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty), compiled 554 CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century
Archaeological evidence from Datong and Luoyang sites

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Northern Wei expansion is well-established. Specific events in 385 are less clearly documented in surviving sources. Dating relies on later compilations.

events.title: Alaric I becomes leader of the Visigoths
events.description: Alaric I, a Visigothic nobleman of mixed Roman and Gothic descent, rose to leadership of the Visigothic people in the Balkans. Alaric had served in the Roman army but increasingly led Gothic resistance to Roman authority. His rise marked the beginning of a process that would culminate in the sack of Rome in 410 CE. Alaric's leadership represented a shift from accommodation to confrontation between Germanic peoples and the Roman state, driven by disputes over settlement rights, military pay, and autonomy.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History (contemporary, but written earlier; later sources rely on him)
Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Alaric's exact rise to power is not precisely dated in sources. 385 is a conventional date, but some sources place his emergence as a leader in the 390s. The chronology depends on later compilations.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Theodosius's anti-pagan edicts is contested. Some modern scholars argue that the most severe legislation (CTh 16.10.10) belongs to 391–392 rather than 385, making the significance of 385 as a turning point less clear. Additionally, the extent to which these edicts were actually enforced across the empire is debated—pagan practices persisted in many regions for centuries. Priscillian's execution is sometimes interpreted not primarily as a heresy trial but as a political conflict between bishops, complicating its significance as a precedent for doctrinal persecution. Alaric's exact date of emergence is uncertain; some sources suggest the early 390s rather than 385.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 385 CE is dominated by Roman and Christian sources, creating a Western-centric bias. Eastern Roman (Byzantine) sources are limited. Chinese sources on Northern Wei are abundant but compiled centuries later. Germanic and other non-Roman perspectives are almost entirely absent from surviving documentation. The sources tend to emphasize religious and political elite events while obscuring economic, demographic, and cultural changes affecting ordinary people. Archaeological evidence is increasingly important for understanding this period but remains unevenly distributed geographically.

graph_edges.note: Theodosius's anti-pagan policy escalated in subsequent years
Priscillian's execution exemplified the enforcement of religious orthodoxy
Alaric's rise to power set in motion events leading to the sack of Rome

era_context: The Late Roman Empire under Theodosius I, consolidating power after the civil war with Maximus (383–388). The Eastern Empire was increasingly Christianized and bureaucratized. The Western Empire faced mounting pressure from Germanic peoples and internal factionalism. In China, the Northern Wei Dynasty was expanding. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization continued its decline.

events.title: Theodosius I consolidates power after civil war with Maximus
events.description: Following his victory over the usurper Maximus in 388 CE (but the consolidation process extended through 384–385), Theodosius I worked to stabilize the empire and assert control over both Eastern and Western halves. In 384, Theodosius was solidifying his authority, reorganizing the military, and beginning to enforce religious orthodoxy. This period saw the rise of Ambrose of Milan as a key advisor and the increasing influence of the Christian church in imperial policy. Theodosius's reign marked a turning point toward a more explicitly Christian empire.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History (though written earlier, covers this period)

events.certainty_note: The broad political situation is clear, but specific events in 384 are not sharply documented. The consolidation process extended over several years.

events.title: Jerome begins translation of the Bible into Latin (Vulgate)
events.description: Jerome, a Christian scholar and ascetic, began his monumental task of translating the Hebrew Bible and Greek New Testament into Latin. This work, which would become known as the Vulgate, was commissioned by Pope Damasus I. Jerome's translation was revolutionary—it aimed to replace the older, inconsistent Latin translations (the Old Latin versions) with a standardized, accurate text. The Vulgate would become the dominant biblical text in Western Christianity for over a thousand years and profoundly shaped Christian theology, liturgy, and culture. Jerome's work exemplified the intellectual resources the Christian church was mobilizing.

sources.name: Jerome, Prefaces to the Vulgate and Letters
Jerome, Life by Gennadius of Marseille (5th century)

events.certainty_note: Jerome's work on the Vulgate began in the 380s, with 384 being a conventional date for the start. The exact chronology is uncertain, as Jerome worked on different books at different times. The project extended over decades.
cross_references: 405-001 (Completion of major portions of the Vulgate)

events.title: Petition of the Altar of Victory controversy in Rome
events.description: Pagan senators in Rome, led by Symmachus, petitioned the young Western Emperor Valentinian II to restore the Altar of Victory in the Senate house, which had been removed by Christian emperors. The altar was a symbol of pagan religious tradition and Roman military success. Symmachus's petition was eloquent and politically sophisticated, appealing to tradition and religious tolerance. However, Bishop Ambrose of Milan vigorously opposed the petition, arguing that pagan symbols had no place in a Christian empire. The petition was denied, marking a symbolic victory for Christian dominance and the marginalization of pagan institutions in the imperial center.

sources.name: Ambrose of Milan, Letters 17–18 (response to the petition)

events.certainty_note: The petition and responses are preserved in primary sources. The date is well-established as 384 CE.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty expands military campaigns in northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei (Tuoba) Dynasty continued military campaigns to consolidate control over northern China. In this period, the Wei were engaged in conflicts with rival powers and in the process of expanding their territorial base. These campaigns were part of the longer process of unification that would culminate in the Wei's control of all of northern China by the early 5th century. The military campaigns were accompanied by administrative reforms and the gradual adoption of Chinese governmental structures.

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Northern Wei expansion is clear, but specific events in 384 are not well-documented in surviving sources. Dating relies on later compilations.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Jerome's Vulgate translation is uncertain. Some scholars place the beginning of his work in the late 380s rather than 384. The exact chronology of his translation of different biblical books is debated. The Altar of Victory petition is sometimes interpreted as less significant than traditional accounts suggest—it may have been a rhetorical exercise rather than a serious political threat. The extent of Northern Wei military activity in 384 specifically is unclear from surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The record for 384 CE is dominated by Roman and Christian sources, with a strong bias toward elite political and religious events. The sources are primarily from the Eastern Empire and Rome itself. Chinese sources on Northern Wei are abundant but compiled centuries later. The perspectives of ordinary people, women, and non-elite groups are largely absent. Archaeological evidence is increasingly important but unevenly distributed.

graph_edges.note: Theodosius's consolidation of power enabled his anti-pagan legislation
Part of the broader conflict between paganism and Christianity
Jerome's Vulgate translation project extended over decades

era_context: The Late Roman Empire in the midst of civil war. The usurper Magnus Maximus, proclaimed emperor by troops in Britannia, challenged the legitimate emperors Theodosius I (East) and Valentinian II (West). This conflict would dominate the period until 388. In China, the Northern Wei Dynasty was consolidating power. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization was in advanced decline.

events.title: Magnus Maximus proclaimed emperor in Britannia; civil war begins
events.description: Magnus Maximus, a Roman general of Spanish origin, was proclaimed emperor by troops in Britannia. Maximus then invaded Gaul and defeated the Western Roman Emperor Gratian, establishing himself as a rival claimant to imperial power. This triggered a civil war that would last until 388 CE. Maximus represented a challenge to the legitimacy of Theodosius I and the young Valentinian II. The conflict revealed the fragility of imperial authority and the power of the military to make and unmake emperors. Maximus's challenge was ultimately unsuccessful, but it destabilized the Western Empire and contributed to the conditions that would lead to Germanic invasions.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History (though written earlier, covers this period indirectly)
Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Coins and inscriptions from the period

events.certainty_note: The proclamation of Maximus and the beginning of civil war are well-attested in multiple sources. The date 383 CE is secure.

events.title: Battle of the Frigidus (or Frigidus River) — preliminary conflicts
events.description: In 383, the first major military confrontation between Theodosius I and Magnus Maximus occurred near the Frigidus River in the Balkans. The exact details and outcome of this engagement are debated by historians, but it marked the beginning of direct military conflict between the two claimants. The battle was inconclusive, and the conflict would continue for several more years. The Frigidus campaign demonstrated the military resources both sides could mobilize and the stakes of the civil war.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The Frigidus campaign is attested in sources, but the exact chronology and details are disputed. Some sources place major fighting in 388 rather than 383. The dating is uncertain.

events.title: Gratian assassinated in Lyons
events.description: The Western Roman Emperor Gratian, defeated by Magnus Maximus's forces, was assassinated in Lyons while attempting to flee. Gratian's death removed a legitimate claimant and consolidated Maximus's control over the Western Empire. The assassination was carried out by Maximus's supporters, though the exact circumstances are unclear. Gratian's death marked a significant shift in the balance of power and left the young Valentinian II as the nominal Western emperor, dependent on the support of Theodosius I.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Ambrose of Milan, Letters (contemporary witness accounts)

events.certainty_note: Gratian's assassination is well-attested in multiple sources. The date 383 CE is secure.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty continues consolidation in northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei (Tuoba) Dynasty continued its process of consolidating control over northern China. In 383, the Wei were engaged in administrative reforms and military campaigns to expand their territorial base. This period saw the gradual sinicization of the Tuoba people and the adoption of Chinese governmental structures. The Wei were emerging as a major power that would eventually unify northern China.

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Northern Wei consolidation is clear, but specific events in 383 are not well-documented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of the civil war between Theodosius and Maximus is disputed. Some sources place the major military confrontations in 388 rather than 383. The Battle of the Frigidus may have occurred in 388 rather than 383. The circumstances of Gratian's assassination are unclear, and some sources suggest he may have died in battle rather than being assassinated. The significance of Maximus's challenge is sometimes overstated—he was ultimately defeated relatively quickly, and his reign did not fundamentally alter the trajectory of the empire.
historiographic_note: The record for 383 CE is dominated by Roman sources, with a strong bias toward political and military events. The sources are primarily from the Eastern Empire and later compilations. Chinese sources on Northern Wei are abundant but compiled centuries later. The perspectives of ordinary people, women, and non-elite groups are largely absent. The sources tend to emphasize the dramatic political conflict while obscuring economic and social changes.

graph_edges.note: Maximus's proclamation led to Gratian's assassination
The civil war initiated by Maximus lasted until 388
Military campaigns between Theodosius and Maximus

era_context: The Late Roman Empire under Theodosius I, who had recently consolidated power in the East. The Western Empire was ruled by the young Gratian and his half-brother Valentinian II. This was a period of relative stability before the civil war with Magnus Maximus (383–388). In China, the Northern Wei Dynasty was consolidating power. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization was in decline.

events.title: Treaty between Theodosius I and the Goths (foederati arrangement)
events.description: Following the Gothic War (376–382), Theodosius I negotiated a treaty with the Goths, establishing them as foederati (allied troops) within the Roman Empire. This arrangement allowed the Goths to settle within Roman territory in exchange for military service. The treaty was a pragmatic response to the military threat posed by the Goths and reflected the empire's increasing reliance on Germanic peoples for military manpower. However, the arrangement also created tensions, as the Goths maintained a degree of autonomy and the Romans struggled to integrate them fully. This foederati system would become a model for later Germanic settlements and contributed to the eventual fragmentation of the Western Empire.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History (though written earlier, covers this period indirectly)
Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The treaty is attested in sources, but the exact date and terms are debated. 382 is a conventional date, but some sources place the treaty in 381 or 383. The details of the arrangement are unclear.

events.title: Theodosius I establishes religious orthodoxy through church councils
events.description: Theodosius I continued his policy of establishing Christian orthodoxy as the official religion of the empire. In this period, he was working with church leaders to suppress heretical movements and establish doctrinal uniformity. This involved supporting orthodox bishops and suppressing Arian and other non-orthodox Christian groups. Theodosius's religious policy was part of a broader effort to use imperial power to enforce religious conformity, a precedent that would shape Christian history for centuries.
key_figures: Gregory of Nazianzus (Christian bishop and theologian)

sources.name: Gregory of Nazianzus, Orations and Letters

events.certainty_note: Theodosius's religious policy is well-attested, but specific events in 382 are not sharply documented. The policy evolved over several years.

events.title: Pope Damasus I commissions Jerome to revise biblical translations
events.description: Pope Damasus I commissioned Jerome, a Christian scholar, to revise and standardize the Latin biblical translations. This commission would lead to Jerome's monumental Vulgate translation project. Damasus recognized the need for a standardized, accurate biblical text in Latin, as the existing Old Latin translations were inconsistent and sometimes inaccurate. Jerome's work would become the foundation of Western Christian biblical scholarship and practice for over a thousand years.
events.certainty_note: The commission is attested in Jerome's writings, but the exact date is uncertain. 382 is a conventional date, but some sources place it in the early 380s or late 380s.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty consolidates control over northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei (Tuoba) Dynasty continued its consolidation of power over northern China. In 382, the Wei were engaged in administrative reforms and military campaigns to expand their territorial base. This period saw the gradual sinicization of the Tuoba people and the adoption of Chinese governmental structures. The Wei were emerging as a major power that would eventually unify northern China by the early 5th century.

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Northern Wei consolidation is clear, but specific events in 382 are not well-documented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the treaty between Theodosius and the Goths is disputed. Some sources place it in 381, others in 382 or 383. The terms and significance of the foederati arrangement are debated—some scholars argue it was a pragmatic military necessity rather than a deliberate policy of integration. The extent to which Theodosius's religious policy was coordinated with church leaders versus imposed unilaterally is unclear. Jerome's commission by Damasus I may have occurred in a different year than 382.
historiographic_note: The record for 382 CE is dominated by Roman and Christian sources, with a strong bias toward political and religious events. The sources are primarily from the Eastern Empire and Rome itself. Chinese sources on Northern Wei are abundant but compiled centuries later. The perspectives of ordinary people, women, and non-elite groups are largely absent. The sources tend to emphasize elite political and religious developments while obscuring economic and social changes.

graph_edges.note: The foederati arrangement with Goths set precedent for Germanic settlement
Theodosius's religious policy escalated in subsequent years
Damasus's commission led to Jerome's Vulgate translation project

era_context: The Late Roman Empire under Theodosius I, who had recently consolidated power in the East following the Battle of Adrianople (378). The Western Empire was ruled by the young Gratian and his half-brother Valentinian II. This was a period of relative stability and religious consolidation. In China, the Northern Wei Dynasty was consolidating power. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization was in decline.

events.title: First Council of Constantinople (Second Ecumenical Council)
events.description: The First Council of Constantinople, convened by Emperor Theodosius I, met to address theological disputes within the Christian church, particularly regarding the nature of the Holy Spirit and the relationship between the three persons of the Trinity. The council affirmed the Nicene Creed and condemned various heresies, including Arianism and Apollinarianism. The council's decisions, embodied in what became known as the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed, established the orthodox Christian doctrine that would dominate Western and Eastern Christianity for centuries. The council represented the imperial church's increasing power to define and enforce doctrinal orthodoxy.
key_figures: Gregory of Nazianzus (Christian bishop and theologian)
Gregory of Nyssa (Christian bishop and theologian)

sources.name: Council of Constantinople, Acts and Canons (preserved in later compilations)
Gregory of Nazianzus, Orations and Letters
Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine (though written earlier, covers church councils)

events.certainty_note: The First Council of Constantinople is well-attested in multiple sources. The date 381 CE is secure, though some sources give 381–382 as the duration of the council.
cross_references: 325-001 (First Council of Nicaea, which this council affirmed)
451-001 (Council of Chalcedon, further development of Christological doctrine)

events.title: Edict of Theodosius I establishing Christian orthodoxy as state religion
events.description: Theodosius I issued edicts establishing Christian orthodoxy (as defined by the Council of Constantinople) as the official religion of the Roman Empire. These edicts prohibited heretical Christian practices and began the process of suppressing pagan religions. The edicts represented a major shift in imperial policy—from toleration of multiple religions to active enforcement of Christian orthodoxy. This policy had profound consequences for the survival of classical pagan institutions and the development of Christian hegemony in the Mediterranean world.

sources.name: Theodosian Code (Codex Theodosianus), compiled 438 CE but preserving earlier edicts

events.certainty_note: Theodosius's edicts establishing Christian orthodoxy are attested in sources, but the exact dating and sequence are debated. The Theodosian Code preserves the text but was compiled decades later. Some scholars place key legislation in 380 (Edict of Thessalonica) rather than 381.
cross_references: 380-001 (Edict of Thessalonica, earlier statement of Christian orthodoxy)

events.title: Gregory of Nazianzus resigns as Bishop of Constantinople
events.description: Gregory of Nazianzus, one of the most important Christian theologians of the period, resigned as Bishop of Constantinople following the First Council of Constantinople. Gregory had been appointed to the position to defend orthodox Christianity against Arian and other heretical movements. However, he found the political and ecclesiastical conflicts surrounding the position intolerable and withdrew from active church leadership. Gregory's resignation reflected the tensions between theological integrity and ecclesiastical politics that characterized the late 4th-century church.
key_figures: Gregory of Nazianzus (Christian bishop and theologian)

sources.name: Gregory of Nazianzus, Orations and Letters
Gregory of Nyssa, Life of Gregory of Nazianzus (written later)

events.certainty_note: Gregory's resignation is attested in his own writings. The date 381 CE is secure.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty consolidates control over northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei (Tuoba) Dynasty continued its consolidation of power over northern China. In 381, the Wei were engaged in administrative reforms and military campaigns to expand their territorial base. This period saw the gradual sinicization of the Tuoba people and the adoption of Chinese governmental structures. The Wei were emerging as a major power that would eventually unify northern China by the early 5th century.

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Northern Wei consolidation is clear, but specific events in 381 are not well-documented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The First Council of Constantinople is sometimes called the 'Second Ecumenical Council,' but this designation is not universal in early Christian sources—the council's ecumenical status was established retrospectively. The council's decisions were not immediately accepted by all Christian communities, and disputes over its authority continued for centuries. The extent to which Theodosius personally drove the council's decisions versus allowing church leaders to determine doctrine is debated. Gregory of Nazianzus's resignation may have been motivated by factors beyond ecclesiastical politics, including health and personal preference for contemplative life.
historiographic_note: The record for 381 CE is dominated by Roman and Christian sources, with a strong bias toward religious and political events. The sources are primarily from the Eastern Empire. Chinese sources on Northern Wei are abundant but compiled centuries later. The perspectives of ordinary people, women, and non-elite groups are largely absent. The sources tend to emphasize elite theological and political developments while obscuring economic and social changes. The council's proceedings are known primarily through later compilations and summaries rather than complete contemporary records.

graph_edges.note: The council's decisions informed Theodosius's edicts on Christian orthodoxy
The council's conflicts contributed to Gregory's resignation
Theodosius's religious policy escalated in subsequent years
Continuation of the process of defining Christian orthodoxy begun at Nicaea

era_context: The late Roman Empire under Theodosius I, who ruled the Eastern Roman Empire (379–395 CE) and briefly reunified the empire (394–395 CE). Christianity was rapidly becoming the dominant religion following Constantine's legalization in 313 CE; Theodosius would make it the state religion in 380 CE itself. The Western Roman Empire was fragmenting under pressure from Germanic migrations and internal instability. The Eastern Empire faced threats from the Goths, Persians, and internal religious schism.

events.title: Edict of Thessalonica (Cunctos Populos)
events.description: Theodosius I and co-emperors Gratian and Valentinian II issued the Edict of Thessalonica on 27 February 380 CE, declaring Nicene Christianity (Trinitarian orthodoxy) the official state religion of the Roman Empire. The edict condemned Arian, Apollinarian, and other heterodox Christian sects as heretical and subject to legal penalties. This marked the first time a Roman emperor used state power to enforce religious orthodoxy and effectively ended the period of Christian pluralism within the empire. The edict had profound consequences for the development of Christian theology, church-state relations, and the persecution of religious minorities.

sources.name: Codex Theodosianus (Theodosius II's law code, compiled 438 CE, preserving the text of the edict)
Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine (written early 4th century, discusses Theodosius's religious policies)
Socrates Scholasticus, Ecclesiastical History (written early 5th century)

events.certainty_note: The edict is preserved in the Codex Theodosianus and is one of the most securely dated events of this period. The date of 27 February 380 CE is well-attested.
cross_references: Follows from Constantine's legalization of Christianity (313 CE)
Led to First Council of Constantinople (381 CE)
Part of broader Christianization of the Roman Empire

events.title: Battle of the Frigidus River (preliminary campaigns)
events.description: Theodosius I conducted military campaigns against Gothic and other barbarian incursions along the Danube frontier in 380 CE. These campaigns were part of his broader strategy to stabilize the Eastern Empire's borders following the Gothic victory at Adrianople in 378 CE. While the major Battle of the Frigidus River would occur in 394 CE, the 380 CE campaigns represent the beginning of Theodosius's military consolidation and his eventual settlement with the Goths through the foederati system (treaty-based alliance).
key_figures: Alaric (Gothic leader, though his prominence came later)

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (written late 4th century, covers military campaigns)
Zosimus, New History (written early 6th century, Byzantine historian)

events.certainty_note: Theodosius's military activities in 380 CE are attested in later sources, but specific battle details are sparse. The broader campaign context is secure; exact chronology of individual engagements is less certain.
cross_references: Follows from Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)
Led to foederati treaties with Goths (382 CE)

events.title: Consolidation of Theodosius I's authority in the Eastern Empire
events.description: In 380 CE, Theodosius I had been ruling the Eastern Roman Empire for approximately one year (since 379 CE). He worked to consolidate his authority, suppress usurpers, and stabilize the empire after the catastrophic loss at Adrianople. His policies combined military reorganization, religious orthodoxy enforcement (via the Edict of Thessalonica), and diplomatic settlement with barbarian groups. This year marks a turning point in his consolidation of power, which would culminate in his brief reunification of the entire Roman Empire in 394–395 CE.
events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Theodosius's consolidation are well-attested; specific events within 380 CE are less detailed in surviving sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Theodosius I reign (379–395 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Edict of Thessalonica is sometimes portrayed as an immediate, total suppression of non-Nicene Christianity, but modern scholarship (e.g., Ramsay MacMullen) notes that enforcement was gradual and uneven. Arian and other heterodox communities persisted for centuries in various regions, particularly among Germanic peoples and in the Eastern Empire's periphery. The edict's immediate practical impact may have been overstated in earlier historiography.
historiographic_note: The year 380 CE is exceptionally well-documented for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding the Edict of Thessalonica, which is preserved in legal compilations. However, sources are predominantly written from the perspective of the imperial court and the Nicene Christian establishment. The experiences of non-Nicene Christians, pagans, and barbarian groups are underrepresented. Sources on military campaigns and frontier conditions are less detailed than for political and religious events.

graph_edges.note: The Edict of Thessalonica prompted the convocation of the First Council of Constantinople in 381 CE to further define Nicene orthodoxy.
Military campaigns of 380 CE led to the foederati treaty with the Goths in 382 CE.
The crisis following Adrianople (378 CE) created conditions for Theodosius's religious consolidation.

era_context: The year Theodosius I became Eastern Roman Emperor following the death of Valens at Adrianople (378 CE). The Eastern Empire was in crisis: the Goths had defeated and killed the emperor, and Germanic peoples were migrating into Roman territory. The Western Empire under Gratian was attempting to stabilize the situation. Christianity was rapidly expanding, though religious schisms (Arianism, Apollinarianism) remained contentious. This year marks the beginning of Theodosius's reign, which would reshape the late Roman Empire.

events.title: Theodosius I proclaimed Eastern Roman Emperor
events.description: Following the death of Valens at the Battle of Adrianople in August 378 CE, the Eastern Roman Empire was without an emperor. Gratian, the Western Roman Emperor, appointed Theodosius I (a Spanish-born general with military experience in Britain and the Balkans) as Eastern Roman Emperor in January 379 CE. Theodosius was proclaimed in Sirmium (modern Serbia) and immediately faced the task of stabilizing the empire after the catastrophic defeat and the ongoing Gothic incursions. His appointment marked a turning point: Theodosius would prove to be one of the most significant emperors of the late Roman period, eventually reunifying the empire and establishing Christianity as the state religion.
key_figures: Theodosius I (newly proclaimed Eastern Roman Emperor)
Gratian (Western Roman Emperor, who appointed Theodosius)

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (written late 4th century, covers the succession)

events.certainty_note: The appointment of Theodosius in January 379 CE is well-attested in multiple sources. The date and location are secure.
cross_references: Follows from Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)
Led to Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE)
Part of broader Theodosius I reign (379–395 CE)

events.title: Gothic settlement negotiations and military campaigns
events.description: In 379 CE, Theodosius I initiated military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations with the Goths and other barbarian groups that had invaded the Eastern Empire following Adrianople. Rather than attempting immediate reconquest, Theodosius pursued a strategy of containment and negotiation. These efforts would eventually lead to the foederati treaty of 382 CE, in which Goths were settled within Roman territory as allied troops. The 379 CE campaigns represent the beginning of this pragmatic approach, which contrasted with earlier Roman military doctrine and reflected the empire's weakened military capacity.
key_figures: Alaric (Gothic leader, though his prominence increased later)

sources.name: Jordanes, Getica (written 6th century, covers Gothic history)

events.certainty_note: Theodosius's military activities in 379 CE are attested in later sources, but specific details are sparse. The broader strategy of negotiation is secure; exact chronology of individual campaigns is less certain.
cross_references: Follows from Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)

events.title: Religious tensions and Arian-Nicene disputes in the Eastern Empire
events.description: In 379 CE, the Eastern Roman Empire was deeply divided over Christian theology. The Arian controversy (which had begun in the early 4th century) remained unresolved despite the Council of Nicaea (325 CE). Many Eastern bishops and even some emperors had favored Arianism or semi-Arian positions. Theodosius I, though a Christian, initially inherited an empire where religious orthodoxy was contested. His appointment as emperor would eventually lead to his enforcement of Nicene orthodoxy via the Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE), but in 379 CE, the religious landscape was still fluid and contested.
key_figures: Theodosius I (newly proclaimed emperor, Christian but not yet enforcing orthodoxy)
Gregory of Nazianzus (Nicene theologian, would become influential)

sources.name: Gregory of Nazianzus, Orations (written late 4th century, discusses religious tensions)
Historiographic consensus on late 4th-century Christian disputes

events.certainty_note: The existence of religious tensions in 379 CE is well-attested; specific events or developments within this year are less clearly documented.
cross_references: Led to Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE)
Led to First Council of Constantinople (381 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Theodosius as a strong, decisive emperor who immediately stabilized the empire is somewhat idealized in later sources. Modern scholarship (e.g., Peter Brown, Ramsay MacMullen) suggests that Theodosius's early years were marked by significant challenges, military setbacks, and a gradual consolidation of power rather than immediate success. The foederati system, often portrayed as a pragmatic solution, may have been more of a necessity born from military weakness than a deliberate strategic choice.
historiographic_note: The year 379 CE is well-documented for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding Theodosius's appointment and the broader political situation. However, sources are predominantly written from the perspective of the imperial court and the Nicene Christian establishment. Information on barbarian perspectives, non-Nicene Christian communities, and the experiences of ordinary people is limited. Military details are sparse compared to political narratives.

graph_edges.note: Theodosius's appointment as emperor in 379 CE led to his issuance of the Edict of Thessalonica in 380 CE.
Military campaigns and negotiations in 379 CE led to the foederati treaty with the Goths in 382 CE.
Theodosius's appointment was a direct consequence of the Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE.

era_context: The late Roman Empire under the Eastern Emperor Valens and Western Emperor Gratian. The empire faced severe military pressure from Germanic peoples, particularly the Goths, who were migrating into Roman territory. The Arian-Nicene Christian controversy remained unresolved. This year is marked by one of the most catastrophic military defeats in Roman history, the Battle of Adrianople, which killed the emperor and fundamentally altered the trajectory of the late Roman Empire.

events.title: Battle of Adrianople
events.description: On 9 August 378 CE, the Eastern Roman Emperor Valens was defeated and killed in battle against the Goths near Adrianople (modern Edirne, Turkey). The Gothic forces, led by Fritigern and other chieftains, had been migrating into Roman territory and were initially settled as refugees (foederati) within the empire. Tensions over food supplies, mistreatment by Roman officials, and disputes over settlement terms led to a Gothic uprising. Valens marched to confront them with an army estimated at 40,000–60,000 troops. The battle resulted in a catastrophic Roman defeat: Valens was killed, along with much of the Eastern Roman army. This defeat was one of the most significant military disasters in Roman history and fundamentally altered the balance of power between Rome and the Germanic peoples. It marked the beginning of the end of Roman military dominance in the Balkans and contributed to the eventual fall of the Western Roman Empire.
key_figures: Valens (Eastern Roman Emperor, killed in battle)

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (written late 4th century, eyewitness account or near-contemporary)
Jordanes, Getica (written 6th century, covers Gothic perspective)
Archaeological evidence from the Adrianople region

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Adrianople is one of the most securely dated events of the late Roman period. The date of 9 August 378 CE is well-attested in multiple sources. Ammianus Marcellinus provides a near-contemporary account.
cross_references: Led to Theodosius I's appointment as Eastern Roman Emperor (379 CE)
Led to foederati treaty with Goths (382 CE)
Part of broader Gothic migrations and barbarian invasions of the late 4th century

events.title: Gothic migrations and settlement crisis in the Balkans
events.description: In the years leading up to 378 CE, and particularly in 378 CE itself, large numbers of Goths and other Germanic peoples were migrating into Roman territory, fleeing pressure from the Huns in Central Asia. The Roman Empire, under Valens, initially attempted to settle these refugees as foederati (allied troops) within the empire, particularly in the Balkans. However, the settlement process was chaotic and poorly managed. Roman officials allegedly exploited the Goths, providing inadequate food supplies and mistreating them. These tensions came to a head in 378 CE with the Gothic uprising that led to the Battle of Adrianople. This crisis marked the beginning of a new phase in Roman-barbarian relations, characterized by large-scale migrations and the eventual settlement of Germanic peoples within Roman territory.
key_figures: Lupicinus (Roman general, allegedly mistreated the Goths)
events.certainty_note: The Gothic migrations and settlement crisis are well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The chronology and details are secure.
cross_references: Part of broader barbarian migrations of the late 4th century
Led to Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)

events.title: Gratian's response to the Adrianople crisis
events.description: Following the Battle of Adrianople and the death of Valens, Gratian, the Western Roman Emperor, attempted to respond to the crisis in the Eastern Empire. He moved troops eastward and eventually appointed Theodosius I as the new Eastern Roman Emperor in 379 CE. In 378 CE itself, Gratian was consolidating his position and preparing for the succession crisis. His response to the Adrianople disaster was to seek a strong military commander (Theodosius) to stabilize the Eastern Empire, rather than attempting direct military intervention himself.
key_figures: Theodosius I (future Eastern Roman Emperor, not yet appointed in 378 CE)
events.certainty_note: Gratian's general response to the crisis is well-attested; specific actions within 378 CE are less detailed in surviving sources.
cross_references: Led to Theodosius I's appointment (379 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Adrianople as a simple military defeat has been complicated by modern scholarship. Some historians (e.g., Peter Heather) argue that the battle was not as catastrophic as traditionally portrayed and that Roman military capacity was not as thoroughly destroyed as once believed. Additionally, the characterization of the Goths as invaders has been challenged; they were initially refugees fleeing the Huns, and their uprising was a response to Roman mistreatment rather than a deliberate invasion. The role of Valens's religious policies (his Arian sympathies) in contributing to the crisis is debated.
historiographic_note: The year 378 CE is exceptionally well-documented for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding the Battle of Adrianople, which is covered in detail by Ammianus Marcellinus, a near-contemporary source. However, sources are predominantly written from the Roman perspective. The Gothic perspective is preserved mainly in later compilations (Jordanes) and is less detailed. The experiences of ordinary soldiers, refugees, and non-combatants are underrepresented. Archaeological evidence from the Adrianople region is limited.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Adrianople and death of Valens led to Theodosius I's appointment as Eastern Roman Emperor in 379 CE.
The Gothic settlement crisis and mistreatment of refugees led to the Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE.
The Battle of Adrianople eventually led to the foederati treaty with the Goths in 382 CE.

era_context: The late Roman Empire under Eastern Emperor Valens and Western Emperor Gratian. The empire was experiencing increasing pressure from Germanic peoples, particularly the Goths, who were migrating southward from the Danube region. The Arian-Nicene Christian controversy remained a major source of religious and political tension. This year marks the period immediately before the catastrophic Battle of Adrianople (378 CE), during which Gothic refugees were being settled within Roman territory.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Non-Roman regions (Persia, China, India, sub-Saharan Africa) have limited documentation in surviving sources for this specific year.

events.title: Gothic refugees seek settlement within the Roman Empire
events.description: In 377 CE, large numbers of Goths and other Germanic peoples, fleeing pressure from the Huns in Central Asia, sought refuge within the Roman Empire. The Eastern Roman Emperor Valens, facing military pressure and seeking to replenish his armies, agreed to settle these refugees as foederati (allied troops) within the empire, particularly in the Balkans. The settlement process was chaotic and poorly managed. Roman officials allegedly exploited the Goths, providing inadequate food supplies and mistreating them. These tensions would eventually lead to the Gothic uprising and the Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE. The 377 CE settlement represents a critical moment in the transformation of the late Roman Empire, as it marked the beginning of large-scale Germanic settlement within Roman territory.
key_figures: Lupicinus (Roman general, allegedly mistreated the Goths)

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (written late 4th century, covers the settlement)
Jordanes, Getica (written 6th century, covers Gothic perspective)

events.certainty_note: The Gothic settlement in 377 CE is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. Ammianus Marcellinus provides detailed coverage.
cross_references: Led to Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)
Part of broader barbarian migrations of the late 4th century

events.title: Valens's military preparations and frontier defense
events.description: In 377 CE, Valens was preparing for military campaigns against the Goths and other barbarian groups along the Danube frontier. He was consolidating troops and resources for what he believed would be a decisive military campaign. However, his military preparations were hampered by the need to manage the settlement of Gothic refugees and by the complexity of the frontier situation. Valens's military strategy would ultimately prove inadequate, leading to the catastrophic defeat at Adrianople in 378 CE.
events.certainty_note: Valens's military preparations in 377 CE are attested in sources, but specific details are less comprehensive than for 378 CE.
cross_references: Led to Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)

events.title: Religious tensions under Valens's Arian policies
events.description: In 377 CE, the Eastern Roman Empire under Valens was experiencing significant religious tensions. Valens, unlike his predecessor Constantine, favored Arian Christianity over Nicene orthodoxy. His religious policies favored Arian bishops and suppressed Nicene Christians, creating deep divisions within the Christian community. These religious tensions would persist and eventually be resolved (in favor of Nicene orthodoxy) under Theodosius I, who would issue the Edict of Thessalonica in 380 CE. The 377 CE religious situation reflects the broader instability of the late 4th-century empire.
key_figures: Gregory of Nazianzus (Nicene theologian, critic of Valens)

sources.name: Gregory of Nazianzus, Orations (written late 4th century, criticizes Valens's religious policies)
Socrates Scholasticus, Ecclesiastical History (written early 5th century)
Historiographic consensus on late 4th-century Christian disputes

events.certainty_note: Valens's Arian sympathies and the resulting religious tensions are well-attested in contemporary sources, particularly the writings of Gregory of Nazianzus.
cross_references: Led to Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE) under Theodosius I
Led to First Council of Constantinople (381 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Valens as a weak or incompetent emperor has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. Some historians argue that Valens was a capable administrator and military commander, and that his defeat at Adrianople was due to specific tactical errors and unfavorable circumstances rather than general incompetence. Additionally, the narrative of Valens's Arian policies as uniformly oppressive has been complicated; some scholars note that Valens's religious policies were more pragmatic and less uniformly hostile to Nicene Christians than sometimes portrayed.
historiographic_note: The year 377 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding the Gothic settlement and Valens's religious policies. Ammianus Marcellinus provides detailed coverage of the military and political situation. However, sources are predominantly written from the Roman perspective. The Gothic perspective is less detailed. Information on non-Roman regions (Persia, China, India, sub-Saharan Africa) is sparse or absent from surviving sources.
