era_context: The Roman Empire is in the early stages of a civil war between Constantius II and the usurper Magnentius, which began in 350 CE. Magnentius controls much of the Western Empire (Gaul, Spain, Britain, North Africa), while Constantius II controls the East. The conflict is beginning to drain resources and military manpower from both sides. In the East, the Sassanid Persian Empire under Shapur II remains a persistent threat. Christianity is consolidating its position as the dominant religion, with theological disputes over Arianism shaping imperial policy.

events.title: Magnentius consolidates control of Western Roman Empire
events.region: Roman Empire (Gaul, Spain, Britain, North Africa)
events.description: Following his usurpation in 350 CE, Magnentius consolidates his control over the Western Roman Empire. He controls Gaul, Spain, Britain, and North Africa, and is recognized as emperor by the armies and populations of these regions. Magnentius attempts to legitimize his rule by minting coins, issuing edicts, and maintaining the administrative structures of the empire. However, his rule is contested by Constantius II, who controls the Eastern Empire and is preparing military campaigns to reclaim the West. The year 351 CE marks the height of Magnentius's power, before Constantius II's military campaigns begin to turn the tide against him.
events.certainty_note: Magnentius's consolidation of power in the Western Empire is well-attested in multiple sources. The date 351 CE is widely accepted as marking the period of his greatest territorial control.

events.title: Constantius II begins military campaigns against Magnentius
events.description: Constantius II, having consolidated his control of the Eastern Empire, begins military campaigns against the usurper Magnentius. The campaigns start in the Balkans and along the Danube frontier, with Constantius II's forces gradually pushing westward. The civil war, which will last until 353 CE, is now entering its active military phase. Constantius II's superior resources and military organization give him an advantage, but the campaigns are costly and will require several years to complete. The year 351 CE marks the beginning of the military phase of the civil war.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Constantius II's military campaigns beginning in 351 CE is well-attested, but specific battles and dates are not always clearly documented. The year is part of the broader civil war (350–353 CE).
cross_references: Part of broader civil war (350–353 CE)

events.title: Shapur II of Sassanid Persia campaigns in Mesopotamia
events.description: Shapur II, the Sassanid Persian king, continues his military campaigns in Mesopotamia and the Caucasus region. These campaigns are part of a broader pattern of Sassanid expansion and consolidation of control over territories contested with the Roman Empire. Shapur II is one of the most militarily active Sassanid rulers, and his campaigns pose a significant threat to Roman interests in the East. The exact details of campaigns in 351 CE are sparse, but Shapur II's reign (309–379 CE) is marked by continuous military activity. The campaigns are particularly significant because they occur while Constantius II is distracted by the civil war with Magnentius in the West.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (later Islamic source)

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's reign is well-documented, but specific campaigns in 351 CE are not clearly dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader pattern of Sassanid military activity during this period.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman-Sassanid conflict (3rd–7th centuries CE)
Contemporary with Constantius II's civil war with Magnentius

events.title: Synod of Sirmium (first) on Arian controversy
events.description: A church synod is held in Sirmium to address the ongoing Arian controversy. The synod produces a creed that attempts to find a middle ground between Nicene orthodoxy and Arianism, but which is generally seen as leaning toward Arianism. Constantius II, who favors a semi-Arian position, uses imperial authority to enforce the synod's decisions. The synod represents an early stage in the series of councils and synods that will dominate the 350s CE, as Constantius II attempts to impose theological uniformity on the empire. The synod's creed is controversial and will be rejected by later councils.
events.certainty_note: The Synod of Sirmium is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is debated. Some sources place it in 351 CE, others in 352 CE or later. The synod's canons are known, but the precise attendees and proceedings are reconstructed from fragmentary sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Arian controversy (325–381 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Synod of Sirmium is disputed among modern scholars. Some sources place the first synod in 351 CE, others in 352 CE, and still others in later years. The exact number of synods held in Sirmium is also debated—some scholars identify three or four separate synods, while others see them as a single ongoing process. The synod's exact significance and the nature of its creed are also debated. Additionally, the extent to which Constantius II personally endorsed the synod's decisions is unclear from the surviving sources. Regarding Shapur II's campaigns, the specific military actions in 351 CE are not clearly documented in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 351 CE is moderately rich for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding political and military events. Ammianus Marcellinus is the most detailed source, but he is writing retrospectively and may have biases. Ecclesiastical sources provide detailed information about the Arian controversy but are less concerned with political and military events. The record for the Sassanid Empire is limited and often filtered through later Islamic historiography. The narrative is heavily shaped by court-sponsored histories and later Christian compilations, which may emphasize theological disputes over political and military realities. The record for regions outside the Mediterranean and Near East is virtually absent.

era_context: The mid-4th century CE spans the reign of Constantius II (337–361 CE) in the Eastern Roman Empire and a period of rapid succession in the West. Christianity is now the dominant religion of the empire following Constantine's conversion. The Sasanid Persian Empire under Shapur II is at its height, conducting major military campaigns. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty controls the south while the Northern Wei and other northern states fragment the north. This is a period of religious consolidation, military pressure on imperial frontiers, and significant theological controversy within Christianity.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record for this specific year)

events.title: Usurpation of Magnentius in the Western Roman Empire
events.description: Magnentius, a Roman general of mixed Germanic and Roman descent, revolted against Constans I and seized control of the Western Roman Empire. He was proclaimed emperor by his troops in Augustodunum (modern Autun) and rapidly consolidated power across Gaul, Britain, and Hispania. This usurpation triggered a civil war that would dominate the 350s, fragmenting imperial authority in the West and forcing Constantius II to redirect military resources from the Persian frontier. The revolt reflected deep discontent with Constans' rule and the instability of succession mechanisms in the late Roman Empire.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (History of Rome)

events.certainty_note: Multiple near-contemporary sources confirm the usurpation in 350 CE. Ammianus Marcellinus, writing in the late 4th century, provides detailed narrative. The date is secure.
cross_references: Follows from 337-001 (Constantine's death and succession crisis)

events.title: Shapur II's Campaign Against the Kushans and Eastern Frontier
events.description: Shapur II, the Sasanid Great King, conducted military campaigns against the Kushans and other eastern enemies to secure the Sasanid frontier. This campaign was part of Shapur II's broader strategy to consolidate Sasanid power and eliminate threats from Central Asia. The campaign demonstrated Sasanid military organization and Shapur II's aggressive expansionism, which would later be directed against the Roman Empire. These eastern victories strengthened Shapur II's prestige and freed resources for western campaigns.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's eastern campaigns are attested in later sources, but precise dating to 350 CE is approximate. Ammianus places them in the early 350s, but exact chronology is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader Sasanid expansion under Shapur II (309–379 CE)

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty Consolidation Under Yao Xing's Influence
events.description: In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty continued its rule over southern China, though the period was marked by internal factional struggles among the aristocratic families (particularly the Wang and Xie clans) who wielded real power behind the throne. The emperor remained largely ceremonial. This period saw continued cultural flourishing, including advances in calligraphy, painting, and Buddhist philosophy, even as political fragmentation in the north intensified. The Eastern Jin maintained a precarious balance between these powerful families.
key_figures: Emperor Kangdi of Eastern Jin (r. 342–344 CE) or successor

sources.name: Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled by Fang Xuanling et al.
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang

events.certainty_note: Eastern Jin political history is well-documented in Chinese sources, but specific events in 350 CE are less precisely dated. The general context of aristocratic factionalism is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of Eastern Jin Dynasty (317–420 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Magnentius' usurpation is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as a 'major crisis.' Modern scholarship (e.g., Heather, The Fall of the Roman Empire) notes that while disruptive, the usurpation did not threaten the empire's fundamental stability—Constantius II successfully defeated Magnentius by 353 CE. The event is better understood as a succession dispute within an intact system rather than a systemic collapse. Additionally, some sources conflate Magnentius with later usurpers; clarity on his specific policies and support base remains limited.
historiographic_note: The year 350 CE is well-documented for the Roman Empire through Ammianus Marcellinus and other late antique sources, though these are not contemporary. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Sasanid sources are sparse and often transmitted through later Islamic historiography. The record is biased toward political-military events and elite actors; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa and northern Europe are almost entirely absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Magnentius' usurpation directly triggered the civil war culminating in the Battle of Mursa Major
Magnentius' defeat and suicide in 353 CE ended the usurpation
Part of Shapur II's broader military campaigns (350s–360s CE)

era_context: The year 349 CE falls in the reign of Constantius II (337–361 CE) in the Eastern Roman Empire and the final years of Constans I (337–350 CE) in the West. The empire is experiencing theological turmoil following the Council of Nicaea (325 CE), with Arianism gaining imperial favor under Constantius II. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty continues its rule in the south amid aristocratic factionalism. The Sasanid Persian Empire under Shapur II is consolidating power. This is a period of religious controversy, political instability in the West, and military reorganization on multiple frontiers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record)
Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent (limited contemporary documentation)

events.title: Constans I's Continued Rule and Religious Policy in the Western Roman Empire
events.description: Constans I continued his rule over the Western Roman Empire in 349 CE, though his reign was increasingly marked by unpopularity and military instability. He maintained a policy of religious tolerance but was criticized by both pagan traditionalists and Christian factions for perceived weakness. His reliance on the eunuch Eusebius and other court favorites alienated the military aristocracy. This year represents the calm before the storm of Magnentius' usurpation in 350 CE. Constans' reign exemplified the challenges of late Roman succession and the difficulty of maintaining unified imperial authority.
key_figures: Constans I (Western Roman Emperor, r. 337–350 CE)
events.certainty_note: Constans I's reign and policies are well-attested in late antique sources. The year 349 CE falls securely within his reign (337–350 CE).
cross_references: Preceded by 337-001 (Constantine's death and division of empire)

events.title: Theological Disputes and Arianism in the Eastern Roman Empire
events.region: Roman Empire (Eastern provinces, particularly Constantinople and the Levant)
events.description: In 349 CE, theological disputes over Arianism and Nicene orthodoxy continued to dominate religious and political discourse in the Eastern Roman Empire. Constantius II, though nominally orthodox, increasingly favored Arian bishops and used religious policy as a tool of imperial control. The Council of Nicaea (325 CE) had condemned Arianism, but Constantius II's support for Arian theologians created a complex religious landscape. These disputes would culminate in the Council of Constantinople (360 CE) and shape Christian theology for centuries. The year 349 CE represents a critical moment in the Arian controversy, with imperial favor shifting toward Arian positions.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The Arian controversy and Constantius II's religious policies are extensively documented in ecclesiastical sources. The year 349 CE falls within the period of active theological dispute.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Continued Aristocratic Factionalism
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty in southern China continued to experience internal political struggles dominated by aristocratic families, particularly the Wang and Xie clans. The imperial throne remained largely ceremonial, with real power wielded by these families and their military commanders. This period saw continued cultural development, including advances in Buddhist philosophy and the arts, even as political fragmentation persisted. The Eastern Jin maintained control over the Yangtze River valley and southern territories, but faced constant pressure from northern states.
key_figures: Emperor Kangdi of Eastern Jin (r. 342–344 CE) or successor

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Eastern Jin political history is well-documented in Chinese sources, but specific events in 349 CE are not precisely dated. The general context of aristocratic factionalism is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of Eastern Jin Dynasty (317–420 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Constans I as uniformly 'weak' or 'unpopular' is somewhat oversimplified. Modern scholarship (e.g., Lenski, The Cambridge Ancient History) notes that Constans I was militarily competent and successfully defended the Rhine frontier against Germanic tribes. His unpopularity was concentrated among the military aristocracy and was partly a result of his reliance on court eunuchs—a practice that was not unusual in late Roman administration. The sources hostile to Constans (particularly Ammianus Marcellinus, writing after his death) may exaggerate his failings.
historiographic_note: The year 349 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through late antique sources, though these are not contemporary. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. The record is heavily biased toward political-military events and elite religious disputes; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. The perspective is overwhelmingly Mediterranean and East Asian; other regions are absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Constans I's unpopularity and weak position directly enabled Magnentius' usurpation in 350 CE
Theological disputes of 349 CE culminated in the Council of Constantinople (360 CE)

era_context: The year 348 CE falls in the reign of Constantius II (337–361 CE) in the Eastern Roman Empire and Constans I (337–350 CE) in the West. The empire is experiencing theological turmoil following the Council of Nicaea (325 CE), with Arianism gaining imperial favor. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty controls the south while the north remains fragmented among multiple states. The Sasanid Persian Empire under Shapur II is consolidating power and preparing for military expansion. This is a period of religious controversy, political instability, and military reorganization.

events.title: Athanasius of Alexandria's Exile and the Arian Controversy
events.description: Athanasius of Alexandria, the leading defender of Nicene orthodoxy against Arianism, faced continued pressure and exile during this period. Constantius II, though nominally orthodox, increasingly favored Arian bishops and used religious policy as a tool of imperial control. Athanasius' opposition to Arianism and his defense of the Nicene position made him a target for imperial displeasure. His multiple exiles (346–353 CE is one period) exemplified the religious and political turmoil of the era. Athanasius' theological writings and his resistance to imperial pressure became foundational to Christian orthodoxy.
events.certainty_note: Athanasius' exile and the Arian controversy are well-attested, but the precise dating of his exile periods is debated. Some sources place his exile in 346–353 CE, others in different periods. The year 348 CE likely falls within a period of pressure on Athanasius.

events.title: Shapur II's Consolidation of Sasanid Power
events.description: Shapur II, the Sasanid Great King (r. 309–379 CE), continued his consolidation of Sasanid power and military organization in 348 CE. He had recently completed campaigns against the Kushans and other eastern enemies, and was now preparing for major military campaigns against the Roman Empire. Shapur II's reign was marked by aggressive expansionism, religious orthodoxy (Zoroastrianism), and military innovation. His campaigns would eventually lead to major conflicts with Constantius II and Julian in the 350s–360s CE.
key_figures: Shapur II (Sasanid Great King, r. 309–379 CE)

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's reign and consolidation of power are attested in later sources, but precise dating to 348 CE is approximate. The general context of his military preparation is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of Shapur II's reign (309–379 CE)
Led to 358-001 (Shapur II's campaigns against Rome)

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Cultural Flourishing and Political Instability
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty in southern China continued to experience a period of cultural flourishing despite political instability. This era saw significant advances in calligraphy, painting, and Buddhist philosophy. The aristocratic families who controlled the government patronized the arts and supported Buddhist monasteries. The period is known for the works of poets like Tao Yuanming and the development of landscape painting. However, this cultural achievement coexisted with political fragmentation and the constant threat from northern states.
key_figures: Tao Yuanming (poet, though his major works came later)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Eastern Jin cultural history is well-documented in Chinese sources, but specific events in 348 CE are not precisely dated. The general context of cultural flourishing is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of Eastern Jin Dynasty (317–420 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Arian controversy is sometimes presented as a simple binary conflict between 'Arianism' and 'Nicene orthodoxy,' but modern scholarship (e.g., Williams, Arius: Heresy and Tradition) reveals a much more complex theological landscape with multiple positions and factions. Constantius II's religious policy was not simply 'pro-Arian' but rather an attempt to find a middle ground that would preserve imperial unity. The sources hostile to Arianism (particularly Athanasius and later ecclesiastical historians) may exaggerate the coherence and threat of Arian positions.
historiographic_note: The year 348 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through late antique sources, though these are not contemporary. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Sasanid sources are sparse and often transmitted through later Islamic historiography. The record is heavily biased toward political-military events and elite religious disputes; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. The perspective is overwhelmingly Mediterranean and East Asian; other regions are absent from surviving documentation.

In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty controls the south while the north remains fragmented. The Sasanid Persian Empire under Shapur II is consolidating power. This is a period of religious controversy, political instability, and military reorganization on multiple frontiers.

events.title: Council of Sardica (or Serdica) and the Arian Controversy
events.description: The Council of Sardica (modern Sofia, Bulgaria) was convened around 343–347 CE to address the Arian controversy and the status of Athanasius of Alexandria. The council was attended by bishops from both the Eastern and Western Roman Empires, though the Eastern bishops largely boycotted it. The council reaffirmed Nicene orthodoxy and condemned Arianism, but the division between Eastern and Western bishops reflected the broader political and theological split in the empire. The council's decisions were largely ineffective in resolving the controversy, as Constantius II continued to favor Arian bishops. The council is sometimes dated to 343 CE, but 347 CE is also attested in some sources.
events.certainty_note: The Council of Sardica is dated variously to 343 CE or 347 CE in different sources. The exact date is uncertain, with scholarly consensus favoring 343 CE, but 347 CE is also attested. The council's occurrence and general significance are confirmed.

events.title: Constans I's Military Campaigns on the Rhine Frontier
events.description: Constans I, the Western Roman Emperor, conducted military campaigns on the Rhine frontier to defend against Germanic tribes and maintain Roman control over Gaul. These campaigns demonstrated Constans' military competence, though they were often overshadowed by later sources' focus on his unpopularity at court. The Rhine frontier remained a critical area of Roman military activity throughout the 4th century. Constans' campaigns were generally successful in maintaining Roman control, though they required constant military attention and resources.
key_figures: Constans I (Western Roman Emperor, r. 337–350 CE)
events.certainty_note: Constans I's military campaigns on the Rhine are attested in late antique sources, but precise dating to 347 CE is approximate. The general context of Rhine frontier defense is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of Constans I's reign (337–350 CE)

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Aristocratic Factionalism and Cultural Development
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty in southern China continued to experience internal political struggles dominated by aristocratic families. The imperial throne remained largely ceremonial, with real power wielded by the Wang and Xie clans and their military commanders. This period saw continued cultural development, including advances in Buddhist philosophy, calligraphy, and painting. The Eastern Jin maintained control over the Yangtze River valley and southern territories, but faced constant pressure from northern states.

cross_references: Part of Eastern Jin Dynasty (317–420 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Sardica is sometimes presented as a major turning point in the Arian controversy, but modern scholarship (e.g., Hanson, The Search for the Christian Doctrine of God) notes that the council had limited immediate impact. The Eastern bishops' boycott and Constantius II's continued favor toward Arian bishops meant that the council's decisions were largely ineffective. The council is better understood as a symptom of the empire's religious division rather than a solution to it. Additionally, the precise date of the council (343 vs. 347 CE) remains debated among scholars.

Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Sasanid sources are sparse and often transmitted through later Islamic historiography. The record is heavily biased toward political-military events and elite religious disputes; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. The perspective is overwhelmingly Mediterranean and East Asian; other regions are absent from surviving documentation.

events.title: Athanasius of Alexandria's Exile and Return
events.description: Athanasius of Alexandria, the leading defender of Nicene orthodoxy against Arianism, experienced a period of exile and return around 346 CE. After being exiled by Constantius II for his opposition to Arianism, Athanasius was briefly allowed to return to Alexandria in 346 CE, though his position remained precarious. This return was short-lived, as he would be exiled again in 356 CE. Athanasius' multiple exiles and returns exemplified the religious and political turmoil of the era. His theological writings and his resistance to imperial pressure became foundational to Christian orthodoxy.
events.certainty_note: Athanasius' exile and return are attested in ecclesiastical sources, but the precise dating of his exile periods is debated. The year 346 CE is often cited as a period of return, but some sources place his return in different years. The general pattern of exile and return is confirmed.

events.title: Shapur II's Military Preparations and Consolidation
events.description: Shapur II, the Sasanid Great King (r. 309–379 CE), continued his consolidation of Sasanid power and military organization in 346 CE. He had recently completed campaigns against the Kushans and other eastern enemies, and was now preparing for major military campaigns against the Roman Empire. Shapur II's reign was marked by aggressive expansionism, religious orthodoxy (Zoroastrianism), and military innovation. His campaigns would eventually lead to major conflicts with Constantius II and Julian in the 350s–360s CE.

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's reign and consolidation of power are attested in later sources, but precise dating to 346 CE is approximate. The general context of his military preparation is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of Shapur II's reign (309–379 CE)
Led to 358-001 (Shapur II's campaigns against Rome)

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Political Instability and Succession Struggles
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty in southern China experienced political instability and succession struggles in 346 CE. Emperor Kangdi of Eastern Jin died in 344 CE and was succeeded by Emperor Mu of Eastern Jin (r. 345–361 CE). The succession was marked by continued aristocratic factionalism, with the Wang and Xie clans competing for power. The new emperor was young and largely ceremonial, with real power wielded by the aristocratic families. This period saw continued cultural development despite political fragmentation.
key_figures: Emperor Mu of Eastern Jin (r. 345–361 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Emperor Mu's reign and the succession are well-documented in Chinese sources. The year 346 CE falls within the early years of his reign (345–361 CE).
cross_references: Part of Eastern Jin Dynasty (317–420 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: Athanasius' return to Alexandria in 346 CE is sometimes presented as a triumphant vindication of Nicene orthodoxy, but modern scholarship (e.g., Hanson, The Search for the Christian Doctrine of God) notes that the return was temporary and politically motivated. Constantius II allowed Athanasius to return not out of theological conviction but as a political maneuver to consolidate support in Egypt. The return was short-lived, and Athanasius was exiled again in 356 CE. The sources favorable to Athanasius may exaggerate the significance of his brief return.
historiographic_note: The year 346 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through late antique sources, though these are not contemporary. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Sasanid sources are sparse and often transmitted through later Islamic historiography. The record is heavily biased toward political-military events and elite religious disputes; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. The perspective is overwhelmingly Mediterranean and East Asian; other regions are absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Athanasius' return in 346 CE was followed by his second exile in 356 CE
Emperor Mu's reign (345–361 CE) was marked by continued aristocratic factionalism

era_context: The mid-4th century CE saw the consolidation of Christianity within the Roman Empire following Constantine's legalization of the faith. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire under Constantius II was engaged in military campaigns against Persia and internal religious disputes over Arianism. In the West, Roman authority was fragmenting as Germanic tribes pressed the frontiers. China was divided among competing dynasties during the period of disunity following the Han collapse.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological record sparse for specific years)
Mesoamerica (no written records for specific dating)

events.title: Council of Serdica (Synod of Serdica)
events.region: Serdica (modern Sofia, Bulgaria); Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: A major ecumenical council convened to address the Arian controversy and schism within the Christian church. Bishops from both Eastern and Western Roman territories gathered, though the council became divided along regional lines. The Western bishops condemned Arianism and reaffirmed Nicene orthodoxy, while Eastern bishops (many Arian-sympathetic) rejected the council's authority. The council issued canons on ecclesiastical discipline and attempted to reconcile the Eastern and Western churches, but ultimately deepened the schism. This council is sometimes called the 'Synod of Serdica' and represents a critical moment in the formalization of Christian orthodoxy and the East-West ecclesiastical divide.

sources.name: Athanasius, Apologia contra Arianos (Defense Against the Arians)
Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The council's occurrence in 345 is attested in multiple later ecclesiastical histories, but no contemporary primary source documents the exact date. Scholarly consensus places it in 345 or 346 CE, with ±1 year uncertainty. The council's canons survive in later compilations.
cross_references: 325-001 (Council of Nicaea, which Serdica attempted to enforce)
381-001 (First Council of Constantinople, which superseded Serdica)

events.title: Constantius II's Persian Campaign
events.region: Mesopotamia and Persia; Eastern Roman Empire vs. Sassanid Persia
events.description: Emperor Constantius II launched a major military campaign against the Sassanid Persian Empire under Shapur II, continuing the long-standing Roman-Persian conflict over Mesopotamian territory and influence. The campaign involved siege warfare and mobile operations across the Euphrates frontier. While initial Roman successes were achieved, the campaign ultimately proved inconclusive, with neither side achieving decisive victory. The conflict drained resources from both empires and contributed to the broader pattern of frontier instability that would characterize the late 4th century.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Ammianus Marcellinus, writing in the late 4th century, provides the most detailed account of Constantius II's Persian wars. The campaign of 345 is mentioned in the context of ongoing hostilities, though specific battle dates are sometimes uncertain. Scholarly consensus places major operations in 345-346.

events.title: Athanasius Exiled from Alexandria
events.description: Athanasius, Bishop of Alexandria and leading opponent of Arianism, was exiled from his see by order of Emperor Constantius II, who favored Arian theology. This was Athanasius's second exile (the first being under Constantine in 335). The exile lasted until 346 CE and represented the political triumph of Arian factions at court. Athanasius's removal weakened anti-Arian resistance in Egypt and demonstrated the emperor's willingness to use imperial power to enforce theological positions. His exile became a symbol of persecution of orthodox Christianity by an Arian emperor.
events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources confirm Athanasius's exile in 345. The date is well-established in ecclesiastical records.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Council of Serdica has been reassessed by modern scholarship. While traditionally portrayed as a major ecumenical council comparable to Nicaea, recent historians note that it was poorly attended, regionally divided, and had minimal immediate impact on the broader church. Its canons were largely ignored in the East, and it did not resolve the Arian controversy as intended. The council's importance has been somewhat inflated in later ecclesiastical historiography. Additionally, the dating of Constantius II's Persian campaign to 345 specifically is debated; some sources suggest the major operations occurred in 346-347, with 345 being a period of preparation or minor skirmishing.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 345 CE is dominated by ecclesiastical sources, particularly the later compilations of Socrates Scholasticus and Theodoret of Cyrus, written 50-100 years after the events. These sources are biased toward religious history and reflect the theological perspectives of their authors. Secular political and military history is less well-documented. The sources are heavily weighted toward the Eastern Roman Empire and the Mediterranean; information about China, India, and other regions is absent from surviving Western sources. The reliability of specific dates for military campaigns depends on Ammianus Marcellinus, whose account is generally trustworthy but sometimes vague about chronology. Archaeological evidence for this period is limited.

graph_edges.note: Council of Serdica was convened to enforce the decisions of the Council of Nicaea
Serdica's failure to resolve the Arian controversy contributed to the need for the First Council of Constantinople
Constantius II's Persian campaign was part of the ongoing Roman-Persian conflict inherited from Constantine's reign
Athanasius's exile in 345 was the second of multiple exiles during his episcopate

era_context: The mid-4th century CE continued the consolidation of Christianity within the Roman Empire and the deepening of the Arian controversy. Constantius II ruled the Eastern Roman Empire and was engaged in military campaigns against Persia and internal religious conflicts. The Western Roman Empire under Constans was experiencing increasing pressure from Germanic tribes. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty was in decline, with regional warlords gaining power.

events.title: Constans I Visits Rome
events.description: Emperor Constans I, ruler of the Western Roman Empire, made a ceremonial visit to Rome, the first imperial visit to the city in many years. This visit was significant as a symbolic reaffirmation of imperial authority and the importance of Rome as the symbolic center of the empire, even as political power had shifted to other cities. The visit included public ceremonies and demonstrations of imperial power. However, the visit also highlighted the declining military and economic importance of Rome itself, which was no longer the primary seat of imperial administration.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The visit is attested in later sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Some sources place it in 344, others in 343 or 345. The dating is approximate within ±1-2 years.

events.title: Continued Arian-Nicene Disputes in the Eastern Church
events.description: The Arian controversy continued to dominate ecclesiastical politics in the Eastern Roman Empire. Following the Council of Serdica in 345 (or 344, depending on dating), disputes over the nature of Christ and the relationship between the Father and Son remained unresolved. Arian and Nicene factions competed for imperial favor and control of major sees. The controversy was not merely theological but deeply political, with emperors using religious authority to consolidate power and regional bishops using theology to advance their influence.
events.certainty_note: The ongoing nature of the Arian controversy in 344 is well-attested, but specific events for this particular year are not well-documented. The certainty reflects the general historical context rather than specific dated events.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Constans I's visit to Rome has been questioned by modern historians. Some scholars argue that the visit was less ceremonially important than later sources suggest and may have been primarily a military or administrative inspection rather than a grand ceremonial occasion. The exact date of the visit is disputed, with sources varying by 1-2 years. Additionally, the portrayal of Constans as a strong emperor has been revised; some modern scholarship emphasizes his military failures and the growing instability of the Western Empire during his reign.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 344 CE is very sparse. Most information comes from later ecclesiastical histories written 50-100 years after the events, which are biased toward religious history and theological disputes. Secular political and military history is poorly documented. The sources are heavily weighted toward the Eastern Mediterranean and the imperial court; information about the provinces, the military frontier, and non-Christian populations is minimal. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is essentially non-existent.

graph_edges.note: Constans I's visit to Rome was part of the broader political consolidation following Constantine's death
Continued disputes over the Council of Nicaea's decisions

era_context: The mid-4th century CE saw the Roman Empire divided among Constantine's sons, with Constantius II ruling the East and Constans ruling the West. The Arian controversy dominated ecclesiastical politics. In the East, the Sassanid Persian Empire under Shapur II remained a major military threat. China was fragmented among competing dynasties during the period of disunity.

events.title: Council of Serdica (Alternative Dating)
events.region: Serdica (modern Sofia, Bulgaria); Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: Some sources date the Council of Serdica to 343 CE rather than 345 CE. The council was convened to address the Arian controversy and attempted to reconcile Eastern and Western churches. The council issued canons on ecclesiastical discipline and condemned Arianism, but ultimately deepened the schism between Eastern and Western bishops. The exact date of the council remains disputed among modern scholars, with evidence supporting both 343 and 345 as possible dates.
events.certainty_note: The dating of the Council of Serdica is disputed. Some sources place it in 343, others in 345 or 346. The traditional date in some ecclesiastical sources is 343, but modern scholarship is divided. The uncertainty is ±2-3 years.

events.title: Constans I and Constantius II Diplomatic Tensions
events.description: Tensions between the Western Roman Emperor Constans I and the Eastern Roman Emperor Constantius II increased during this period. The two brothers had divided the empire following Constantine's death in 337, but disputes over territorial boundaries, religious policy, and imperial authority created friction. Constans favored the Nicene position in the Arian controversy, while Constantius II was more sympathetic to Arianism. These theological differences reflected deeper political rivalries. The tensions would eventually lead to military conflict in the following years.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of tensions between Constans and Constantius is well-attested, but specific events for 343 are not well-documented. The certainty reflects the broader historical context.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Council of Serdica to 343 is disputed by modern scholarship. Many modern historians prefer 345 or 346 as the date, based on a more careful analysis of the sources. The traditional date of 343 appears in some ecclesiastical sources but may reflect later confusion or conflation with other councils. Additionally, the significance of diplomatic tensions between Constans and Constantius in 343 specifically is unclear; the sources do not provide detailed information about events in this particular year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 343 CE is extremely sparse. Most information comes from later ecclesiastical histories written 50-100 years after the events. Secular political and military history is poorly documented. The sources are heavily weighted toward the Eastern Mediterranean and the imperial court. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is essentially non-existent. The uncertainty about the dating of the Council of Serdica reflects the general unreliability of dating for events in this period.

graph_edges.note: Council of Serdica was convened to enforce the decisions of the Council of Nicaea
Tensions between Constans and Constantius resulted from the division of the empire

era_context: The mid-4th century CE saw the Roman Empire divided among Constantine's sons. Constantius II ruled the East and was engaged in military campaigns against Persia. Constans ruled the West and faced increasing pressure from Germanic tribes. The Arian controversy continued to dominate ecclesiastical politics. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty was in decline.
disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 342 CE is extremely sparse. No specific events of major historical significance are well-documented for this year. The sources that do exist are later ecclesiastical histories written 50-100 years after the events, and they do not provide detailed information about 342 specifically. The year falls within a period of relative stability in the Roman Empire, between the division of the empire in 337 and the major conflicts of the 350s. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is essentially non-existent. The absence of documented events does not mean nothing happened; rather, it reflects the limitations of the surviving historical record.

era_context: The mid-4th century CE saw the Roman Empire divided among Constantine's sons. Constantius II ruled the East and was engaged in military campaigns against Persia. Constans ruled the West. The Arian controversy dominated ecclesiastical politics. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty was in decline. The period was characterized by relative stability in the Roman Empire, though tensions between the imperial brothers were increasing.

events.title: Constantius II's Continued Persian Campaigns
events.region: Mesopotamia and Persia; Eastern Roman Empire vs. Sassanid Persia
events.description: Emperor Constantius II continued military operations against the Sassanid Persian Empire under Shapur II. These campaigns were part of the long-standing Roman-Persian conflict over Mesopotamian territory and influence. The campaigns involved siege warfare and mobile operations across the Euphrates frontier. The conflict was inconclusive, with neither side achieving decisive victory. These operations drained resources from the Eastern Roman Empire and contributed to the broader pattern of frontier instability.
events.certainty_note: Ammianus Marcellinus provides information about Constantius II's Persian campaigns during this period, but specific dating for individual years is uncertain. The campaigns of 341-343 are mentioned in the context of ongoing hostilities, but the exact chronology is unclear. The dating is approximate within ±2-3 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance and dating of Constantius II's Persian campaigns in 341 are uncertain. Ammianus Marcellinus, the primary source for these campaigns, does not provide precise dates for individual operations. Modern scholars have attempted to reconstruct the chronology, but there is disagreement about which campaigns occurred in which years. The campaigns of 341-343 may have been relatively minor operations or preparations for larger campaigns, rather than major military engagements.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 341 CE is extremely sparse. The only documented event is Constantius II's continued military operations against Persia, which are attested in Ammianus Marcellinus's later chronicle. No contemporary primary sources document events in 341 specifically. The sources are heavily weighted toward military history and the imperial court; information about civilian life, the provinces, and non-military events is absent. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is essentially non-existent. The year falls within a period of relative stability in the Roman Empire, and the absence of documented events reflects the limitations of the surviving historical record rather than the absence of historical activity.

graph_edges.note: Constantius II's Persian campaigns were part of the ongoing Roman-Persian conflict inherited from Constantine's reign

era_context: The late Classical period in Greece, dominated by the rise of Macedon under Philip II. The Persian Empire remains a major power in Asia Minor and the Levant under Artaxerxes III. In China, the Warring States period continues with intense interstate competition. This is a pivotal decade for the geopolitical reordering of the Mediterranean and Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness
Central Asia beyond Persian tributary networks

events.title: Siege of Perinthus by Philip II of Macedon
events.region: Thrace, northern Aegean coast (modern Turkey/Bulgaria border)
events.description: Philip II of Macedon besieged the Greek city of Perinthus, a strategic port on the Propontis (Sea of Marmara) and ally of Athens. The siege lasted several months and was ultimately unsuccessful, as the city received support from Athens and possibly Persian naval assistance. This campaign was part of Philip's broader strategy to dominate Thrace and threaten Greek city-states. The failure to take Perinthus marked a rare setback for Philip but did not halt his expansion.
key_figures: Demosthenes (Athenian orator, vocal opponent of Philip)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica (compiled later but drawing on contemporary sources)

events.certainty_note: Attested in multiple ancient sources; Demosthenes' contemporary speeches provide direct evidence of the siege and its political context.
cross_references: 339-001 (Siege of Byzantium, continuation of Philip's Thracian campaign)

events.title: Artaxerxes III's Egyptian campaign and reconquest
events.description: Artaxerxes III Ochus of Persia launched a major military campaign to reconquer Egypt, which had rebelled under native pharaohs (the 30th Dynasty). The campaign involved a large army and navy. After initial setbacks, Persian forces under the general Bagoas achieved success, though the final conquest may have extended into 339 BCE. The reconquest reasserted Persian control over Egypt and its wealth, though the occupation would prove unstable.
key_figures: Nectanebo II (Egyptian pharaoh, resisting Persian reconquest)
events.certainty_note: The campaign is attested in Greek historiographic sources, but dating between 340–339 BCE is approximate. Egyptian sources are fragmentary. The final conquest may have occurred in 339 BCE.

events.title: Demosthenes delivers the First Philippic
events.description: Demosthenes, the Athenian orator, delivered his First Philippic (Olynthiac speech) warning Athens of the threat posed by Philip II's expansion in Macedon and Thrace. The speech urged military action and resource mobilization against Philip's growing power. This oration became a landmark in ancient political rhetoric and reflects the intense anxiety among Greek city-states about Macedonian hegemony. The speech had limited immediate political effect but established Demosthenes as the leading voice against Philip.
events.certainty_note: The text of the First Philippic survives; however, some scholars debate whether it was delivered in 351 or 349 BCE. The traditional dating to 349 BCE is more widely accepted, but 340 BCE is also proposed by some sources. Certainty is moderate due to dating ambiguity.
cross_references: 339-003 (Second Philippic, continuation of Demosthenes' anti-Macedonian campaign)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Demosthenes' First Philippic is disputed; some scholars place it in 351 BCE or 349 BCE rather than 340 BCE. The exact chronology of Artaxerxes III's Egyptian campaign is uncertain, with some sources suggesting it began earlier (342 BCE) or concluded later (339 BCE). The significance of the Perinthus siege has been debated: while traditionally viewed as a major Macedonian setback, some modern scholars argue Philip's strategic objectives were achieved despite the tactical failure.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 340 BCE is dominated by Greek and Persian sources, reflecting the bias of surviving written traditions. Demosthenes' speeches are primary sources but are rhetorical and politically motivated, not neutral historical accounts. Egyptian sources are sparse and fragmentary. Chinese and Indian history for this period are documented in their respective traditions but are underrepresented in Western historiography. The record is skewed toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are less visible.

graph_edges.note: The failed siege of Perinthus was followed by Philip's siege of Byzantium in 339 BCE, continuing his Thracian campaign.
Demosthenes' anti-Macedonian rhetoric contributed to the political context leading to the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE.
The Egyptian reconquest campaign likely extended across 340–339 BCE.

era_context: The final years before Philip II's decisive victory at Chaeronea (338 BCE). Macedon is at the height of its military expansion; Greek city-states are fractured and unable to mount a unified defense. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes III is consolidating control over Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean. In China, the Warring States period continues with the state of Qi rising in prominence.

events.title: Siege of Byzantium by Philip II of Macedon
events.description: Philip II besieged the wealthy Greek city of Byzantium on the Bosporus, a crucial trading hub controlling access to the Black Sea. The siege lasted approximately one year (339–338 BCE) and was ultimately unsuccessful, as the city held out with support from Athens and possibly Persian naval forces. However, the siege demonstrated Philip's reach and ambition to control the straits. The failure did not significantly impede Philip's overall strategic position, as he achieved his primary objective of intimidating Greek city-states.
events.certainty_note: The siege is attested in multiple ancient sources; Demosthenes' contemporary speeches provide corroboration.
cross_references: 338-001 (Battle of Chaeronea, culmination of Philip's expansion)

events.title: Completion of Artaxerxes III's Egyptian reconquest
events.description: The Persian reconquest of Egypt under Artaxerxes III was completed, with the final defeat of Nectanebo II and the native 30th Dynasty. Egypt was reincorporated into the Persian Empire as a satrapy. The conquest was brutal; ancient sources report widespread destruction and desecration of temples. However, Persian control over Egypt would prove short-lived, as the empire faced internal instability and the rise of Macedon.

sources.name: Manetho, History of Egypt (preserved in later compilations)

events.certainty_note: The reconquest is attested in Greek and Egyptian sources, but the exact dating (340–339 BCE) is approximate. Egyptian sources are fragmentary and often damaged.

events.title: Third Sacred War concludes; Amphictyonic Council crisis
events.description: The Third Sacred War, which had raged intermittently since 356 BCE over control of Delphi and the Amphictyonic Council, reached a critical juncture. Philip II of Macedon, having been invited by Thessalian allies to intervene in the conflict, used the war as a pretext to expand his influence into central Greece. The Amphictyonic Council, the religious body governing Delphi, became a tool of Macedonian power. This political maneuver set the stage for Philip's confrontation with Athens and Thebes at Chaeronea in 338 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of the Third Sacred War's conclusion is debated among scholars. Some sources place key events in 338 BCE rather than 339 BCE. The exact sequence of Philip's intervention is uncertain.

events.title: Demosthenes delivers the Third Philippic
events.description: Demosthenes delivered his Third Philippic, a passionate and urgent appeal to Athens to mobilize against Philip II's expansion. The speech emphasized the existential threat posed by Macedon and called for military action and alliances with other Greek city-states. The oration is considered one of the finest examples of ancient Greek political rhetoric. Despite Demosthenes' eloquence, Athens' response was delayed and ultimately insufficient to prevent Philip's victory at Chaeronea.
events.certainty_note: The text of the Third Philippic survives; the date of delivery is traditionally placed in 341 or 340 BCE by some scholars, but 339 BCE is also widely accepted.
cross_references: 338-001 (Battle of Chaeronea, outcome of the political crisis Demosthenes warned about)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Third Sacred War's conclusion and Philip's intervention is disputed; some scholars place the critical events in 338 BCE rather than 339 BCE. The chronology of Demosthenes' Philippics is contested, with scholars proposing dates ranging from 351 to 340 BCE for the First Philippic and 341 to 339 BCE for the Third. The significance of the Byzantium siege has been debated: while traditionally viewed as a failure for Philip, some modern scholars argue it served his strategic purposes by intimidating Greek city-states and demonstrating Macedonian military reach.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 339 BCE is heavily influenced by Greek sources, particularly Demosthenes' speeches and later compilations by Diodorus and Plutarch. These sources are politically motivated and reflect Athenian perspectives. Persian sources are limited; Egyptian sources are fragmentary. The record privileges elite political and military events over social, economic, and cultural developments. The bias toward Greek city-state history obscures the experiences of non-Greek populations in the regions affected by Macedonian expansion.

graph_edges.note: The siege of Byzantium and Philip's continued expansion directly precipitated the Battle of Chaeronea.
The Third Sacred War crisis and Philip's intervention in the Amphictyonic Council set the stage for Chaeronea.
Demosthenes' Third Philippic was a final call to action before the Battle of Chaeronea.
The Persian reconquest of Egypt contributed to the empire's overextension, which would be exploited by Alexander the Great after 336 BCE.

era_context: The pivotal year of the Battle of Chaeronea, which marked the end of Greek city-state independence and the beginning of Macedonian hegemony over Greece. Philip II's victory fundamentally altered the political landscape of the Mediterranean. The Persian Empire remains a major power but is increasingly vulnerable to Macedonian expansion. In China, the Warring States period continues with shifting alliances and military campaigns.

events.title: Battle of Chaeronea
events.description: Philip II of Macedon defeated a coalition of Greek city-states led by Athens and Thebes at the Battle of Chaeronea. The Macedonian phalanx, under the command of the young Alexander (Philip's son), crushed the Greek hoplite forces. The battle resulted in approximately 1,000 Athenian and Theban deaths and marked the end of Greek city-state independence. Philip's victory established Macedonian hegemony over Greece and set the stage for the planned invasion of the Persian Empire. The battle is considered one of the most consequential military engagements in ancient history.
key_figures: Alexander (Philip's son, commanded the Macedonian left wing)

sources.name: Demosthenes, On the Crown (speech delivered later, referencing the battle)

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple ancient sources with consistent details. The date of August 338 BCE is widely accepted by modern scholarship. Archaeological evidence from the battlefield has been identified.

events.title: Artaxerxes III's death and succession crisis in Persia
events.description: Artaxerxes III Ochus died, likely in 338 BCE (some sources place it in 337 BCE). His death triggered a succession crisis in the Persian Empire. The eunuch Bagoas, who had been a powerful general and administrator, orchestrated the succession, initially supporting Artaxerxes III's son Arses and then eliminating him in favor of Darius III. This period of instability weakened the Persian Empire and made it vulnerable to external threats, particularly the rising power of Macedon under Philip II and his son Alexander.
events.certainty_note: The death of Artaxerxes III is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date is disputed (338 or 337 BCE). The succession crisis and Bagoas's role are confirmed, but details are fragmentary.

events.title: Macedonian naval victory at the Battle of the Hellespont
events.description: Philip II's forces achieved a naval victory in the Hellespont, securing Macedonian control over the straits connecting the Aegean Sea to the Black Sea. This victory was crucial for Philip's strategic plans to invade Asia Minor and the Persian Empire. The control of the Hellespont also cut off Persian access to the Aegean and demonstrated Macedonian naval capability, which had previously been limited.
events.certainty_note: The naval victory is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date and details are uncertain. Some sources place it in 337 BCE. The significance of the victory is debated among modern scholars.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Artaxerxes III's death is disputed; some sources place it in 337 BCE rather than 338 BCE. The significance of the Battle of Chaeronea has been reassessed by modern scholars: while traditionally viewed as an absolute turning point in Greek history, some argue that Greek city-states retained considerable autonomy and cultural influence even after the battle. The role of Alexander at Chaeronea is debated; some sources emphasize his leadership of the cavalry charge, while others suggest his role was more limited. The naval victory in the Hellespont is poorly attested and may be conflated with other Macedonian naval operations.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 338 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly later compilations by Diodorus, Plutarch, and Pausanias. These sources are often based on earlier works that are now lost. Demosthenes' speeches provide contemporary Athenian perspectives but are politically motivated. Persian sources are limited and fragmentary. The record privileges the Battle of Chaeronea and its political consequences, while economic, social, and cultural developments are less visible. The bias toward Greek city-state history obscures the experiences of non-Greek populations in the regions affected by Macedonian expansion.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Chaeronea directly led to the formation of the Corinthian League under Macedonian hegemony.
Philip's victory at Chaeronea set the stage for his assassination in 336 BCE and Alexander's succession.
Artaxerxes III's death triggered a succession crisis that continued into 337 BCE.
The Battle of Chaeronea and Macedonian hegemony over Greece enabled Alexander's invasion of Persia in 334 BCE.

era_context: The year following the Battle of Chaeronea, marked by the consolidation of Macedonian power over Greece and preparations for the invasion of the Persian Empire. The Persian Empire is in internal turmoil following Artaxerxes III's death. In China, the Warring States period continues with the state of Qi experiencing significant military campaigns. This is a transitional year between the Classical Greek period and the Hellenistic age.

events.title: Formation of the Corinthian League (League of Corinth)
events.description: Philip II convened a congress of Greek city-states at Corinth and established the Corinthian League (also called the League of Corinth or Hellenic League). The league was ostensibly a voluntary association of Greek states, but it was effectively under Macedonian hegemony. Philip was elected hegemon (leader) of the league, and the league agreed to support a planned invasion of the Persian Empire. The formation of the league represented the political consolidation of Macedonian power over Greece and provided the legal and organizational framework for the Persian campaign.
key_figures: Philip II of Macedon (hegemon of the league)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander (references the league)

events.certainty_note: The formation of the Corinthian League is attested in multiple ancient sources. The date of 337 BCE is widely accepted, though some sources place it in 338 BCE.
cross_references: 338-001 (Battle of Chaeronea, which enabled the league's formation)
336-003 (Philip's assassination, which affected the league's leadership)

events.title: Darius III becomes Persian Great King
events.description: Following the death of Artaxerxes III and the brief reign of Arses, Darius III (originally named Artashata) became the Persian Great King, likely in 337 BCE (some sources place it in 336 BCE). Darius III was not of direct royal descent; he was elevated to the throne by the eunuch Bagoas, who wielded considerable power. Darius III's reign was marked by attempts to stabilize the empire and prepare for the threat posed by Macedon. However, internal instability and the loss of Egypt weakened the empire's position.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander (references Darius III)

events.certainty_note: Darius III's accession is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date is disputed (337 or 336 BCE). The role of Bagoas is confirmed, but details are fragmentary.
cross_references: 334-002 (Alexander's invasion of Persia, which Darius III would face)

events.title: Philip II's marriage to Cleopatra Eurydice
events.description: Philip II married Cleopatra Eurydice, a Macedonian noblewoman of royal descent. This marriage was politically significant because it produced a potential heir of pure Macedonian blood, which threatened the position of Alexander (Philip's son by his first wife, Olympias of Epirus). The marriage created dynastic tension and contributed to the political instability that would lead to Philip's assassination in 336 BCE. The marriage also reflected Philip's efforts to consolidate power among the Macedonian nobility.
key_figures: Alexander (Philip's son, threatened by the marriage)
events.certainty_note: The marriage is attested in multiple ancient sources. The date of 337 BCE is widely accepted, though some sources place it in 336 BCE.
cross_references: 336-003 (Philip's assassination, which may have been related to dynastic tensions)

events.title: Macedonian advance force crosses into Asia Minor
events.description: Philip II sent an advance force of Macedonian troops across the Hellespont into Asia Minor to begin preparations for the planned invasion of the Persian Empire. The force, commanded by Parmenion and Attalus, secured a beachhead and began reconnaissance and military operations against Persian-held territories. This advance force was intended to establish a foothold and gather intelligence for the main invasion, which was planned for the following year.
events.certainty_note: The advance force is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date and details are uncertain. Some sources place the crossing in 336 BCE.
cross_references: 336-003 (Philip's assassination, which interrupted the invasion plans)
334-001 (Alexander's invasion of Persia, which continued the campaign)

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Darius III's accession is disputed; some sources place it in 336 BCE rather than 337 BCE. The date of Philip's marriage to Cleopatra Eurydice is also debated, with some sources placing it in 336 BCE. The significance of the marriage in contributing to Philip's assassination is speculative; modern scholars debate whether dynastic tensions were the primary cause or merely a contributing factor. The advance force crossing into Asia Minor is poorly attested, and some sources suggest it occurred in 336 BCE or even later.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 337 BCE is based primarily on later compilations by Diodorus, Plutarch, and Arrian, which are often based on earlier works that are now lost. Contemporary sources are limited. The record privileges political and military events, particularly those related to Philip II and the Macedonian expansion. Persian sources are fragmentary and limited. The bias toward Greek and Macedonian perspectives obscures the experiences of Persian, Egyptian, and other populations affected by these events. Economic, social, and cultural developments are less visible in the surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The formation of the Corinthian League was interrupted by Philip's assassination in 336 BCE.
Darius III's accession set the stage for his confrontation with Alexander's invasion in 334 BCE.
Philip's marriage to Cleopatra Eurydice created dynastic tensions that may have contributed to his assassination.
The advance force crossing into Asia Minor was the precursor to Alexander's full invasion in 334 BCE.

era_context: The year of Philip II's assassination and Alexander the Great's accession to the Macedonian throne. This pivotal year marks the transition from Philip's reign to Alexander's, which would reshape the ancient world through military conquest and cultural diffusion. The Persian Empire under Darius III faces the imminent threat of Macedonian invasion. In China, the Warring States period continues with ongoing military campaigns and political realignments.

events.title: Philip II of Macedon is assassinated
events.description: Philip II of Macedon was assassinated at the wedding of his daughter Cleopatra in the city of Aegae. The assassin was Pausanias, a Macedonian nobleman. The motives for the assassination are debated by ancient sources and modern scholars; theories include personal grievance, dynastic rivalry (related to Philip's marriage to Cleopatra Eurydice), and possible involvement of Olympias (Alexander's mother) or Persian agents. Philip's death was sudden and unexpected, occurring just as he was preparing to launch the invasion of the Persian Empire. The assassination had immediate consequences for the succession and the stability of Macedon.
events.certainty_note: Philip's assassination is attested in multiple ancient sources with consistent basic facts. The date of 336 BCE is widely accepted. However, the motives and the extent of involvement by other parties are disputed.
cross_references: 337-003 (Philip's marriage to Cleopatra Eurydice, which created dynastic tensions)
336-002 (Alexander's consolidation of power following the assassination)

events.title: Alexander the Great becomes King of Macedon
events.description: Following Philip II's assassination, Alexander, then approximately 20 years old, was proclaimed King of Macedon by the Macedonian army and nobility. Alexander's succession was not automatic; he faced potential rivals and had to consolidate his power quickly. He secured the support of the Macedonian generals, particularly Parmenion, and moved swiftly to eliminate potential threats to his rule. Within months, Alexander had secured his position and was preparing to continue his father's planned invasion of the Persian Empire. His accession marked the beginning of one of history's most consequential reigns.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's accession is attested in multiple ancient sources. The date of 336 BCE is widely accepted. The process of consolidating power is well-documented.
cross_references: 335-001 (Alexander's campaigns in Greece and the Balkans to secure his position)

events.title: Alexander secures Macedonian hegemony over Greece
events.description: In the months following his accession, Alexander moved to secure Macedonian control over the Greek city-states and the Corinthian League. Some Greek cities, particularly Athens and Thebes, attempted to exploit the transition in Macedonian leadership to reassert their independence. Alexander responded swiftly with military campaigns, reasserting Macedonian hegemony and ensuring the loyalty of the Greek city-states. By the end of 336 BCE, Alexander had consolidated his control over Greece and was ready to proceed with the invasion of Persia.
key_figures: Demosthenes (Athenian orator, opponent of Macedonian hegemony)
events.certainty_note: Alexander's consolidation of control over Greece is attested in ancient sources, but the exact chronology and details of individual campaigns are uncertain. Some events may have occurred in 335 BCE.
cross_references: 335-001 (Alexander's campaigns in Greece and the Balkans)

events.title: Darius III consolidates Persian power and prepares for invasion
events.description: Darius III, the new Persian Great King, worked to consolidate his power and prepare the empire for the anticipated Macedonian invasion. He faced internal challenges, including the need to stabilize the succession and manage the powerful eunuch Bagoas. Darius III also worked to strengthen the Persian military and secure the empire's borders. However, the Persian Empire was weakened by internal instability, the loss of Egypt, and the fragmentation of authority among regional satraps. These weaknesses would become apparent when Alexander invaded in 334 BCE.
events.certainty_note: Darius III's consolidation of power is inferred from the political context and later accounts of the Persian Empire's state when Alexander invaded. Direct contemporary evidence is limited.
cross_references: 337-002 (Darius III's accession as Persian Great King)
334-002 (Alexander's invasion of Persia, which Darius III would face)

disconfirming_evidence: The motives for Philip's assassination are highly disputed. Ancient sources propose various theories (personal grievance, dynastic rivalry, Persian involvement), but modern scholars debate which, if any, is accurate. Some scholars argue that the assassination may have been a simple act of personal revenge rather than a politically motivated conspiracy. The extent of Olympias's involvement in the assassination is speculative and contested. The chronology of Alexander's consolidation of power is uncertain; some events attributed to 336 BCE may have occurred in 335 BCE. The significance of Alexander's early actions in securing Greece has been reassessed by modern scholars; some argue that Greek city-states retained considerable autonomy even after Alexander's campaigns.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 336 BCE is based primarily on later compilations by Diodorus, Plutarch, and Arrian, which are often based on earlier works that are now lost. Contemporary sources are limited. The record privileges political and military events, particularly those related to Philip II's assassination and Alexander's succession. The motives for the assassination are heavily influenced by later political narratives and may not reflect historical reality. Persian sources are fragmentary and limited. The bias toward Greek and Macedonian perspectives obscures the experiences of Persian and other populations. Economic, social, and cultural developments are less visible in the surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Philip's assassination directly led to Alexander's accession as King of Macedon.
Alexander's accession required him to consolidate Macedonian hegemony over Greece.
Alexander's consolidation of control over Greece enabled him to launch the invasion of Persia in 334 BCE.
Darius III's consolidation of Persian power set the stage for his confrontation with Alexander's invasion.
Philip's assassination and Alexander's accession led to campaigns to secure Alexander's position in Greece and the Balkans.

era_context: The Macedonian Empire under Philip II is at its zenith, having consolidated control over Greece and the Balkans. Philip's son Alexander is now 21 years old and has recently returned from exile in Illyria. The Persian Achaemenid Empire remains the dominant power in Asia, Egypt, and the Levant under Artaxerxes III Ochus. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin consolidating power in the west. This is the final year before Alexander's accession and the beginning of his campaigns that will reshape the Mediterranean and Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (contemporary but no synchronization with Old World chronology)

events.title: Philip II of Macedon assassinated; Alexander becomes king
events.description: Philip II, king of Macedon and architect of Macedonian hegemony over Greece, is assassinated at the wedding of his daughter Cleopatra Eurydice in Aegae (modern Vergina). The assassination is carried out by Pausanias, a disgruntled nobleman. Alexander, Philip's 20-year-old son by Olympias, is immediately proclaimed king by the Macedonian assembly and the army. This event marks the end of Philip's reign and the beginning of Alexander's rise to power, though his position is initially precarious due to rivals and rebellious Greek city-states.
events.certainty_note: The assassination of Philip II in 336 BCE is one of the most securely dated events in ancient history, attested by multiple ancient sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence from the royal tombs at Vergina. However, the exact year is sometimes given as 336 BCE in modern scholarship; the discrepancy arises from ancient calendar systems. Using the proleptic Gregorian calendar, this event occurred in 336 BCE, not 335 BCE.

events.title: Alexander suppresses rebellion in Thessaly and southern Greece
events.description: Following his accession, Alexander moves swiftly to consolidate his power in Greece. He marches south through Thessaly, where he is recognized as leader of the Thessalian League (a position his father held). He then moves to Corinth, where the Greek city-states recognize him as hegemon (leader) of the Hellenic League, a position previously held by Philip. The city of Thebes, however, remains restless and will rebel the following year. This campaign establishes Alexander's authority over Greece and prevents the fragmentation of the Macedonian-Greek alliance that Philip had built.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Alexander's early campaigns is somewhat uncertain. Ancient sources place these events in the year following Philip's death, but the exact dating within 336–335 BCE is debated. Most modern scholars place the consolidation of Greece in 335 BCE, after the Theban rebellion of 336 BCE.

events.title: Theban rebellion and destruction by Alexander
events.description: Thebes, one of the most powerful Greek city-states, rebels against Macedonian hegemony, possibly encouraged by rumors of Alexander's death in the north (fighting against Illyrian tribes). Alexander responds with remarkable speed, marching south and besieging Thebes. After a brief siege, the city is taken by storm. Alexander orders the destruction of Thebes as a warning to other potential rebels: the city is razed, its population enslaved or killed (estimates range from 6,000 to 30,000 deaths), and only temples and the house of the poet Pindar are spared. This act of terror effectively ends Greek resistance to Macedonian rule and allows Alexander to prepare for his Asian campaign without fear of rebellion at home.
events.certainty_note: The destruction of Thebes is one of the most famous events of Alexander's early reign and is attested by multiple ancient sources. However, the exact dating is debated: some sources place it in 336 BCE (immediately after Philip's death), while others place it in 335 BCE. Modern scholarship is divided, with many placing it in 335 BCE after Alexander's consolidation of power in the north.

events.title: Alexander campaigns against Illyrian and Thracian tribes
events.description: Before turning his attention to Asia, Alexander secures the northern frontier of Macedon by campaigning against Illyrian and Thracian tribes. He crosses the Danube River (the ancient Ister), becoming the first Macedonian king to do so, and defeats the Getae tribe on the far bank. These campaigns ensure that Macedon's northern borders are secure and prevent potential threats from the rear while Alexander is campaigning in Asia. The campaigns also demonstrate Alexander's military genius and his ability to move rapidly with his army.
key_figures: Cleitus the Black (general, later famous at the Hydaspes River)
events.certainty_note: The northern campaigns are attested in ancient sources, but the exact chronology is uncertain. Most scholars place them in 335 BCE, between the consolidation of Greece and the invasion of Asia Minor in 334 BCE.

events.title: Artaxerxes III Ochus rules the Persian Achaemenid Empire
events.region: Persian Achaemenid Empire (Egypt, Levant, Mesopotamia, Anatolia, Central Asia)
events.description: Artaxerxes III Ochus continues his reign as the Great King of Persia. He is known for his military campaigns to suppress rebellions in Egypt and the Levant, and for his efforts to centralize Persian power. In 335 BCE, the Persian Empire remains the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and Near East, though it faces increasing pressure from Macedon under Alexander. Artaxerxes III will die in 338 BCE, and the empire will enter a period of instability that will facilitate Alexander's conquest.
