sources.name: Ctesias, Persica (fragments preserved in later sources)

events.certainty_note: Artaxerxes III Ochus's reign is well-attested in ancient sources. He ruled from approximately 358 to 338 BCE, so 335 BCE falls within his reign.

events.title: Qin state consolidates power in China during Warring States period
events.region: China (Qin state, central and western China)
events.description: In China, the Qin state, located in the western part of China, continues to consolidate its power during the Warring States period (475–221 BCE). Under the leadership of successive rulers, Qin implements administrative reforms and military innovations that will eventually allow it to unify China. In 335 BCE, Qin is one of several competing states, but its centralized government and military strength are beginning to set it apart. The Qin will eventually conquer all other states and establish the Qin Dynasty in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Qin rulers of the period (specific ruler in 335 BCE uncertain)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for this period are preserved in later compilations. The exact events of 335 BCE in Qin are not well-documented in surviving sources. The dating of Warring States events is uncertain and may differ by several years depending on the source.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Philip II's assassination is traditionally given as 336 BCE, not 335 BCE. Modern scholarship is divided on whether the event occurred in 336 or 335 BCE, depending on the calendar system used and how ancient sources are interpreted. Some scholars argue that the Theban rebellion occurred in 336 BCE (immediately after Philip's death), while others place it in 335 BCE. The exact chronology of Alexander's early campaigns is uncertain due to the nature of ancient sources, which were written centuries after the events. Additionally, the destruction of Thebes is sometimes portrayed as a singular act of terror, but some modern scholars argue that the destruction was less complete than ancient sources suggest, and that the city was later rebuilt.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 335 BCE is heavily dominated by Greek and Macedonian sources, particularly the later biographical accounts of Alexander by Plutarch and Arrian. These sources are based on earlier histories that are now lost, and they were written centuries after the events they describe. The perspective is decidedly pro-Alexander and Macedonian-centric. Persian sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of the Persian Empire in this period comes primarily from Greek sources, which are often hostile or biased. Chinese sources are preserved in later compilations and may contain chronological errors. The destruction of Thebes is a dramatic event that may have been exaggerated in later accounts for rhetorical effect. Archaeological evidence from Macedon (particularly the royal tombs at Vergina) provides some corroboration for the chronology of Philip's death and Alexander's accession, but does not resolve all uncertainties.

graph_edges.note: Philip's assassination prompted Alexander to consolidate his power in Greece
Alexander's consolidation of Greece led to the suppression of the Theban rebellion
The destruction of Thebes secured Greece and allowed Alexander to invade Asia Minor
Alexander's northern campaigns secured Macedon's borders before the Asian invasion
Artaxerxes III Ochus ruled Persia while Alexander prepared to invade

era_context: Alexander the Great, now firmly established as king of Macedon and hegemon of Greece, launches his invasion of the Persian Achaemenid Empire. This year marks the beginning of one of history's most consequential military campaigns, which will result in the conquest of the Persian Empire and the spread of Hellenistic culture across the Mediterranean and Near East. The Persian Empire, though still vast and powerful, is weakened by internal instability following the death of Artaxerxes III Ochus. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin consolidating power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (contemporary but no synchronization with Old World chronology)

events.title: Alexander invades Asia Minor; Battle of the Granicus River
events.description: Alexander crosses the Hellespont (Dardanelles Strait) with an army of approximately 35,000 infantry and 5,000 cavalry, beginning his invasion of the Persian Achaemenid Empire. He lands in northwestern Anatolia (ancient Phrygia) and immediately encounters a Persian force commanded by satraps (provincial governors) of the region. At the Granicus River (modern Biga River), Alexander defeats the Persian cavalry in a decisive battle, killing approximately 1,000 Persian soldiers while suffering only about 110 casualties. This victory opens the way for Alexander to advance into Anatolia and demonstrates the superiority of Macedonian tactics and discipline over Persian forces. The victory also boosts Alexander's prestige and attracts Greek mercenaries and allies to his cause.
key_figures: Darius III Codomannus (Persian Great King, not present at this battle)

sources.name: Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great

events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Granicus River is one of the most famous battles of antiquity and is attested by multiple ancient sources. The date of 334 BCE is well-established in ancient chronologies. However, the exact casualty figures and some tactical details are debated among modern scholars.

events.title: Alexander advances through Anatolia; siege of Miletus
events.region: Anatolia (Ionia, western coast of modern Turkey)
events.description: Following his victory at the Granicus River, Alexander advances southward through Anatolia, liberating Greek cities from Persian rule. He besieges and captures the city of Miletus, an important Greek city on the Ionian coast that had been under Persian control. The siege demonstrates Alexander's ability to conduct siege warfare and his use of naval support. The capture of Miletus and other coastal cities begins to undermine Persian naval power in the Aegean Sea, which had been a major advantage for Persia.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Miletus is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 334 BCE. The exact duration and details of the siege are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Siege of Halicarnassus
events.region: Anatolia (Caria, southwestern coast of modern Turkey)
events.description: Alexander continues his advance southward along the Anatolian coast and besieges the city of Halicarnassus, the capital of the Persian satrapy of Caria. The city is defended by Memnon of Rhodes, a capable Persian general, and a strong garrison. The siege is lengthy and difficult, lasting several months. Alexander eventually captures the city through a combination of siege warfare and assault, though Memnon and some of the garrison escape by sea. The capture of Halicarnassus further consolidates Alexander's control of the Anatolian coast and eliminates a major Persian stronghold.
key_figures: Ada of Caria (Persian satrap, later allied with Alexander)
events.certainty_note: The siege of Halicarnassus is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 334 BCE (continuing into 333 BCE). The exact duration and details are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Darius III Codomannus becomes Persian Great King
events.description: Darius III Codomannus becomes the Great King of Persia following the death of Artaxerxes III Ochus in 338 BCE and a period of instability. Darius III is a capable military commander but faces challenges in consolidating his power and dealing with rebellions in Egypt and the Levant. He is aware of Alexander's invasion of Anatolia but does not immediately respond with his full military force. Darius III will eventually face Alexander in battle at Issus in 333 BCE and at Gaugamela in 331 BCE.
events.certainty_note: Darius III's reign is well-attested in ancient sources. He became Great King around 336 BCE and ruled until his death in 330 BCE. The exact date of his accession is somewhat uncertain, but he was definitely ruling in 334 BCE.

events.title: Alexander visits the Oracle of Apollo at Didyma
events.region: Anatolia (Ionia, western coast of modern Turkey)
events.description: During his campaign in Anatolia, Alexander visits the Oracle of Apollo at Didyma (near Miletus), one of the most important oracles in the Greek world. The oracle, which had been silent for many years, is said to have spoken to Alexander, pronouncing him invincible. This event is significant for Alexander's propaganda and for his self-presentation as a divinely favored leader. The oracle's pronouncement boosts the morale of his troops and enhances his prestige among the Greek cities he is liberating from Persian rule.
key_figures: The priestess of Apollo at Didyma
events.certainty_note: The visit to Didyma is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date and the oracle's pronouncement are somewhat uncertain. The event may have been embellished in later accounts for propaganda purposes.

events.title: Aristotle's Lyceum continues in Athens under Theophrastus
events.description: In Athens, the philosophical school founded by Aristotle, the Lyceum, continues to operate. Aristotle himself is absent, having left Athens after the death of Alexander's father Philip II (due to anti-Macedonian sentiment in Athens). The school is being managed by Theophrastus, one of Aristotle's most accomplished students. The Lyceum is a center of learning and philosophical inquiry, covering subjects such as logic, metaphysics, ethics, politics, natural philosophy, and biology. The school will continue to be an important intellectual center for centuries.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The existence of the Lyceum in 334 BCE is well-established, but the exact details of its operation and Theophrastus's role are somewhat uncertain. Aristotle left Athens around 323 BCE, so he was not present in 334 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Granicus River is sometimes portrayed as a decisive victory that immediately opened the way for Alexander to conquer the Persian Empire. However, modern scholars note that the battle was primarily a cavalry engagement and that Persian resistance continued for several more years. The casualty figures reported in ancient sources (1,000 Persian soldiers killed vs. 110 Macedonian casualties) are likely exaggerated. The oracle at Didyma is said to have pronounced Alexander invincible, but this account may be a later embellishment designed to enhance Alexander's prestige. Some modern scholars question whether the oracle actually spoke to Alexander or whether this is a fabrication by later historians.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 334 BCE is dominated by Greek and Macedonian sources, particularly the accounts of Alexander's campaigns by Arrian, Plutarch, and Diodorus Siculus. These sources are based on earlier histories that are now lost, and they were written centuries after the events they describe. The perspective is decidedly pro-Alexander and Macedonian-centric. Persian sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of the Persian Empire in this period comes primarily from Greek sources, which are often hostile or biased. The accounts of Alexander's campaigns are often written in a heroic or propagandistic style, emphasizing Alexander's military genius and divine favor. Archaeological evidence from Anatolia provides some corroboration for the locations of battles and sieges, but does not resolve all uncertainties about the exact chronology and details of events.

graph_edges.note: Victory at the Granicus River allowed Alexander to advance through Anatolia
Capture of Miletus was followed by the siege of Halicarnassus
The Granicus victory set the stage for the Battle of Issus
Darius III's rule led to his confrontation with Alexander at Issus
The oracle at Didyma was visited during Alexander's Anatolian campaign

era_context: Alexander continues his conquest of the Persian Achaemenid Empire, advancing from Anatolia into Syria and the Levant. This year sees the decisive Battle of Issus, where Alexander defeats the Persian Great King Darius III in direct combat for the first time. The victory at Issus demonstrates Alexander's military superiority and begins the process of dismantling the Persian Empire. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin consolidating power. In Egypt, the Persian satrapy remains under Persian control but will soon fall to Alexander.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (contemporary but no synchronization with Old World chronology)

events.title: Battle of Issus; Alexander defeats Darius III
events.description: Alexander, advancing southward through Anatolia and into Syria, encounters the Persian Great King Darius III and his army near the city of Issus in Cilicia. The battle is fought in a narrow coastal plain, which negates the Persian advantage in numbers (Darius III commanded an army estimated at 100,000 to 600,000 soldiers, while Alexander had approximately 35,000). Alexander's superior tactics and the discipline of his Macedonian phalanx allow him to defeat the Persian forces decisively. Darius III flees the battlefield, abandoning his family and treasure. The victory at Issus is a turning point in Alexander's campaign, demonstrating that he can defeat the Persian Great King in direct combat. The battle also gives Alexander control of Syria and the Levant, and opens the way for his invasion of Egypt.

sources.name: Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Issus is one of the most famous battles of antiquity and is attested by multiple ancient sources. The date of 333 BCE is well-established in ancient chronologies. However, the exact casualty figures and some tactical details are debated among modern scholars. The location of the battle has been identified with reasonable confidence at the Payas River in southern Turkey.

events.title: Alexander captures Darius III's family and treasure at Issus
events.description: Following the Battle of Issus, Alexander captures the Persian royal camp, which contains Darius III's family (including his mother Sisygambis, his wife Stateira, and his children) and a vast amount of treasure. The capture of the royal family is a significant propaganda victory for Alexander, as it demonstrates his dominance over the Persian Great King. Alexander treats the royal family with respect, which enhances his prestige and may have encouraged other Persian nobles to defect to his cause. The treasure captured at Issus provides Alexander with the resources to continue his campaign.
key_figures: Darius III Codomannus (Persian Great King, absent)
events.certainty_note: The capture of Darius III's family and treasure is well-attested in ancient sources. The details of Alexander's treatment of the royal family are somewhat uncertain, but the general account is consistent across sources.

events.title: Alexander advances into Phoenicia; siege of Tyre begins
events.description: Following his victory at Issus, Alexander advances southward into Phoenicia, the coastal region of the Levant. He encounters the city of Tyre, a major Phoenician city and an important naval base for the Persian Empire. Tyre is located on an island off the coast, making it difficult to assault. Alexander decides to besiege the city, beginning a lengthy siege that will last several months and continue into 332 BCE. The siege of Tyre is one of the most famous sieges of antiquity, as Alexander constructs a causeway from the mainland to the island to allow his siege engines to reach the city walls.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Tyre is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 333–332 BCE. The exact duration and details of the siege are somewhat uncertain, but the general account is consistent across sources.

events.title: Alexander visits Jerusalem (traditional account)
events.description: According to Jewish tradition preserved in later sources, Alexander visits Jerusalem during his campaign in the Levant. The high priest of the Jewish temple is said to have come out to meet Alexander, and Alexander is said to have shown respect for the Jewish religion and the temple. This account is significant for Jewish-Hellenistic relations and for Alexander's reputation as a respectful conqueror. However, the historicity of this account is disputed by modern scholars, and it may be a later embellishment or fabrication.
key_figures: The high priest of Jerusalem (name uncertain)
events.certainty_note: The account of Alexander's visit to Jerusalem is preserved in Jewish sources, particularly in Josephus's Antiquities of the Jews, which was written in the 1st century CE, more than 250 years after Alexander's death. The historicity of this account is highly disputed by modern scholars. Some scholars argue that Alexander may have passed through Judea, but the specific account of his visit to Jerusalem and his respect for the temple is likely a later embellishment or fabrication designed to enhance the prestige of the Jewish people and their religion.

events.title: Qin state continues consolidation during Warring States period
events.region: China (Qin state, central and western China)
events.description: In China, the Qin state continues to consolidate its power during the Warring States period. The Qin implements administrative reforms and military innovations that will eventually allow it to unify China. In 333 BCE, Qin is one of several competing states, but its centralized government and military strength are beginning to set it apart. The Qin will eventually conquer all other states and establish the Qin Dynasty in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Qin rulers of the period (specific ruler in 333 BCE uncertain)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for this period are preserved in later compilations. The exact events of 333 BCE in Qin are not well-documented in surviving sources. The dating of Warring States events is uncertain and may differ by several years depending on the source.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Issus is sometimes portrayed as a decisive victory that immediately led to the collapse of the Persian Empire. However, modern scholars note that the Persian Empire continued to resist for several more years, and Darius III remained a threat until his death in 330 BCE. The casualty figures reported in ancient sources (Persian casualties estimated at 100,000 or more, while Macedonian casualties were minimal) are likely exaggerated. The account of Alexander's visit to Jerusalem is highly disputed by modern scholars, and many argue that it is a later fabrication or embellishment. Some scholars suggest that Alexander may have passed through Judea, but the specific account of his visit to the temple and his respect for the Jewish religion is likely a later invention.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 333 BCE is dominated by Greek and Macedonian sources, particularly the accounts of Alexander's campaigns by Arrian, Plutarch, and Diodorus Siculus. These sources are based on earlier histories that are now lost, and they were written centuries after the events they describe. The perspective is decidedly pro-Alexander and Macedonian-centric. Persian sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of the Persian Empire in this period comes primarily from Greek sources, which are often hostile or biased. The accounts of Alexander's campaigns are often written in a heroic or propagandistic style, emphasizing Alexander's military genius and divine favor. The account of Alexander's visit to Jerusalem comes from Jewish sources written centuries after the events, and its historicity is highly disputed. Archaeological evidence from the Levant provides some corroboration for the locations of battles and sieges, but does not resolve all uncertainties about the exact chronology and details of events.

graph_edges.note: Victory at Issus resulted in the capture of Darius III's family and treasure
Victory at Issus allowed Alexander to advance into Phoenicia and besiege Tyre
The siege of Tyre continued into 332 BCE
The Granicus victory set the stage for the Battle of Issus
The Issus victory set the stage for the Battle of Gaugamela

era_context: Alexander continues his conquest of the Persian Achaemenid Empire, consolidating his control of the Levant and preparing to invade Egypt. This year sees the completion of the siege of Tyre and Alexander's invasion of Egypt, where he is recognized as pharaoh and the son of the god Amun. The Persian Empire is weakening, though Darius III remains at large and will attempt to mount a final resistance. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin consolidating power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (contemporary but no synchronization with Old World chronology)

events.title: Completion of the siege of Tyre; city destroyed
events.description: After a siege lasting approximately seven months (beginning in 333 BCE), Alexander finally captures the city of Tyre. The siege is one of the most famous in antiquity, as Alexander constructs a causeway from the mainland to the island to allow his siege engines to reach the city walls. The city is eventually taken by storm, and Alexander orders the destruction of Tyre as a warning to other potential rebels. The population is either killed or enslaved (estimates range from 6,000 to 30,000 deaths). The destruction of Tyre eliminates a major Persian naval base and demonstrates Alexander's determination to crush resistance. The causeway built by Alexander remains visible for centuries and eventually becomes a permanent land bridge.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Tyre is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 333–332 BCE. The exact duration and casualty figures are somewhat uncertain, but the general account is consistent across sources. Archaeological evidence from Tyre provides some corroboration for the siege.

events.title: Alexander invades Egypt; recognized as pharaoh
events.description: Following the destruction of Tyre, Alexander advances into Egypt, which had been under Persian control. The Egyptians, who resented Persian rule, welcome Alexander as a liberator. Alexander is crowned pharaoh of Egypt and is recognized as the son of the god Amun-Ra by the Egyptian priesthood. This event is significant for Alexander's self-presentation as a divinely favored leader and for his integration into Egyptian religious and political traditions. Alexander spends several months in Egypt, consolidating his control and establishing himself as the legitimate successor to the pharaohs. He also visits the Oracle of Amun at Siwa, where he is said to have received a prophecy confirming his divine parentage.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's invasion of Egypt and his recognition as pharaoh are well-attested in ancient sources and are dated to 332 BCE. Egyptian temple inscriptions provide some corroboration for his recognition as pharaoh. However, the details of his coronation and his visit to the Oracle of Siwa are somewhat uncertain.
cross_references: 332-003 (Alexander's visit to the Oracle of Siwa)

events.title: Alexander visits the Oracle of Siwa; receives prophecy of divine parentage
events.description: During his stay in Egypt, Alexander travels to the Oracle of Siwa in the western desert to consult the oracle of Amun-Ra. The oracle is said to have confirmed Alexander's divine parentage, pronouncing him the son of Amun-Ra (the Egyptian equivalent of Zeus). This event is significant for Alexander's self-presentation as a divinely favored leader and for his integration into Egyptian religious traditions. The prophecy enhances Alexander's prestige and legitimacy as pharaoh of Egypt. However, the exact details of the oracle's pronouncement are uncertain, and the account may have been embellished in later sources.
key_figures: The priestess of Amun at Siwa
events.certainty_note: The visit to Siwa is attested in ancient sources, but the exact details of the oracle's pronouncement are uncertain. The account may have been embellished in later sources for propaganda purposes. Some modern scholars question whether Alexander actually visited Siwa, though most accept that he did.

events.title: Alexander founds the city of Alexandria in Egypt
events.description: During his stay in Egypt, Alexander founds the city of Alexandria on the Mediterranean coast. The city is designed by the Greek architect Dinocrates and is intended to be a major port and center of Hellenistic culture. Alexandria will become one of the most important cities in the ancient world, serving as the capital of the Ptolemaic Kingdom after Alexander's death. The city is laid out on a grid plan and includes a famous lighthouse (the Pharos) and a great library (the Library of Alexandria). The founding of Alexandria is one of Alexander's most lasting achievements, as the city will remain a major center of learning and culture for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The founding of Alexandria is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 332 BCE. Archaeological evidence from Alexandria provides some corroboration for the city's founding and layout. However, some details about the founding (such as the role of Dinocrates) are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Darius III prepares for final confrontation with Alexander
events.description: While Alexander is consolidating his control of Egypt, Darius III is preparing for a final confrontation with the Macedonian king. Darius III gathers a large army from across the Persian Empire, including troops from Mesopotamia, Persia, and other provinces. He plans to meet Alexander in battle in Mesopotamia, where he hopes to use his numerical superiority to defeat the Macedonian forces. This preparation will lead to the Battle of Gaugamela in 331 BCE, which will be the final major battle between Alexander and Darius III.
events.certainty_note: The preparation of Darius III's army is attested in ancient sources, but the exact details and timing are somewhat uncertain. The sources focus primarily on Alexander's perspective, so information about Persian preparations is limited.

events.title: Qin state continues consolidation during Warring States period
events.region: China (Qin state, central and western China)
events.description: In China, the Qin state continues to consolidate its power during the Warring States period. The Qin implements administrative reforms and military innovations that will eventually allow it to unify China. In 332 BCE, Qin is one of several competing states, but its centralized government and military strength are beginning to set it apart. The Qin will eventually conquer all other states and establish the Qin Dynasty in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Qin rulers of the period (specific ruler in 332 BCE uncertain)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for this period are preserved in later compilations. The exact events of 332 BCE in Qin are not well-documented in surviving sources. The dating of Warring States events is uncertain and may differ by several years depending on the source.

disconfirming_evidence: The siege of Tyre is sometimes portrayed as a quick and decisive victory, but in fact it lasted approximately seven months and was one of the most difficult sieges Alexander undertook. The casualty figures reported in ancient sources are likely exaggerated. The account of Alexander's visit to the Oracle of Siwa is attested in ancient sources, but some modern scholars question whether the visit actually occurred or whether it is a later embellishment. The founding of Alexandria is well-attested, but some details about the city's founding (such as the role of Dinocrates) are uncertain. The account of Alexander's recognition as pharaoh is attested in Egyptian temple inscriptions, but the exact nature of his coronation and his integration into Egyptian religious traditions are somewhat uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 332 BCE is dominated by Greek and Macedonian sources, particularly the accounts of Alexander's campaigns by Arrian, Plutarch, and Diodorus Siculus. These sources are based on earlier histories that are now lost, and they were written centuries after the events they describe. The perspective is decidedly pro-Alexander and Macedonian-centric. Egyptian sources are limited, but temple inscriptions provide some corroboration for Alexander's recognition as pharaoh. The accounts of Alexander's campaigns are often written in a heroic or propagandistic style, emphasizing Alexander's military genius and divine favor. Archaeological evidence from Tyre and Alexandria provides some corroboration for the locations of events, but does not resolve all uncertainties about the exact chronology and details of events.

graph_edges.note: Completion of the siege of Tyre allowed Alexander to invade Egypt
Alexander's invasion of Egypt led to his visit to the Oracle of Siwa
Alexander's invasion of Egypt led to the founding of Alexandria
The siege of Tyre, begun in 333 BCE, was completed in 332 BCE
Darius III's preparation for battle led to the Battle of Gaugamela

era_context: Alexander the Great faces the Persian Achaemenid Empire in its final major battle at Gaugamela in Mesopotamia. This year marks the decisive defeat of Darius III and the effective end of Persian resistance to Alexander's conquest. Following the battle, Alexander advances into Mesopotamia and Persia, consolidating his control over the former Persian Empire. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin consolidating power. This year represents a turning point in world history, as the Hellenistic age begins to replace the Persian imperial order.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (contemporary but no synchronization with Old World chronology)

events.title: Battle of Gaugamela; Alexander defeats Darius III decisively
events.description: Alexander, having consolidated his control of Egypt and the Levant, advances northward into Mesopotamia to confront Darius III and the Persian army. The two armies meet near the village of Gaugamela (also called the Battle of Arbela) on the plain of Mesopotamia. Darius III commands a massive army, estimated at 100,000 to 1,000,000 soldiers (the exact number is uncertain), while Alexander has approximately 47,000 troops. Despite being heavily outnumbered, Alexander uses superior tactics and the discipline of his Macedonian phalanx to defeat the Persian forces decisively. Darius III flees the battlefield once again, and the Persian army is routed. The Battle of Gaugamela is the final major battle between Alexander and Darius III, and it effectively ends Persian resistance to Alexander's conquest. The victory opens the way for Alexander to advance into Mesopotamia and Persia.

sources.name: Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Gaugamela is one of the most famous battles of antiquity and is attested by multiple ancient sources. The date of 331 BCE is well-established in ancient chronologies. However, the exact casualty figures and some tactical details are debated among modern scholars. The location of the battle has been identified with reasonable confidence near Erbil in modern Iraq.

events.title: Alexander advances into Mesopotamia; captures Babylon
events.description: Following his victory at Gaugamela, Alexander advances southward into Mesopotamia. He captures the city of Babylon, the ancient capital of the Babylonian Empire and one of the most important cities in the Persian Empire. The city surrenders without a prolonged siege, and Alexander is welcomed by the population. Alexander enters Babylon in triumph and is recognized as the legitimate successor to the Babylonian kings. He spends several months in Babylon, consolidating his control and establishing himself as the ruler of Mesopotamia. The capture of Babylon is a symbolic victory, as it represents the end of Persian rule and the beginning of Hellenistic rule in the Near East.
key_figures: Mazaeus (Persian satrap of Babylon, surrenders to Alexander)
events.certainty_note: The capture of Babylon is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 331 BCE. Babylonian cuneiform inscriptions provide some corroboration for Alexander's entry into the city. The exact details of the surrender and Alexander's reception are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Alexander advances to Susa; captures Persian royal treasury
events.description: Continuing his advance eastward, Alexander marches to the city of Susa, one of the major capitals of the Persian Empire. The city surrenders without a prolonged siege, and Alexander captures the Persian royal treasury, which contains an enormous amount of gold and silver. The capture of Susa's treasury provides Alexander with the resources to continue his campaign and to reward his troops. The treasury also symbolizes the transfer of Persian imperial power to Alexander. Alexander spends time in Susa consolidating his control and establishing himself as the successor to the Persian Great Kings.
events.certainty_note: The capture of Susa is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 331 BCE. The exact amount of treasure captured is uncertain, but the sources agree that it was enormous.

events.title: Darius III flees eastward; attempts to rally resistance
events.description: Following his defeat at Gaugamela, Darius III flees eastward into Persia, attempting to rally resistance against Alexander. He gathers remnants of his army and attempts to organize a final stand, but his position is increasingly desperate. Darius III is aware that Alexander is pursuing him and that his empire is collapsing. He will eventually be assassinated by his own generals in 330 BCE, ending the Achaemenid dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Darius III's flight following the Battle of Gaugamela is well-attested in ancient sources. The exact details of his movements and his attempts to rally resistance are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Qin state continues consolidation during Warring States period
events.region: China (Qin state, central and western China)
events.description: In China, the Qin state continues to consolidate its power during the Warring States period. The Qin implements administrative reforms and military innovations that will eventually allow it to unify China. In 331 BCE, Qin is one of several competing states, but its centralized government and military strength are beginning to set it apart. The Qin will eventually conquer all other states and establish the Qin Dynasty in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Qin rulers of the period (specific ruler in 331 BCE uncertain)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for this period are preserved in later compilations. The exact events of 331 BCE in Qin are not well-documented in surviving sources. The dating of Warring States events is uncertain and may differ by several years depending on the source.

events.title: Aristotle's Lyceum continues in Athens under Theophrastus
events.description: In Athens, the philosophical school founded by Aristotle, the Lyceum, continues to operate under the direction of Theophrastus. The school is a center of learning and philosophical inquiry, covering subjects such as logic, metaphysics, ethics, politics, natural philosophy, and biology. The school will continue to be an important intellectual center for centuries. Theophrastus is one of Aristotle's most accomplished students and will eventually become the head of the Lyceum after Aristotle's death.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The existence of the Lyceum in 331 BCE is well-established, but the exact details of its operation and Theophrastus's role are somewhat uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Gaugamela is sometimes portrayed as a quick and decisive victory, but in fact it was a hard-fought battle that lasted several hours. The casualty figures reported in ancient sources (Persian casualties estimated at 100,000 or more, while Macedonian casualties were minimal) are likely exaggerated. Some modern scholars argue that the Persian army was not as large as ancient sources suggest, and that the battle was more closely contested than traditional accounts indicate. The capture of Babylon and Susa is sometimes portrayed as occurring without resistance, but some sources suggest that there was at least some military action. The exact chronology of Alexander's advance through Mesopotamia and Persia is somewhat uncertain, and different sources give slightly different accounts of the sequence of events.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 331 BCE is dominated by Greek and Macedonian sources, particularly the accounts of Alexander's campaigns by Arrian, Plutarch, and Diodorus Siculus. These sources are based on earlier histories that are now lost, and they were written centuries after the events they describe. The perspective is decidedly pro-Alexander and Macedonian-centric. Persian sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of the Persian Empire in this period comes primarily from Greek sources, which are often hostile or biased. The accounts of Alexander's campaigns are often written in a heroic or propagandistic style, emphasizing Alexander's military genius and divine favor. Babylonian cuneiform inscriptions provide some corroboration for Alexander's entry into Babylon, but Persian sources are largely absent. Archaeological evidence from Mesopotamia and Persia provides some corroboration for the locations of events, but does not resolve all uncertainties about the exact chronology and details of events.

graph_edges.note: Victory at Gaugamela allowed Alexander to advance into Mesopotamia and capture Babylon
Capture of Babylon was followed by the capture of Susa
Defeat at Gaugamela forced Darius III to flee eastward
The Issus victory set the stage for the Battle of Gaugamela
Darius III's flight led to his assassination in 330 BCE

era_context: The Hellenistic Age is beginning. Alexander III of Macedon has conquered the Persian Achaemenid Empire and is consolidating control over the eastern Mediterranean, Mesopotamia, Egypt, and the Iranian plateau. The classical Greek city-state system is being superseded by large territorial monarchies. In China, the Warring States period continues with intense interstate competition. In India, the Mauryan Empire is consolidating power under Chandragupta.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones

events.title: Battle of Gaugamela and Fall of Persepolis
events.description: Alexander defeated Darius III at Gaugamela (near modern Mosul) in October 330 BCE, decisively ending Achaemenid military resistance. Darius fled eastward and was later assassinated. Alexander subsequently marched to Persepolis, the ceremonial capital of the Persian Empire, and burned the royal palace—a symbolic act marking the complete conquest of the Persian state. This victory consolidated Alexander's control over the former Persian territories and established him as the undisputed ruler of the eastern Mediterranean and Near East.
events.certainty_note: The battle and burning of Persepolis are attested in multiple near-contemporary Greek sources compiled within 300–400 years of the event. The date of October 330 BCE is well-established in historiographic consensus.
cross_references: Led to 329-001 (Pursuit of Darius and consolidation in Bactria)

events.title: Execution of Darius III and Consolidation of Persian Territories
events.description: Following his flight from Gaugamela, Darius III was pursued eastward by Alexander. In July 330 BCE, Darius was overtaken and killed by Bessus, the satrap of Bactria, who sought to preserve Persian independence. Bessus proclaimed himself successor as Artaxerxes V. Alexander's subsequent pursuit and execution of Bessus (in 329 BCE) eliminated the last organized Persian resistance and secured Alexander's claim to the Persian throne. The death of Darius marked the definitive end of the Achaemenid dynasty after nearly 220 years of rule.
key_figures: Bessus (Satrap of Bactria, later Artaxerxes V)
events.certainty_note: The death of Darius and the succession of Bessus are attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in July 330 BCE by modern scholarship, though ancient sources vary slightly.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya Consolidates Power in Northern India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, with the aid of the political strategist Kautilya (Vishnugupta), consolidated control over the Magadha region and began expanding the Mauryan Empire across northern India. By 330 BCE, Chandragupta had eliminated the Nanda dynasty and was establishing the administrative and military foundations of what would become the largest empire in pre-Islamic India. The Mauryan Empire would eventually extend from the Indus Valley to the Bay of Bengal.
key_figures: Kautilya / Vishnugupta (political strategist and author of Arthashastra)
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Chandragupta's power is attested in later Sanskrit sources and the Puranas. The precise dating of events in the 330s BCE is uncertain; Indian chronology for this period relies on later compilations and is subject to scholarly debate. The Arthashastra may have been compiled centuries after Kautilya's lifetime.

events.title: Qi State Dominance in Warring States China
events.description: During the Warring States period, the state of Qi remained one of the most powerful polities in China. Under the rule of King Xuan of Qi (ruled 319–301 BCE), Qi pursued aggressive military expansion and diplomatic maneuvering. In 330 BCE, Qi was consolidating its position as a major regional power, though it would face increasing pressure from the rising state of Qin to the west. The period was characterized by intensive interstate warfare, diplomatic alliances, and the development of military theory and statecraft.
key_figures: King Xuan of Qi (Qi ruler, r. 319–301 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are compiled in texts written centuries later. The Shiji is the primary source, but its chronology for this era is subject to scholarly revision. Exact dating of events in 330 BCE is approximate.
cross_references: Contemporary with 330-001 (Alexander's conquests in the west)
Part of broader Warring States competition

disconfirming_evidence: The burning of Persepolis is traditionally attributed to Alexander as a deliberate act of vengeance against Persian aggression in Greece. However, some modern scholars (e.g., based on archaeological evidence and reexamination of sources) suggest the destruction may have been more accidental or less symbolically motivated than later Greek sources imply. The date of Darius's death is given as July 330 BCE in most sources, but some ancient accounts place it slightly differently. The extent of Chandragupta's actual control in 330 BCE is debated; some scholars argue the consolidation of the Mauryan Empire occurred more gradually over the 320s BCE. The traditional dating of Chandragupta's reign relies heavily on later sources and synchronisms with Greek accounts of Alexander, which are themselves uncertain.
historiographic_note: The year 330 BCE is well-documented for the Hellenistic world through Greek and later Greco-Roman sources, though these are not contemporary and reflect Greek perspectives. The burning of Persepolis, in particular, is a heavily mythologized event in Western historiography. For India, sources are sparse and compiled centuries later; Indian chronology for this period is debated among scholars. For China, the Shiji provides a framework, but its accuracy for specific years in the Warring States period is uncertain. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean and Near East; events in sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Gaugamela was the culmination of Alexander's campaign against Darius III, which began with the Battle of Issus in 331 BCE
The victory at Gaugamela and burning of Persepolis prompted Alexander's pursuit of Darius and subsequent campaigns in Bactria and Sogdiana
The death of Darius and succession of Bessus led directly to Alexander's pursuit and execution of Bessus in 329 BCE
Chandragupta's consolidation of power in 330 BCE laid the foundation for the rapid expansion of the Mauryan Empire in the following decades

era_context: Alexander III continues his consolidation of the former Persian Empire, now pursuing remaining resistance in Bactria and Sogdiana. The Hellenistic world is being reshaped by Alexander's conquests. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is expanding the Mauryan Empire. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin rising as a dominant power. In Greece, the city-states remain under Macedonian hegemony.

events.title: Alexander's Campaign in Bactria and Sogdiana
events.description: Alexander pursued the remnants of Persian resistance into Bactria and Sogdiana (modern Afghanistan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan). These regions, ruled by Bessus (who had proclaimed himself Artaxerxes V), were heavily fortified and inhabited by nomadic and semi-nomadic peoples resistant to Macedonian rule. Alexander's campaigns in 329–327 BCE were among his most difficult, involving siege warfare, guerrilla resistance, and harsh terrain. The conquest of these regions extended Macedonian control to the edges of the known Greek world and brought Alexander into contact with Central Asian peoples and trade routes.
key_figures: Bessus (Satrap of Bactria, self-proclaimed Artaxerxes V)
events.certainty_note: Alexander's campaigns in Bactria and Sogdiana are well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The campaign lasted from 329–327 BCE, with 329 marking the beginning of intensive operations in the region.
cross_references: Led to 327-001 (Completion of Bactrian conquest and marriage to Roxana)

events.title: Execution of Bessus
events.description: Bessus, the satrap of Bactria who had proclaimed himself Artaxerxes V after the death of Darius III, was captured by Alexander's forces in 329 BCE. Bessus was executed—accounts vary on whether he was crucified or flayed alive—as punishment for his role in Darius's assassination and his resistance to Alexander's rule. The execution of Bessus eliminated the last claimant to the Persian throne and consolidated Alexander's position as the legitimate successor to the Achaemenid dynasty.
key_figures: Bessus (Satrap of Bactria, self-proclaimed Artaxerxes V)
events.certainty_note: The capture and execution of Bessus are attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in 329 BCE by modern scholarship, though some sources place it slightly later.
cross_references: Follows from 330-002 (Death of Darius III and succession of Bessus)
Led to 327-001 (Consolidation of Alexander's rule in Central Asia)

events.title: Mauryan Empire Expansion Under Chandragupta
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continued his rapid expansion of the Mauryan Empire across northern India. By 329 BCE, the Mauryan state had consolidated control over much of the Gangetic plain and was beginning to expand southward and westward. The empire's growth was facilitated by Chandragupta's military innovations, administrative reforms (attributed to Kautilya), and the use of espionage and diplomacy. The Mauryan Empire would become the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent by the early 3rd century BCE.
events.certainty_note: The expansion of the Mauryan Empire is attested in later Sanskrit sources. The precise dating of events in 329 BCE is uncertain; Indian chronology for this period relies on later compilations and synchronisms with Greek accounts.

events.title: Qin State Expansion in Warring States China
events.description: The state of Qin, located in the western part of China, was undergoing rapid military and administrative development under the rule of King Huiwen of Qin (r. 338–311 BCE). Qin pursued aggressive territorial expansion and military reforms, including the adoption of advanced military tactics and the development of a centralized bureaucracy. By 329 BCE, Qin was emerging as a major power in the Warring States system, though it would not achieve hegemony until the late 3rd century BCE.
key_figures: King Huiwen of Qin (Qin ruler, r. 338–311 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are compiled in texts written centuries later. The Shiji is the primary source, but its chronology for this era is subject to scholarly revision.
cross_references: Contemporary with 329-001 (Alexander's campaigns in Central Asia)
Part of broader Warring States competition

disconfirming_evidence: The method of Bessus's execution is disputed in ancient sources; some accounts describe crucifixion, others flaying, and still others simply execution. The exact date of Bessus's capture and execution is placed in 329 BCE by most modern scholars, but some sources suggest it may have occurred in 328 BCE. The extent and pace of Chandragupta's expansion in 329 BCE is debated; some scholars argue the consolidation was more gradual. The chronology of the Warring States period in China is subject to ongoing scholarly revision, and the precise dating of events in 329 BCE is approximate.
historiographic_note: The year 329 BCE is well-documented for Alexander's campaigns through Greek and later Greco-Roman sources, though these are not contemporary. The sources emphasize Alexander's military achievements and tend to downplay the difficulty and brutality of the campaigns. For India, sources are sparse and compiled centuries later; the chronology is debated. For China, the Shiji provides a framework, but its accuracy for specific years is uncertain. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean, Near East, and parts of Asia; events in sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The victory at Gaugamela in 330 BCE prompted Alexander's pursuit of Darius and subsequent campaigns in Bactria and Sogdiana beginning in 329 BCE
The execution of Bessus consolidated Alexander's control over Bactria and allowed him to consolidate his rule in Central Asia
Chandragupta's expansion of the Mauryan Empire eventually led to conflict with Seleucus I, Alexander's successor in the Near East

era_context: Alexander III is consolidating his control over Bactria and Sogdiana, facing continued resistance from local populations and rebel leaders. The Hellenistic world is being reshaped by Alexander's conquests and the establishment of new cities and administrative structures. In India, the Mauryan Empire continues its expansion. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing competition among major powers.

events.title: Spitamenes' Rebellion in Sogdiana
events.description: Spitamenes, a Sogdian nobleman and rebel leader, led a major uprising against Alexander's rule in Sogdiana. Spitamenes had initially cooperated with Alexander against Bessus but subsequently rebelled, seeking to preserve Sogdian independence. The rebellion lasted from approximately 329–327 BCE and involved guerrilla warfare, raids on Macedonian forces, and alliances with nomadic tribes from the steppes. Spitamenes' resistance was one of the most serious challenges to Alexander's authority in Central Asia and demonstrated the difficulty of controlling the region.
events.certainty_note: Spitamenes' rebellion is well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The rebellion lasted several years (329–327 BCE), with 328 BCE marking a period of intense conflict.
cross_references: Led to 327-001 (Completion of Bactrian conquest and suppression of rebellion)

events.title: Massacre at the Court of Alexander (Philotas and Parmenion)
events.description: In 328 BCE, Alexander executed Philotas, a high-ranking Macedonian general and son of Parmenion, on charges of conspiracy. Philotas was accused of knowing about a plot against Alexander and failing to report it. Following Philotas's execution, Alexander ordered the assassination of Parmenion, Philotas's elderly father and one of Alexander's most experienced generals, who was serving as a satrap in Ecbatana. These executions marked a turning point in Alexander's reign, as he increasingly relied on terror and eliminated potential rivals. The deaths of Philotas and Parmenion shocked the Macedonian court and demonstrated Alexander's willingness to eliminate even his most loyal supporters.
events.certainty_note: The executions of Philotas and Parmenion are well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in 328 BCE by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Led to 327-002 (Alexander's increasing paranoia and adoption of Persian customs)

events.title: Mauryan Empire Consolidation and Administrative Development
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continued to consolidate and expand the Mauryan Empire, implementing administrative reforms and military innovations. The Arthashastra, attributed to Kautilya, provides a detailed account of the administrative and military systems developed during this period. By 328 BCE, the Mauryan Empire had become a major power in the Indian subcontinent, with a centralized bureaucracy, a standing army, and an extensive intelligence network. The empire's rapid growth was facilitated by Chandragupta's military genius and Kautilya's political and administrative innovations.
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of the Mauryan Empire is attested in later Sanskrit sources. The precise dating of events in 328 BCE is uncertain; Indian chronology for this period relies on later compilations.

events.title: Qin State Military Reforms and Territorial Expansion
events.description: The state of Qin continued its military and administrative development under King Huiwen of Qin. Qin pursued aggressive territorial expansion, incorporating neighboring states and developing advanced military tactics. The state's success was based on military innovation, administrative centralization, and the recruitment of talented strategists and generals. By 328 BCE, Qin was consolidating its position as one of the major powers in the Warring States system.
key_figures: King Huiwen of Qin (Qin ruler, r. 338–311 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are compiled in texts written centuries later. The Shiji is the primary source, but its chronology for this era is subject to scholarly revision.
cross_references: Contemporary with 328-001 (Spitamenes' rebellion in Central Asia)
Part of broader Warring States competition

disconfirming_evidence: The charges against Philotas are disputed by modern scholars. Some argue that Philotas was innocent and that Alexander used the conspiracy as a pretext to eliminate a potential rival. The extent of Philotas's actual knowledge of the conspiracy is unclear from the sources. The execution of Parmenion, in particular, is viewed by some scholars as a politically motivated murder rather than a justified punishment. The chronology of Spitamenes' rebellion is debated; some sources place key events in 327 BCE rather than 328 BCE. The extent of Chandragupta's control in 328 BCE is uncertain; some scholars argue the consolidation was more gradual.
historiographic_note: The year 328 BCE is well-documented for Alexander's campaigns and court intrigues through Greek and later Greco-Roman sources, though these are not contemporary and reflect Greek perspectives. The sources on Philotas and Parmenion are particularly subject to bias, as they were written after Alexander's death and may reflect the perspectives of Alexander's supporters or critics. For India, sources are sparse and compiled centuries later; the chronology is debated. For China, the Shiji provides a framework, but its accuracy for specific years is uncertain. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean, Near East, and parts of Asia; events in sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Spitamenes' rebellion was a continuation of resistance to Alexander's rule that began after the execution of Bessus in 329 BCE
The executions of Philotas and Parmenion marked a turning point in Alexander's reign, leading to increased paranoia and adoption of Persian customs
Chandragupta's consolidation of the Mauryan Empire eventually led to conflict with Seleucus I

era_context: Alexander III is consolidating his control over Bactria and Sogdiana and preparing for his invasion of India. The Hellenistic world is being reshaped by Alexander's conquests. In India, the Mauryan Empire is at the height of its early expansion. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin rising as a dominant power. In Greece, the city-states remain under Macedonian hegemony.

events.title: Alexander's Marriage to Roxana and Consolidation of Bactrian Rule
events.description: In 327 BCE, Alexander married Roxana (Roxana), the daughter of the Bactrian nobleman Oxyartes. The marriage was a political act designed to consolidate Alexander's rule over Bactria by legitimizing his authority through a union with the local aristocracy. Roxana was reportedly of great beauty, and the marriage was celebrated with a grand wedding feast. The marriage also marked the completion of Alexander's conquest of Bactria and Sogdiana, as the rebellion of Spitamenes had been suppressed by this time. Roxana would become Alexander's primary wife and would bear him a son, Alexander IV, after his death.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's marriage to Roxana is well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in 327 BCE by modern scholarship.

events.title: Alexander's Adoption of Persian Customs and Court Intrigues
events.description: In 327 BCE, Alexander increasingly adopted Persian customs and court practices, including the wearing of Persian dress, the practice of proskynesis (ritual obeisance), and the adoption of Persian administrative structures. These changes were controversial among the Macedonian court and military, who viewed them as a betrayal of Greek and Macedonian traditions. The adoption of Persian customs reflected Alexander's vision of a unified Greco-Persian empire and his desire to be accepted as a legitimate successor to the Achaemenid kings. However, the changes also contributed to growing tensions within Alexander's court and military, as some Macedonian nobles resisted what they saw as Alexander's transformation into an Oriental despot.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's adoption of Persian customs is well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The process was gradual, but 327 BCE marks a period of intensified adoption of Persian practices.
cross_references: Follows from 328-002 (Executions of Philotas and Parmenion)
Led to 325-001 (Mutiny at the Hyphasis River)

events.title: Mauryan Empire Reaches Peak of Early Expansion
events.description: By 327 BCE, the Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta had reached the peak of its early expansion, controlling much of northern and central India. The empire had consolidated its administrative structures and military forces, establishing a centralized bureaucracy and a standing army. The Mauryan Empire was now the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent, and Chandragupta was recognized as one of the major rulers of the ancient world. The empire's rapid growth was facilitated by Chandragupta's military genius and Kautilya's political and administrative innovations.
events.certainty_note: The expansion of the Mauryan Empire is attested in later Sanskrit sources. The precise dating of events in 327 BCE is uncertain; Indian chronology for this period relies on later compilations and synchronisms with Greek accounts.

events.title: Qin State Consolidation and Territorial Expansion
events.description: The state of Qin continued its consolidation and territorial expansion under King Huiwen of Qin. Qin pursued aggressive military campaigns against neighboring states, incorporating new territories and expanding its power base. The state's success was based on military innovation, administrative centralization, and the recruitment of talented strategists. By 327 BCE, Qin was consolidating its position as one of the major powers in the Warring States system, though it would not achieve hegemony until the late 3rd century BCE.
key_figures: King Huiwen of Qin (Qin ruler, r. 338–311 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are compiled in texts written centuries later. The Shiji is the primary source, but its chronology for this era is subject to scholarly revision.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States competition

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Alexander's marriage to Roxana is debated by modern scholars. Some argue that it was primarily a political act with little romantic significance, while others suggest that Alexander may have had genuine affection for Roxana. The extent to which Alexander's adoption of Persian customs was a deliberate policy versus a gradual cultural assimilation is disputed. Some scholars argue that Alexander's adoption of Persian customs was less radical than the Greek sources suggest, and that he maintained a fundamentally Macedonian identity. The chronology of the Mauryan Empire's expansion is debated; some scholars argue that the consolidation was more gradual than the traditional accounts suggest.
historiographic_note: The year 327 BCE is well-documented for Alexander's campaigns and personal life through Greek and later Greco-Roman sources, though these are not contemporary. The sources on Alexander's adoption of Persian customs reflect Greek perspectives and may be biased against such practices. For India, sources are sparse and compiled centuries later; the chronology is debated. For China, the Shiji provides a framework, but its accuracy for specific years is uncertain. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean, Near East, and parts of Asia; events in sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The completion of Alexander's conquest of Bactria and Sogdiana, marked by his marriage to Roxana, allowed him to prepare for his invasion of India
Alexander's adoption of Persian customs and increasing paranoia contributed to tensions that would lead to the mutiny at the Hyphasis River in 325 BCE
The Mauryan Empire's expansion eventually led to conflict with Seleucus I, Alexander's successor in the Near East

era_context: Alexander III is invading India, seeking to extend his conquests to the eastern edge of the known world. The Hellenistic world is being reshaped by Alexander's conquests and the establishment of new cities and administrative structures. In India, the Mauryan Empire is consolidating its power and will soon encounter Alexander's forces. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin rising as a dominant power. In Greece, the city-states remain under Macedonian hegemony.

events.title: Battle of the Hydaspes River (Jhelum River)
events.description: In May 326 BCE, Alexander defeated King Porus of the Pauravas kingdom at the Battle of the Hydaspes River (modern Jhelum River) in Punjab. Porus commanded a large army including war elephants, which were unfamiliar to the Macedonian forces. Despite being outnumbered, Alexander's superior tactics and cavalry maneuvers allowed him to defeat Porus. The victory was one of Alexander's most celebrated battles and demonstrated the effectiveness of Macedonian military tactics against Indian forces. However, the battle also revealed the limits of Alexander's conquests; the victory was costly, and the Indian kingdoms were far more formidable than the Persian Empire had been. After the battle, Alexander treated Porus with respect and allowed him to continue ruling his kingdom as a vassal.
key_figures: King Porus of the Pauravas (Indian king)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Hydaspes River is well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in May 326 BCE by modern scholarship, based on ancient sources and astronomical calculations.
cross_references: Follows from 327-001 (Alexander's consolidation of Bactrian rule)
Led to 325-001 (Mutiny at the Hyphasis River)

events.title: Alexander's Advance into the Punjab and Founding of Cities
events.description: Following his victory at the Hydaspes River, Alexander advanced further into the Punjab, establishing new cities and consolidating his control over the region. He founded several cities, including Bucephala (named after his horse) and Nicaea (named after his victory). Alexander's advance into India was marked by the establishment of Greek settlements and the integration of Indian territories into his empire. However, the advance also revealed the challenges of maintaining control over such vast distances and diverse populations. The Indian kingdoms were more numerous and better organized than Alexander had anticipated, and the local populations were less receptive to Macedonian rule than the peoples of the Near East had been.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's advance into the Punjab and the founding of cities are well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in 326 BCE by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Follows from 326-001 (Battle of the Hydaspes River)
Led to 325-001 (Mutiny at the Hyphasis River)

events.title: Mauryan Empire Consolidation and Preparation for Conflict with Alexander
events.description: In 326 BCE, the Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta was consolidating its power and preparing for potential conflict with Alexander's advancing forces. The Mauryan Empire had become the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent, controlling much of northern and central India. Chandragupta was aware of Alexander's invasion and was preparing his military forces to resist the Macedonian advance. However, the two empires would not directly clash; Alexander's army would mutiny at the Hyphasis River in 325 BCE, preventing him from advancing further into India. The Mauryan Empire would eventually expand to fill the vacuum left by Alexander's withdrawal.
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of the Mauryan Empire is attested in later Sanskrit sources. The precise dating of events in 326 BCE is uncertain; Indian chronology for this period relies on later compilations and synchronisms with Greek accounts.

events.title: Qin State Continued Expansion and Military Development
events.description: The state of Qin continued its military and territorial expansion under King Huiwen of Qin. Qin pursued aggressive campaigns against neighboring states, incorporating new territories and consolidating its power base. The state's success was based on military innovation, administrative centralization, and the recruitment of talented strategists and generals. By 326 BCE, Qin was consolidating its position as one of the major powers in the Warring States system.
key_figures: King Huiwen of Qin (Qin ruler, r. 338–311 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are compiled in texts written centuries later. The Shiji is the primary source, but its chronology for this era is subject to scholarly revision.
cross_references: Contemporary with 326-001 (Battle of the Hydaspes River)
Part of broader Warring States competition

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of the Hydaspes River is debated by modern scholars. Some argue that the battle was less decisive than the Greek sources suggest, and that Porus's forces were not as thoroughly defeated as the accounts claim. The extent of Alexander's control over the Punjab following the battle is disputed; some scholars argue that his control was limited and that he was forced to rely on local rulers like Porus to maintain order. The founding of cities by Alexander in India is attested in Greek sources, but the extent of these settlements and their long-term significance is debated. The chronology of the Mauryan Empire's consolidation is debated; some scholars argue that the empire's expansion was more gradual than the traditional accounts suggest. The claim that Chandragupta was aware of Alexander's invasion and preparing to resist is not directly attested in Indian sources and is inferred from Greek accounts.
historiographic_note: The year 326 BCE is well-documented for Alexander's invasion of India through Greek and later Greco-Roman sources, though these are not contemporary. The sources emphasize Alexander's military achievements and tend to downplay the difficulty of the campaigns and the resistance of Indian kingdoms. For India, sources are sparse and compiled centuries later; the chronology is debated. The Mauryan sources do not mention Alexander, and the extent of Chandragupta's awareness of Alexander's invasion is uncertain. For China, the Shiji provides a framework, but its accuracy for specific years is uncertain. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean, Near East, and parts of Asia; events in sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Alexander's consolidation of Bactrian rule and marriage to Roxana enabled his invasion of India, culminating in the Battle of the Hydaspes River
The Battle of the Hydaspes River and Alexander's advance into the Punjab led to the mutiny at the Hyphasis River in 325 BCE
Alexander's advance into the Punjab and the founding of cities contributed to the exhaustion of his army, leading to the mutiny at the Hyphasis River
The Mauryan Empire's consolidation and preparation for conflict with Alexander eventually led to direct conflict with Seleucus I in 305 BCE

era_context: The Roman Empire under Constantine I (r. 306–337 CE) is undergoing profound religious and administrative transformation. The Council of Nicaea convenes this year, marking the first ecumenical council and a watershed moment in Christian orthodoxy. Constantine has recently consolidated power after defeating Licinius (324 CE) and is relocating the imperial capital eastward. The empire is administratively divided into Eastern and Western halves, though unified under Constantine's rule.

events.title: First Council of Nicaea
events.description: Constantine I convenes approximately 300 bishops at Nicaea to resolve the Arian controversy—a theological dispute over the nature of Christ and his relationship to God the Father. The council condemns Arianism as heretical and affirms the Nicene Creed, establishing that Christ is of one substance (homoousios) with the Father. This decision becomes foundational to Christian orthodoxy and grants Constantine unprecedented authority over ecclesiastical matters. The council also standardizes Easter calculations and establishes canons governing clerical celibacy and church discipline.
key_figures: Nicholas of Myra (Bishop, later venerated as Saint Nicholas)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine (Vita Constantini)
Athanasius of Alexandria, Against the Arians

events.certainty_note: The council's occurrence and basic proceedings are attested in multiple near-contemporary and later sources. The exact number of bishops (often cited as 318, sometimes 300) varies in sources, but the event itself is historically certain.
cross_references: Led to 381-001 (First Council of Constantinople, which reaffirmed Nicene orthodoxy)

events.title: Constantine's Visit to Rome
events.description: Constantine I visits Rome for the first time as sole emperor, celebrating the 20th anniversary (vicennalia) of his reign. The visit is ceremonial and symbolic, reinforcing his legitimacy as emperor. However, Constantine does not reside in Rome; his attention is focused on the East and the construction of a new capital. The visit marks a subtle shift in the empire's center of gravity away from the traditional capital.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's visit to Rome is attested in sources, though the exact date and duration are debated. Some sources place it in 326 CE. The vicennalia celebration is confirmed, but details of the visit's political significance are partly reconstructed.

events.title: Execution of Crispus, Constantine's Son
events.description: Constantine I orders the execution of his eldest son Crispus, Caesar of the Western Roman Empire, on charges of treason and adultery with his stepmother Fausta. The exact circumstances remain obscure and contested by ancient sources. Some accounts suggest Fausta falsely accused Crispus; others claim genuine conspiracy. The execution shocks the empire and raises questions about Constantine's judgment and the stability of the succession. Shortly after, Constantine also executes Fausta, possibly by scalding her in a bath.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The execution of Crispus is historically certain, but the dating (325 or 326 CE) and the precise charges are disputed. Ancient sources offer conflicting narratives, suggesting the event was politically sensitive and later reinterpreted.
cross_references: Part of broader succession crisis in Constantine's dynasty

events.title: Continued Construction of Constantinople
events.region: Byzantium, Eastern Roman Empire (modern-day Istanbul, Turkey)
events.description: Constantine I continues the massive construction project to transform Byzantium into a new imperial capital, which he will formally dedicate in 330 CE. The city is being rebuilt with new walls, forums, churches, and administrative buildings. This represents an unprecedented investment in urban infrastructure and signals Constantine's intention to shift the empire's political center eastward, away from Rome. The project employs tens of thousands of workers and artisans.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The construction of Constantinople is well-attested in multiple sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence. The timeline of construction phases is debated, but work was ongoing throughout the 320s CE.
cross_references: Part of broader administrative reorganization under Constantine

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Constantine as a pious Christian emperor devoted to religious unity is complicated by several factors: (1) The Council of Nicaea, while presented as Constantine's effort to resolve theological dispute, was also a tool of imperial control over the church—Constantine presided over the council and enforced its decisions through imperial authority, blurring the line between secular and religious power. (2) Constantine's execution of Crispus and Fausta, occurring around the time of the council, suggests that Constantine's piety did not prevent brutal dynastic politics. (3) Eusebius of Caesarea's Life of Constantine, a primary source for this period, is hagiographic and omits or downplays Constantine's violence and political ruthlessness. (4) The Arian controversy did not end with Nicaea; Arianism remained influential in the Eastern Empire and among Germanic peoples for centuries, suggesting the council's 'victory' was less complete than traditional accounts imply.
historiographic_note: The year 325 CE is exceptionally well-documented for the Roman Empire, primarily through ecclesiastical sources (Eusebius, Socrates, later church historians) and imperial records. However, these sources have significant biases: ecclesiastical sources emphasize religious significance and Constantine's role as a Christian emperor, while downplaying political violence. Imperial records are fragmentary. Non-Christian perspectives on Constantine's reign are largely lost. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Eastern Roman Empire; events in the Western Empire, Britain, and beyond the imperial frontiers are poorly attested. The execution of Crispus is a case study in how ancient sources can be unreliable: multiple conflicting accounts survive, and modern historians debate whether Constantine acted justly or was manipulated by Fausta.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Nicaea established the Nicene Creed, which was reaffirmed and refined at the First Council of Constantinople in 381 CE
Nicene orthodoxy became the foundation for subsequent ecumenical councils, including the Council of Chalcedon in 451 CE
The execution of Crispus destabilized the succession and led to the execution of Fausta
Continued construction of Constantinople culminated in its formal dedication in 330 CE
Constantine's sole rule after defeating Licinius enabled him to convene the ecumenical council

era_context: Constantine I consolidates sole power over the Roman Empire after a decade of civil war. The empire has been divided among multiple rulers since the abdication of Diocletian (305 CE); Constantine's victory over Licinius in 324 CE marks the end of this period of fragmentation. Constantine is now the undisputed emperor and begins implementing sweeping religious, administrative, and military reforms. The empire is transitioning from pagan to Christian, though this process is incomplete and contested.

events.title: Battle of Chrysopolis and Constantine's Victory over Licinius
events.region: Chrysopolis (modern-day Üsküdar, Istanbul), Bithynia, Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: Constantine I defeats Licinius, the Eastern Roman Emperor, at the Battle of Chrysopolis on the Bosporus. This decisive victory ends the civil war that has divided the empire since 312 CE and makes Constantine the sole ruler of the Roman Empire. Licinius is captured and later executed (possibly in 325 CE). Constantine's victory is facilitated by superior naval tactics and the defection of some of Licinius's commanders. The battle marks a turning point in Constantine's reign, allowing him to implement his vision for a unified, Christian empire.
key_figures: Constantine I (Western Roman Emperor, now sole emperor)
Crispus (Constantine's son, commander of the fleet)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors (De Mortibus Persecutorum)

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple sources and is historically certain. The exact date (September 18, 324 CE) is given in some sources, though minor variations exist. The strategic significance is undisputed.
cross_references: Precedes 325-001 (Council of Nicaea, enabled by Constantine's sole rule)
Follows from 312-001 (Battle of the Milvian Bridge, Constantine's earlier victory)

events.title: Execution of Licinius
events.description: Constantine I orders the execution of Licinius, his defeated rival, possibly in late 324 CE or early 325 CE. The exact date and location are disputed by sources.
