Licinius is executed on charges of conspiracy and treason. His death eliminates the last potential rival to Constantine's rule and consolidates Constantine's authority. The execution also removes a pagan emperor who had persecuted Christians in the Eastern Empire, allowing Constantine to present himself as the protector of Christianity.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Licinius's execution is historically certain, but the exact date (324 or 325 CE) and location are debated. Some sources suggest he was executed immediately after Chrysopolis; others place it months later.

events.title: Constantine Selects Byzantium as Site for New Capital
events.region: Byzantium, Eastern Roman Empire (modern-day Istanbul, Turkey)
events.description: Constantine I selects the ancient city of Byzantium as the site for a new imperial capital, which he will name Constantinople (City of Constantine). The choice reflects Constantine's strategic vision: Byzantium is located at the intersection of Europe and Asia, controlling the Bosporus and trade routes between the Mediterranean and Black Sea. The city is also positioned to defend against threats from the Danube and Persia. Constantine begins massive construction projects to transform Byzantium into a grand imperial city, rivaling Rome in splendor. This decision marks a fundamental shift in the empire's center of gravity eastward.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's selection of Byzantium and the beginning of construction are well-attested. The exact date of the decision is sometimes placed in 324 or 325 CE, but 324 CE (following his victory over Licinius) is most likely.
cross_references: Part of broader administrative reorganization under Constantine

events.title: Constantine's Edict Against Heresy
events.description: Constantine I issues an edict condemning heresy and ordering the destruction of heretical writings, particularly those of Arius and his followers. This represents Constantine's first major intervention in Christian theology and foreshadows the Council of Nicaea (325 CE). The edict grants Constantine authority over religious matters and establishes a precedent for imperial enforcement of orthodoxy. However, the edict's effectiveness is limited, and Arianism continues to spread despite imperial opposition.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's edict against heresy is attested in sources, but the exact date and wording are uncertain. Some sources place it before the Council of Nicaea; others suggest it followed the council's decisions.
cross_references: Part of Constantine's broader Christianization of the empire

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Constantine as a liberator of Christians and a pious emperor is complicated by several factors: (1) Constantine's execution of Licinius, while presented as necessary for imperial unity, was also a ruthless elimination of a rival. Licinius had persecuted Christians, but Constantine's motives were likely mixed—religious conviction and political pragmatism. (2) Constantine's edict against heresy, issued before the Council of Nicaea, suggests that Constantine was not merely responding to theological disputes but actively shaping Christian orthodoxy for political purposes. (3) Constantine's conversion to Christianity is traditionally dated to 312 CE (after the Battle of the Milvian Bridge), but some scholars argue his commitment was gradual and politically motivated. (4) The sources for Constantine's reign are heavily biased toward portraying him as a Christian hero; pagan sources that might offer alternative perspectives are largely lost.
historiographic_note: The year 324 CE is well-documented through ecclesiastical and imperial sources, particularly Eusebius of Caesarea's Life of Constantine, which is hagiographic and emphasizes Constantine's piety and divine favor. Secular sources like Zosimus offer more critical perspectives but are often fragmentary. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Eastern Roman Empire; events in the Western Empire, Britain, and beyond the imperial frontiers are poorly attested. The sources for Constantine's reign reflect the biases of the Christian church, which had a vested interest in portraying Constantine as a Christian emperor. Modern scholarship has increasingly questioned the traditional narrative, emphasizing Constantine's political pragmatism and the gradual nature of his Christianization.

graph_edges.note: Constantine's victory over Licinius and consolidation of sole power enabled him to convene the Council of Nicaea
Constantine's selection of Byzantium as the new capital led to its formal dedication as Constantinople in 330 CE
Constantine's victory at Chrysopolis was the culmination of his rise to power, which began with the Battle of the Milvian Bridge in 312 CE
Constantine's edict against heresy preceded and foreshadowed the Council of Nicaea's condemnation of Arianism

era_context: The Roman Empire is in the final stages of civil war between Constantine I and Licinius. Constantine controls the Western Empire and has been expanding eastward; Licinius controls the Eastern Empire and is attempting to maintain his position. The conflict will culminate in Constantine's victory at the Battle of Chrysopolis in 324 CE. The empire is administratively fragmented, with multiple power centers and competing claims to legitimacy. Christianity is growing in influence, particularly in the Eastern Empire, though paganism remains dominant in many regions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine and Danube

events.title: Constantine's Campaign Against Licinius
events.description: Constantine I launches a military campaign against Licinius, his rival for control of the Roman Empire. The campaign involves multiple battles and skirmishes across the Balkans and Eastern Roman territories. Constantine's forces gradually push eastward, defeating Licinius's armies and capturing key cities. The campaign culminates in the Battle of Chrysopolis in 324 CE. Constantine's military success is facilitated by superior organization, the loyalty of his troops, and the defection of some of Licinius's commanders.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: Constantine's campaign against Licinius is well-attested in multiple sources. The exact sequence and dates of individual battles are sometimes disputed, but the overall campaign is historically certain.
cross_references: Follows from 312-001 (Constantine's earlier consolidation of power in the West)

events.title: Licinius's Persecution of Christians in the Eastern Empire
events.description: Licinius, facing military pressure from Constantine, intensifies persecution of Christians in the Eastern Roman Empire. Licinius views Christianity as a threat to his authority and as a potential source of support for Constantine, who is increasingly identified with Christianity. The persecution includes restrictions on Christian worship, confiscation of church property, and execution of Christian leaders. However, the extent and severity of Licinius's persecution are debated by modern scholars; some sources may exaggerate the persecution to contrast Licinius unfavorably with Constantine.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: Licinius's persecution of Christians is attested in sources, but the extent and severity are debated. Eusebius and Lactantius, both Christian sources, may have exaggerated the persecution to contrast Licinius unfavorably with Constantine. Modern scholars are cautious about accepting these accounts at face value.
cross_references: Precedes 324-001 (Constantine's victory, which ends the persecution)

events.title: Battle of Adrianople (First)
events.description: Constantine I defeats Licinius at the Battle of Adrianople, a major engagement in their civil war. Constantine's forces, commanded by his son Crispus, achieve a decisive victory, capturing the city and pushing Licinius's forces eastward. The battle demonstrates Constantine's military superiority and foreshadows his ultimate victory at Chrysopolis in 324 CE. The exact date of this battle is sometimes placed in 323 or 324 CE; sources vary.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Adrianople is attested in sources, but the exact date (323 or 324 CE) is debated. Some sources conflate it with other battles in the civil war. The strategic significance is clear, but details are uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Licinius as a pagan persecutor of Christians, contrasted with Constantine as a Christian liberator, is complicated by modern scholarship: (1) The extent of Licinius's persecution of Christians is debated; Christian sources (Eusebius, Lactantius) may have exaggerated the persecution to justify Constantine's military campaign and to portray Constantine as a Christian hero. (2) Constantine's own motives for the civil war were likely mixed—religious conviction, political ambition, and personal rivalry all played roles. (3) Licinius was not uniformly hostile to Christianity; he had previously tolerated Christian worship and had Christian advisors. (4) The sources for Licinius's reign are heavily biased toward Constantine's perspective, as Constantine's victory meant that his version of events became the dominant historical narrative.
historiographic_note: The year 323 CE is moderately well-documented through ecclesiastical and imperial sources, particularly Eusebius of Caesarea's Life of Constantine and Ecclesiastical History. However, these sources are heavily biased toward Constantine and Christianity. Secular sources like Zosimus offer alternative perspectives but are often fragmentary and written centuries after the events. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Eastern Roman Empire; events in the Western Empire, Britain, and beyond the imperial frontiers are poorly attested. The sources for the civil war between Constantine and Licinius reflect the biases of the Christian church, which had a vested interest in portraying Constantine as a Christian hero and Licinius as a pagan persecutor. Modern scholarship has increasingly questioned this narrative, emphasizing the political and military dimensions of the conflict.

graph_edges.note: Constantine's campaign against Licinius in 323 CE culminated in the decisive Battle of Chrysopolis in 324 CE
Licinius's persecution of Christians in 323 CE provided justification for Constantine's execution of Licinius in 324 CE
The Battle of Adrianople in 323 CE was part of the campaign that led to the Battle of Chrysopolis in 324 CE

era_context: The Roman Empire remains divided between Constantine I (West) and Licinius (East), with tensions escalating toward open conflict. Constantine is consolidating power in the Western Empire and expanding his influence eastward. Licinius is attempting to maintain control of the Eastern Empire but faces military pressure from Constantine. The empire is administratively fragmented, with competing power centers and ideologies. Christianity is growing in influence, particularly in urban centers, though paganism remains dominant in rural areas and among the military.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine and Danube

events.title: Tensions Between Constantine and Licinius Escalate
events.description: Tensions between Constantine I and Licinius escalate over control of the Balkans and the Danube frontier. The two emperors dispute territorial boundaries and influence over client states. Constantine views Licinius as an obstacle to his vision of a unified, Christian empire. Licinius, facing military pressure, begins to consolidate his position in the Eastern Empire. The tensions will lead to open warfare in 323 CE and Constantine's ultimate victory in 324 CE. The exact nature of the disputes is not fully documented, but sources suggest conflicts over frontier control and religious policy.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Tensions between Constantine and Licinius are attested in sources, but the specific events and dates of 322 CE are not clearly documented. The escalation is inferred from the broader context of their civil war.
cross_references: Follows from 312-001 (Constantine's consolidation of power in the West)

events.title: Constantine's Military Reorganization
events.description: Constantine I continues to reorganize and strengthen the military forces of the Western Roman Empire in preparation for conflict with Licinius. This includes recruitment, training, and the positioning of troops along the Danube frontier. Constantine's military reforms are part of a broader strategy to consolidate power and prepare for the eventual confrontation with Licinius. The exact details of these reforms are not fully documented, but sources suggest Constantine was building a formidable military machine.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's military reorganization is inferred from the broader context of his reign and his military success in the civil war. Specific events and dates for 322 CE are not clearly documented.
cross_references: Part of Constantine's broader consolidation of power

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for 322 CE reflects a broader historiographic challenge: the sources for Constantine's reign are heavily concentrated on major events (battles, councils, executions) and provide limited detail on the political and military maneuvering that preceded these events. The escalation of tensions between Constantine and Licinius is inferred from the broader context rather than documented in contemporary sources.
historiographic_note: The year 322 CE is poorly documented compared to 323–325 CE. The sources for this period are primarily ecclesiastical (Eusebius of Caesarea) and later secular (Zosimus), both of which focus on major events rather than the gradual escalation of tensions. The historical record for 322 CE is largely reconstructed from the broader context of Constantine's reign and the civil war with Licinius. Events in the Western Roman Empire, Britain, and beyond the imperial frontiers are particularly poorly attested. The sources reflect the biases of the Christian church and Constantine's court, which had vested interests in portraying Constantine as a Christian hero and Licinius as a pagan rival.

graph_edges.note: Escalating tensions between Constantine and Licinius in 322 CE led to open warfare in 323 CE
Constantine's military reorganization in 322 CE prepared him for the campaign against Licinius in 323 CE

era_context: The Roman Empire is divided between Constantine I (West) and Licinius (East), with an uneasy coexistence that is beginning to show signs of strain. Constantine is consolidating power in the Western Empire and has recently defeated other rivals (Maxentius in 312 CE, Maximinus Daia in 313 CE). Licinius controls the Eastern Empire and is attempting to maintain his position. The empire is administratively fragmented, with competing power centers. Christianity is growing in influence, particularly in urban centers and among the military, though paganism remains dominant in many regions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine and Danube

events.title: Constantine's Edict on Sunday Rest
events.description: Constantine I issues an edict declaring Sunday (the Lord's Day) as a day of rest for the empire, exempting farmers and rural workers. This is one of the earliest imperial decrees recognizing the Christian Sabbath and represents a significant step in the Christianization of the empire. The edict does not explicitly forbid work on Sunday but encourages rest and worship. This decree is a landmark in the integration of Christian practice into imperial law, though it is not universally enforced and pagan religious observances continue.

sources.name: Codex Justinianus (Justinian's Code, compilation of imperial decrees)
Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's edict on Sunday rest is attested in later compilations of imperial law (Codex Justinianus) and in Eusebius of Caesarea's Life of Constantine. The exact date (321 CE) is given in some sources, but the original edict does not survive. The edict's wording and scope are reconstructed from later references.
cross_references: Part of Constantine's broader Christianization of the empire
Precedes 325-001 (Council of Nicaea, which further institutionalizes Christian practice)

events.title: Constantine's Legislation on Slavery and Manumission
events.description: Constantine I issues legislation modifying laws on slavery and manumission, influenced by Christian teachings on human dignity and freedom. The legislation allows for easier manumission of slaves in churches and grants certain protections to slaves. However, the extent of these reforms is debated; Constantine does not abolish slavery, which remains a fundamental institution of the Roman economy. The legislation represents a gradual shift in imperial policy toward Christian values, though the practical impact is limited.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's legislation on slavery is attested in later compilations of imperial law. The exact date and scope of the legislation are debated; some reforms may have been issued in different years or compiled together in later sources.
cross_references: Part of Constantine's broader Christianization of the empire

events.title: Constantine's Consolidation of Power in the Western Empire
events.description: Constantine I continues to consolidate his power in the Western Roman Empire, strengthening his administrative apparatus and military forces. By 321 CE, Constantine has eliminated all major rivals in the West (Maxentius in 312 CE, Maximinus Daia in 313 CE) and is the undisputed ruler of the Western Empire. His attention is increasingly focused on the Eastern Empire and his rivalry with Licinius. Constantine's consolidation of power is facilitated by his military victories, his adoption of Christianity, and his administrative reforms.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's consolidation of power in the Western Empire is well-attested in sources. By 321 CE, his position in the West is secure, and his attention is turning toward the East.
cross_references: Follows from 312-001 (Battle of the Milvian Bridge)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Constantine as a pious Christian emperor devoted to Christian values is complicated by several factors: (1) Constantine's edict on Sunday rest, while presented as a Christian reform, was also a pragmatic administrative measure that allowed the empire to regulate labor and maintain social order. The edict does not explicitly forbid work on Sunday and exempts farmers, suggesting practical rather than purely religious motivations. (2) Constantine's legislation on slavery, while influenced by Christian teachings, does not abolish slavery and maintains the institution as a fundamental part of the Roman economy. The reforms are limited and do not represent a wholesale transformation of Roman society. (3) Constantine's Christianization of the empire is gradual and incomplete; pagan religious practices continue, and Constantine himself maintains pagan symbols and practices (e.g., the Sol Invictus imagery on coins). (4) The sources for Constantine's reign are heavily biased toward portraying him as a Christian hero; alternative perspectives are largely lost.
historiographic_note: The year 321 CE is moderately well-documented through ecclesiastical sources (Eusebius of Caesarea) and later compilations of imperial law (Codex Justinianus). However, the original imperial decrees do not survive; they are known only through later references and compilations. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Eastern Roman Empire; events in the Western Empire, Britain, and beyond the imperial frontiers are poorly attested. The sources reflect the biases of the Christian church and Constantine's court, which had vested interests in portraying Constantine as a Christian hero and reformer. Modern scholarship has increasingly questioned the traditional narrative, emphasizing Constantine's political pragmatism and the gradual and incomplete nature of his Christianization of the empire.

graph_edges.note: Constantine's edict on Sunday rest in 321 CE was part of a broader Christianization of the empire that culminated in the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE
Constantine's consolidation of power in the Western Empire in 321 CE enabled him to launch a campaign against Licinius in 323 CE
Constantine's edict on Sunday rest and his legislation on slavery were both part of his broader Christianization of the empire

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the immediate aftermath of Alexander the Great's death (323 BCE). His vast empire is fragmenting among competing diadochi (successors). In Greece, the Lamian War has recently concluded (322 BCE), reasserting Macedonian control. In Egypt, Ptolemy I is consolidating power and has secured Alexander's body. In Asia, Seleucus and Antigonus are maneuvering for territorial dominance. The Indian subcontinent is experiencing the rise of the Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta. China is in the late Warring States period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records of Central Asian kingdoms

events.title: Seleucus I gains control of Babylon
events.description: Seleucus I Nicator, one of Alexander's generals, consolidates his position as satrap of Babylon after the partition of Triparadisus (321 BCE). This marks the beginning of Seleucid dynastic control over Mesopotamia and the eastern portions of Alexander's empire. Seleucus's control of Babylon gives him access to vast resources and positions him as a major power broker among the diadochi. This event is foundational to the eventual Seleucid Empire, which will dominate the Near East for nearly two centuries.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, future founder of Seleucid dynasty)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica (Library of History), Book 18-19

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Seleucus's power in Babylon occurred over 321–320 BCE. Sources are not contemporary but derive from Hellenistic historians writing within a few generations. The exact date of 320 is conventional; sources suggest the process was gradual.
cross_references: Leads to 312-BCE Battle of Gaza and Seleucus's further expansion

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya consolidates northern India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, founder of the Mauryan Empire, is in the process of consolidating control over the Gangetic plain and northern India. By 320 BCE, he has defeated the Nanda dynasty and is establishing the administrative and military infrastructure of what will become the largest empire in pre-Islamic India. The Mauryan Empire will eventually extend from the Indus to the Bay of Bengal. Chandragupta's rise represents a major shift in Indian political organization, moving toward centralized imperial rule.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Chandragupta's reign is debated. Traditional Indian chronology places his rule c. 322–298 BCE; some scholars argue for 315–290 BCE. The year 320 is used here as a conventional midpoint of his consolidation phase. No contemporary written sources from India survive; dating relies on later chronicles and Greek accounts.
cross_references: Contemporary with Seleucus I's rise in Mesopotamia
Leads to 305-BCE diplomatic contact between Seleucus and Chandragupta

events.title: Ptolemy I secures Egypt and Alexander's body
events.description: Ptolemy I Soter consolidates his control over Egypt following the Lamian War (322 BCE). He has secured Alexander the Great's body (brought to Egypt c. 321 BCE) and is establishing Alexandria as the capital and intellectual center of his domain. Ptolemy's control of Egypt, with its vast agricultural wealth and strategic position, makes him one of the most powerful diadochi. The establishment of the Ptolemaic dynasty will make Egypt a major Hellenistic power for the next three centuries.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (Macedonian general, founder of Ptolemaic dynasty)
Alexander the Great (deceased, but his body is a symbol of legitimacy)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's control of Egypt by 320 BCE is well-attested in later Greek sources. The transfer of Alexander's body to Egypt is dated to c. 321 BCE by most sources. Papyrological evidence confirms Ptolemaic administrative structures in place by this period.
cross_references: Leads to ongoing conflicts with other diadochi, especially Antigonus

events.title: Antigonus I consolidates Phrygia and central Anatolia
events.description: Antigonus I Monophthalmus (the One-Eyed), another of Alexander's generals, is consolidating control over Phrygia and central Anatolia. By 320 BCE, he is emerging as a major power broker and will soon challenge Seleucus and Ptolemy for dominance. Antigonus's control of Anatolia gives him significant military and economic resources. His ambitions will lead to the Wars of the Diadochi, which will dominate the next two decades.

sources.name: Plutarch, Life of Demetrius (son of Antigonus)

events.certainty_note: Antigonus's consolidation of Anatolia occurred gradually over 321–320 BCE and beyond. Sources are not contemporary but derive from Hellenistic historians. The exact boundaries and extent of his control in 320 are not precisely documented.
cross_references: Leads to 315-BCE conflicts with Seleucus and Ptolemy

events.title: Lysimachus establishes control in Thrace
events.description: Lysimachus, another of Alexander's generals, consolidates his position as satrap of Thrace. By 320 BCE, he is establishing himself as a major regional power. Lysimachus will eventually become one of the most powerful diadochi and will found cities, including Lysimachia. His control of Thrace gives him access to the Hellespont and significant military resources.
events.certainty_note: Lysimachus's control of Thrace by 320 BCE is attested in later sources, but the exact date of consolidation is not precisely documented. Sources suggest his position was secure by this time.
cross_references: Leads to conflicts with Antigonus and Seleucus

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the diadochi presents them as clearly defined power blocs by 320 BCE, but modern scholarship emphasizes the fluidity and uncertainty of this period. Boundaries were not fixed, alliances shifted rapidly, and the 'partition of Triparadisus' (321 BCE) was not a definitive settlement but rather a temporary arrangement. Some scholars argue that Seleucus's position in Babylon was less secure in 320 than later sources suggest, and that his rise to prominence was more gradual and contested than the traditional account implies. Additionally, the significance attributed to individual diadochi in 320 may be retrospectively inflated based on their later prominence; at the time, the outcome of the succession struggle was far from certain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 320 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus and later Hellenistic historians. These sources are not contemporary and were written generations after the events. There is a strong bias toward the Macedonian/Greek perspective; the viewpoints of conquered peoples and non-Greek civilizations are largely absent. For India, sources are even more problematic: no contemporary Indian written sources survive, and dating relies on later Buddhist and Jain texts, which are themselves uncertain. Chinese sources for this period are more reliable but focus on internal Chinese affairs. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Archaeological evidence is sparse for this specific year but becomes more abundant for the Hellenistic period as a whole.

graph_edges.note: Seleucus's control of Babylon sets the stage for his conflict with Antigonus and Demetrius
Chandragupta's consolidation of India leads to diplomatic contact with Seleucus
Ptolemy's secure position in Egypt allows him to participate in the coalition against Antigonus
Seleucus and Antigonus are both consolidating power in this period, setting up future conflicts

era_context: The Hellenistic world continues to fragment following Alexander's death. The diadochi are consolidating regional power bases and beginning to clash militarily. In Greece, Macedonian control is being reasserted after the Lamian War. In Egypt, Ptolemy I is firmly established. In Asia, Seleucus, Antigonus, and Lysimachus are maneuvering for dominance. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is expanding his empire. China remains in the Warring States period, with the state of Qin rising in power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records of most non-Greek regions

events.title: Death of Antipater; Polyperchon assumes regency in Macedonia
events.description: Antipater, the regent of Macedonia and guardian of Alexander's young son Alexander IV, dies in 319 BCE. Polyperchon, Antipater's chosen successor, assumes the regency. However, Antipater's son Cassander challenges Polyperchon's authority, leading to civil conflict in Macedonia. This succession dispute will destabilize Macedonia and draw in other diadochi, particularly Antigonus. The conflict between Polyperchon and Cassander will dominate Macedonian politics for the next decade.
key_figures: Cassander (son of Antipater, challenger to regency)
Alexander IV (young king of Macedonia, ward of the regent)
events.certainty_note: Antipater's death in 319 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The succession of Polyperchon and the challenge from Cassander are documented in detail by Diodorus Siculus, who drew on near-contemporary sources.
cross_references: Leads to 318-BCE conflict between Polyperchon and Cassander
Related to broader Wars of the Diadochi

events.title: Eumenes of Cardia leads forces in Asia Minor for Polyperchon
events.description: Eumenes of Cardia, a trusted general and administrator, is appointed by Polyperchon to lead forces in Asia Minor against Antigonus. Eumenes is one of the few non-Macedonian generals to gain prominence among the diadochi. His campaign in 319 BCE marks the beginning of a series of conflicts that will define the Wars of the Diadochi. Eumenes's loyalty to the legitimate Macedonian succession (Alexander IV) makes him a key figure in the struggle against Antigonus and Cassander.
events.certainty_note: Eumenes's appointment and campaign in 319 BCE are attested in ancient sources, but the exact chronology and details of his movements are not entirely clear. Sources suggest his campaign began in 319 or early 318 BCE.
cross_references: Related to 318-BCE conflicts in Asia Minor
Leads to 316-BCE capture and execution of Eumenes

events.title: Cassander begins consolidating power in Greece
events.description: Cassander, son of Antipater, begins to consolidate his power in Greece following his father's death. He challenges Polyperchon's regency and gains support from various Greek cities and from Antigonus. Cassander's rise marks the beginning of Macedonian reassertion of control over Greece after the Lamian War. His conflict with Polyperchon will dominate Greek politics for the next decade.
key_figures: Cassander (son of Antipater, challenger to regency)
events.certainty_note: Cassander's consolidation of power in Greece began after Antipater's death in 319 BCE, but the exact timeline and extent of his control in 319 are not precisely documented. Sources suggest his rise was gradual and contested.

events.title: Seleucus I continues consolidation in Babylon
events.description: Seleucus I continues to consolidate his control over Babylon and surrounding territories. By 319 BCE, he is firmly established as the dominant power in Mesopotamia and is beginning to expand his influence eastward. Seleucus's position is strengthened by his distance from the main conflicts among the diadochi in the west, allowing him to build his power base relatively undisturbed.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's continued consolidation in Babylon is attested in later sources, but specific events in 319 BCE are not documented. The year is used here as a conventional marker of his ongoing consolidation.
cross_references: Related to 320-001 (Seleucus gains control of Babylon)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya expands Mauryan Empire
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his expansion of the Mauryan Empire across northern India. By 319 BCE, he has consolidated much of the Gangetic plain and is expanding toward the northwest. The Mauryan Empire is becoming the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent. Chandragupta's military campaigns and administrative reforms are laying the foundation for the empire's later expansion under his grandson Ashoka.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Chandragupta's reign and specific campaigns is uncertain. Traditional chronology places his rule c. 322–298 BCE; some scholars argue for 315–290 BCE. The year 319 is used here as a conventional marker of his ongoing expansion.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative presents Polyperchon as a weak regent easily challenged by Cassander, but some modern scholars argue that Polyperchon was a capable administrator and that the conflict was more evenly matched than later sources suggest. Additionally, the significance of the Macedonian succession dispute may be overstated in Greek sources; from the perspective of other regions (Egypt, Asia, India), these events were of limited importance. The dating of Chandragupta's campaigns is highly uncertain, and the year 319 BCE may not correspond to any significant event in Indian history.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 319 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus. These sources are not contemporary and were written generations after the events. There is a strong bias toward Macedonian and Greek perspectives; the viewpoints of other civilizations are largely absent or filtered through Greek sources. For India, sources are even more problematic: no contemporary Indian written sources survive, and dating relies on later Buddhist and Jain texts. Chinese sources for this period are more reliable but focus on internal Chinese affairs. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Archaeological evidence is sparse for this specific year.

graph_edges.note: Antipater's death triggers succession dispute that dominates the next decade
Eumenes's campaign against Antigonus eventually leads to his capture and death
Cassander's consolidation in Greece leads to open conflict with Polyperchon

era_context: The Wars of the Diadochi are in full swing. The conflict between Polyperchon and Cassander is escalating, with Antigonus and other diadochi taking sides. Eumenes is campaigning in Asia Minor. In Egypt, Ptolemy I is consolidating his position and beginning to expand his influence. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is continuing his expansion. China is in the late Warring States period, with Qin rising in power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records of most non-Greek regions

events.title: Polyperchon and Cassander conflict escalates in Macedonia and Greece
events.description: The conflict between Polyperchon and Cassander escalates into open warfare. Polyperchon attempts to consolidate his position as regent by issuing a decree freeing Greek slaves and promising autonomy to Greek cities, attempting to gain popular support. Cassander, supported by Antigonus and other diadochi, opposes Polyperchon's authority. The conflict destabilizes Macedonia and Greece, with various cities and regions taking sides. This conflict will dominate the next several years and will eventually lead to Cassander's triumph.
key_figures: Demetrius I Poliorcetes (son of Antigonus, military commander)
events.certainty_note: The escalation of conflict between Polyperchon and Cassander in 318 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. Diodorus Siculus provides detailed accounts of the military campaigns and political maneuvering.
cross_references: Leads to 317-BCE further escalation of conflict

events.title: Eumenes campaigns against Antigonus in Asia Minor
events.description: Eumenes of Cardia, commanding forces for Polyperchon, campaigns against Antigonus I in Asia Minor. The conflict between Eumenes and Antigonus is one of the major military struggles of the Wars of the Diadochi. Eumenes is attempting to maintain control of Asia Minor for Polyperchon and the legitimate Macedonian succession, while Antigonus is seeking to expand his power base. The campaign is marked by tactical maneuvering and several engagements, though no decisive battle occurs in 318 BCE.
events.certainty_note: Eumenes's campaign against Antigonus in 318 BCE is attested in ancient sources, but the exact chronology and details of specific engagements are not entirely clear. Sources suggest the campaign was ongoing during this period.
cross_references: Related to 319-002 (Eumenes appointed to lead forces)
Leads to 316-BCE capture and execution of Eumenes

events.title: Ptolemy I expands influence in the eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Ptolemy I, secure in Egypt, begins to expand his influence in the eastern Mediterranean. He seizes Cyprus and other islands, establishing Ptolemaic naval dominance. Ptolemy's expansion is cautious and strategic, avoiding direct confrontation with Antigonus while positioning himself as a major power. His control of Egypt's wealth and resources allows him to build a powerful navy and army.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's expansion in the eastern Mediterranean during this period is attested in later sources, but the exact chronology of specific acquisitions is not always clear. The seizure of Cyprus is dated to c. 318–315 BCE by various sources.

events.title: Seleucus I consolidates eastern territories
events.description: Seleucus I continues to consolidate his control over Mesopotamia and begins to expand eastward toward the Indus Valley. By 318 BCE, Seleucus is firmly established as the dominant power in the eastern portions of Alexander's former empire. His distance from the main conflicts among the diadochi in the west allows him to focus on consolidating his eastern territories.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's consolidation in the east is attested in later sources, but specific events in 318 BCE are not documented. The year is used here as a conventional marker of his ongoing consolidation.
cross_references: Related to 320-001 (Seleucus gains control of Babylon)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya continues expansion in India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his expansion of the Mauryan Empire. By 318 BCE, the Mauryan Empire is becoming the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent. Chandragupta's military campaigns and administrative reforms are laying the foundation for the empire's later expansion.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Chandragupta's reign and specific campaigns is uncertain. The year 318 is used here as a conventional marker of his ongoing expansion.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative presents Polyperchon as clearly losing ground to Cassander by 318 BCE, but some modern scholars argue that the conflict was more evenly matched and that Polyperchon's position was not as weak as later sources suggest. Additionally, the significance of individual military campaigns may be overstated; the Wars of the Diadochi were a prolonged, messy conflict with no clear trajectory until much later. The dating of events in India is highly uncertain, and the year 318 BCE may not correspond to any significant event in Indian history.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 318 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus and Plutarch. These sources are not contemporary and were written generations after the events. There is a strong bias toward Macedonian and Greek perspectives; the viewpoints of other civilizations are largely absent. For India, sources are even more problematic: no contemporary Indian written sources survive. Chinese sources for this period are more reliable but focus on internal Chinese affairs. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Conflict between Polyperchon and Cassander continues to escalate
Eumenes's campaign against Antigonus eventually leads to his capture and death

era_context: The Wars of the Diadochi continue with increasing intensity. The conflict between Polyperchon and Cassander is escalating, with Antigonus and other diadochi taking sides. Eumenes is still campaigning in Asia Minor. In Egypt, Ptolemy I is consolidating his position. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is continuing his expansion. China is in the late Warring States period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records of most non-Greek regions

events.title: Cassander defeats Polyperchon; Polyperchon flees to Epirus
events.description: Cassander, supported by Antigonus and other diadochi, defeats Polyperchon in a series of military engagements. Polyperchon is forced to flee Macedonia and seeks refuge in Epirus. This marks a major turning point in the Wars of the Diadochi, with Cassander emerging as the dominant power in Macedonia and Greece. However, Polyperchon retains some influence and will continue to be a factor in the conflict for several more years. The defeat of Polyperchon strengthens Cassander's position and weakens the faction supporting the legitimate Macedonian succession (Alexander IV).
key_figures: Demetrius I Poliorcetes (military commander for Antigonus)
events.certainty_note: Cassander's defeat of Polyperchon in 317 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. Diodorus Siculus provides detailed accounts of the military campaigns.
cross_references: Leads to 316-BCE further consolidation of Cassander's power

events.title: Eumenes continues campaign against Antigonus
events.description: Eumenes of Cardia continues his campaign against Antigonus I in Asia Minor. Despite Polyperchon's defeat in Macedonia, Eumenes continues to fight for the legitimate Macedonian succession. The campaign is marked by tactical maneuvering and several engagements. Eumenes is attempting to maintain control of Asia Minor, but his position is becoming increasingly precarious as Antigonus gains strength.
key_figures: Demetrius I Poliorcetes (military commander for Antigonus)
events.certainty_note: Eumenes's continued campaign in 317 BCE is attested in ancient sources, but the exact chronology and details of specific engagements are not entirely clear.
cross_references: Leads to 316-BCE capture and execution of Eumenes

events.title: Ptolemy I consolidates control of Cyprus and eastern Mediterranean islands
events.description: Ptolemy I continues to consolidate his control of Cyprus and other eastern Mediterranean islands. By 317 BCE, Ptolemy has established himself as a major naval power and is beginning to challenge Antigonus's dominance. Ptolemy's control of the sea lanes and islands gives him significant strategic advantages.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's consolidation of control over Cyprus and islands is attested in later sources, but the exact chronology is not always clear. The process occurred over several years, c. 318–315 BCE.

events.title: Seleucus I consolidates power in Babylon and surrounding territories
events.description: Seleucus I continues to consolidate his control over Babylon and surrounding territories. By 317 BCE, Seleucus is firmly established as the dominant power in the eastern portions of Alexander's former empire. His distance from the main conflicts among the diadochi in the west allows him to focus on consolidating his eastern territories and preparing for future expansion.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's consolidation in the east is attested in later sources, but specific events in 317 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Related to 320-001 (Seleucus gains control of Babylon)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya expands Mauryan Empire
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his expansion of the Mauryan Empire across the Indian subcontinent. By 317 BCE, the Mauryan Empire is becoming the dominant power in India. Chandragupta's military campaigns and administrative reforms are laying the foundation for the empire's later expansion.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Chandragupta's reign and specific campaigns is uncertain. The year 317 is used here as a conventional marker of his ongoing expansion.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative presents Cassander's victory over Polyperchon as decisive and final, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the conflict continued for several more years and that Polyperchon retained significant influence despite his defeat. Additionally, the significance of individual military campaigns may be overstated; the Wars of the Diadochi were a prolonged, messy conflict with no clear trajectory until much later. The dating of events in India is highly uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 317 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus and Plutarch. These sources are not contemporary and were written generations after the events. There is a strong bias toward Macedonian and Greek perspectives. For India, sources are even more problematic: no contemporary Indian written sources survive. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Cassander's victory over Polyperchon leads to further consolidation of his power
Eumenes's continued campaign against Antigonus eventually leads to his capture and death

era_context: The Wars of the Diadochi are reaching a critical phase. Cassander has defeated Polyperchon and is consolidating his power in Macedonia and Greece. Antigonus is expanding his influence in Asia Minor and the eastern Mediterranean. Eumenes is still campaigning but is increasingly isolated. In Egypt, Ptolemy I is consolidating his position. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is continuing his expansion. China is in the late Warring States period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records of most non-Greek regions

events.title: Eumenes captured and executed by Antigonus
events.description: Eumenes of Cardia, after years of campaigning against Antigonus, is finally captured. Antigonus, viewing Eumenes as a threat to his ambitions, orders his execution. Eumenes's death marks the end of an era of loyalty to the legitimate Macedonian succession and represents a major victory for Antigonus. With Eumenes gone, Antigonus becomes the dominant power in Asia Minor and the eastern Mediterranean. Eumenes's death also removes one of the few non-Macedonian generals to achieve prominence among the diadochi.
key_figures: Demetrius I Poliorcetes (military commander for Antigonus)
events.certainty_note: Eumenes's capture and execution in 316 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. Diodorus Siculus and Plutarch provide detailed accounts of his final campaign and death.
cross_references: Leads to 315-BCE Battle of Gaza and further consolidation of Antigonus

events.title: Antigonus I consolidates power in Asia Minor and eastern Mediterranean
events.description: With Eumenes defeated and executed, Antigonus I consolidates his power over Asia Minor and begins to expand his influence in the eastern Mediterranean. By 316 BCE, Antigonus is emerging as the dominant power in the eastern portions of Alexander's former empire. His ambitions to reunify the empire under his control are becoming increasingly apparent. Antigonus's power is based on his military strength and his control of wealthy territories.
key_figures: Demetrius I Poliorcetes (son and military commander)
events.certainty_note: Antigonus's consolidation of power in 316 BCE is well-attested in ancient sources. His emergence as a major power broker is documented in detail by Diodorus Siculus.
cross_references: Leads to 315-BCE Battle of Gaza and coalition against Antigonus

events.title: Cassander consolidates control of Macedonia and Greece
events.description: Cassander, having defeated Polyperchon, consolidates his control over Macedonia and Greece. By 316 BCE, Cassander is the dominant power in the western portions of Alexander's former empire. He maintains control over the young king Alexander IV and his mother Roxana, using them as symbols of legitimacy. Cassander's control of Macedonia and Greece gives him significant military and economic resources.
key_figures: Alexander IV (young king, ward of Cassander)
events.certainty_note: Cassander's consolidation of power in Macedonia and Greece by 316 BCE is well-attested in ancient sources.

events.title: Ptolemy I continues expansion in eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Ptolemy I continues to expand his influence in the eastern Mediterranean, consolidating his control of Cyprus and other islands. By 316 BCE, Ptolemy has established himself as a major naval power and is beginning to challenge Antigonus's dominance. Ptolemy's expansion is cautious and strategic, avoiding direct confrontation with Antigonus while positioning himself as a major power.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's continued expansion in 316 BCE is attested in later sources, but the exact chronology of specific acquisitions is not always clear.
cross_references: Related to 317-003 (Ptolemy consolidates control of islands)

events.title: Seleucus I consolidates power in Babylon
events.description: Seleucus I continues to consolidate his control over Babylon and surrounding territories. By 316 BCE, Seleucus is firmly established as the dominant power in the eastern portions of Alexander's former empire. His distance from the main conflicts among the diadochi in the west allows him to focus on consolidating his eastern territories and preparing for future expansion.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's consolidation in the east is attested in later sources, but specific events in 316 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Related to 320-001 (Seleucus gains control of Babylon)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya expands Mauryan Empire
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his expansion of the Mauryan Empire across the Indian subcontinent. By 316 BCE, the Mauryan Empire is becoming the dominant power in India. Chandragupta's military campaigns and administrative reforms are laying the foundation for the empire's later expansion under his grandson Ashoka.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Chandragupta's reign and specific campaigns is uncertain. The year 316 is used here as a conventional marker of his ongoing expansion.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative presents Eumenes's death as a decisive turning point, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Wars of the Diadochi continued for several more years with no clear victor until the Battle of Ipsus in 301 BCE. Additionally, Antigonus's position in 316 BCE, while strong, was not as dominant as later sources suggest; he still faced significant challenges from Ptolemy, Cassander, and Seleucus. The dating of events in India is highly uncertain, and the year 316 BCE may not correspond to any significant event in Indian history.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 316 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus and Plutarch. These sources are not contemporary and were written generations after the events. There is a strong bias toward Macedonian and Greek perspectives. For India, sources are even more problematic: no contemporary Indian written sources survive. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Archaeological evidence is sparse for this specific year.

graph_edges.note: Eumenes's death removes a major obstacle to Antigonus's ambitions, but also triggers a coalition against him
Antigonus's consolidation of power in Asia Minor triggers a coalition of other diadochi against him
Cassander's consolidation of power in the west allows him to participate in the coalition against Antigonus

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the immediate aftermath of Alexander the Great's death (323 BCE). His empire is fragmenting among the Diadochi (successor generals). Antigonus I Monophthalmus controls Anatolia and Syria; Seleucus I is consolidating power in Babylon; Ptolemy I rules Egypt; Lysimachus holds Thrace; Cassander dominates Macedonia. This is a period of intense military competition, dynastic instability, and the rapid spread of Greek culture across the Near East and Central Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (China, India) — limited cross-cultural documentation

events.title: Battle of Gaza (or Raphia campaign)
events.description: Antigonus I Monophthalmus, seeking to consolidate control over the eastern Mediterranean, campaigns against Ptolemy I's forces in the Levant. The exact chronology of this campaign is debated, but 315 BCE marks a key phase in Antigonus's attempt to wrest control of Syria and Palestine from Ptolemaic Egypt. This conflict is part of the broader Wars of the Diadochi and demonstrates the fragility of Alexander's territorial inheritance.
key_figures: Antigonus I Monophthalmus (Macedonian general, regent of Asia)
Ptolemy I Soter (Macedonian general, ruler of Egypt)
events.certainty_note: The Wars of the Diadochi are well-attested in later Greek historiography, but precise dating of individual campaigns within 315–312 BCE is uncertain. Ancient sources (Diodorus, Plutarch) were written centuries later and sometimes conflate or reorder events.

events.title: Seleucus I consolidates Babylon
events.description: Seleucus I Nicator, having secured his position as satrap of Babylon after Alexander's death, continues to consolidate control over Mesopotamia and the eastern satrapies. By 315 BCE, he is establishing the administrative and military foundations of what will become the Seleucid Empire. He begins recruiting Greek settlers and reorganizing the Babylonian bureaucracy along Hellenistic lines.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, satrap of Babylon)
events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Seleucus's rise is well-documented, but year-by-year details are sparse. 315 BCE is assigned as a representative date within his consolidation phase (c. 320–312 BCE).
cross_references: 312-002 (Seleucus's victory at the Battle of the Tigers)

events.title: Cassander's consolidation in Macedonia
events.description: Cassander, son of Antipater, is consolidating his control over Macedonia and Greece following his father's death (319 BCE). He faces opposition from Polyperchon (Antipater's chosen successor) and the aging Macedonian regent Olympias (Alexander's mother). By 315 BCE, Cassander is the dominant power in the Macedonian homeland, though his position remains contested.
events.certainty_note: The conflict between Cassander and Polyperchon is well-attested, but precise chronology within 319–315 BCE is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact sequence and dating of campaigns during the Wars of the Diadochi (320–301 BCE) remain contested among modern scholars. Some historians argue that the 'Battle of Gaza' traditionally dated to 312 BCE may have occurred earlier, possibly in 315 or 314 BCE. Additionally, the significance of individual campaigns is sometimes overstated in ancient sources, which were written to glorify particular generals. The extent to which these wars disrupted civilian populations versus remaining elite military contests is debated.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 315 BCE derives almost entirely from later Greek historiographers (Diodorus Siculus, Plutarch, Appian) writing 300–400 years after the events. No contemporary primary texts survive. The sources are often derivative, conflate events, and reflect the biases of their authors (e.g., pro-Ptolemaic or pro-Seleucid sympathies). Archaeological evidence from this period is limited. The narrative is heavily weighted toward military and political events involving the Macedonian elite; the experiences of non-Greek populations, women, and common soldiers are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Both events are part of the broader Wars of the Diadochi; the Gaza campaign may span multiple years.
Seleucus's consolidation in Babylon leads to his major military victory in 312 BCE.
Cassander's consolidation in Macedonia leads to his conflict with Antigonus.

era_context: The Wars of the Diadochi continue to reshape the Hellenistic world. Antigonus I is at the height of his power, controlling much of Anatolia, Syria, and the eastern Mediterranean. Ptolemy I consolidates Egypt and the Levantine coast. Seleucus I is establishing himself in Babylon. Cassander dominates Macedonia and Greece. This year marks a critical phase in the fragmentation of Alexander's empire into the major Hellenistic kingdoms.

events.title: Antigonus I's campaign in the Levant
events.description: Antigonus I Monophthalmus continues his aggressive expansion, campaigning in Syria and the Levant against Ptolemaic forces. He seeks to consolidate control over the wealthy eastern Mediterranean coast and the trade routes connecting the Aegean to Mesopotamia. These campaigns are part of his broader strategy to dominate the eastern half of Alexander's former empire.
events.certainty_note: Antigonus's campaigns in this period are well-attested in Diodorus, but precise year-by-year chronology is uncertain.

events.title: Cassander's conflict with Antigonus
events.description: Tensions escalate between Cassander (dominant in Macedonia and Greece) and Antigonus I (dominant in Asia). Both seek hegemony over the fragmented Hellenistic world. Diplomatic and military posturing characterizes their relationship. This conflict will eventually lead to the formation of coalitions against Antigonus.
events.certainty_note: The rivalry between Cassander and Antigonus is well-documented, but specific events in 314 BCE are not clearly delineated in surviving sources.

events.title: Lysimachus consolidates Thrace
events.description: Lysimachus, satrap of Thrace, continues to consolidate his control over the region and the adjacent territories. He is building a power base that will eventually rival Antigonus and Seleucus. By 314 BCE, he is establishing himself as a major player in the Diadochi conflicts.
events.certainty_note: Lysimachus's consolidation is part of the broader Diadochi narrative, but specific events in 314 BCE are not well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise chronology of Antigonus's campaigns in 314 BCE versus 315 or 313 BCE is debated. Some modern scholars argue that Diodorus's account conflates or reorders events. The significance of individual military campaigns is sometimes exaggerated in ancient sources. Additionally, the extent to which these elite military conflicts affected the broader populations of the eastern Mediterranean is unclear.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 314 BCE is derived from the same later Greek historiographers as 315 BCE (Diodorus, Plutarch, Appian). No contemporary primary texts survive. The sources are often vague about specific dates and events within individual years. The narrative is heavily weighted toward military and political events involving the Macedonian elite. The experiences of non-Greek populations, women, and common soldiers are largely absent. Archaeological evidence is limited.

graph_edges.note: Antigonus's campaigns continue as part of the broader Wars of the Diadochi.
Tensions between Cassander and Antigonus eventually lead to the Peace of the Diadochi in 311 BCE.

era_context: The Wars of the Diadochi are entering a critical phase. Antigonus I remains the most powerful of the successor generals, but opposition to his hegemony is coalescing. Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, Cassander, and Lysimachus are increasingly coordinating against him. The Hellenistic world is stabilizing into regional power blocs, though major conflicts remain inevitable.

events.title: Antigonus I's dominance in Asia Minor and Syria
events.description: By 313 BCE, Antigonus I Monophthalmus has consolidated control over much of Asia Minor, Syria, and the eastern Mediterranean coast. He is the most powerful of the Diadochi and is actively working to expand his influence. However, his aggressive expansion is beginning to provoke a coalition of rivals.
events.certainty_note: Antigonus's dominance in this period is well-attested, but specific events in 313 BCE are not clearly delineated.

events.title: Coalition forming against Antigonus
events.description: Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, Cassander, and Lysimachus are increasingly coordinating their efforts to check Antigonus's power. Diplomatic negotiations and military preparations characterize this period. The coalition is motivated by the fear that Antigonus will dominate the entire Hellenistic world.
events.certainty_note: The formation of the coalition against Antigonus is well-documented in broad outline, but specific diplomatic events in 313 BCE are not clearly dated.

events.title: Ptolemy I's consolidation of Egypt and the Levantine coast
events.description: Ptolemy I Soter continues to consolidate his control over Egypt and the Levantine coast (including Cyprus and parts of Syria). He is establishing the Ptolemaic dynasty as a major Hellenistic power. His control of Egypt's wealth and the Mediterranean trade routes makes him a formidable rival to Antigonus.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 313 BCE are not clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact timeline of the coalition forming against Antigonus is debated. Some scholars argue that the coalition was not fully formed until after 312 BCE, while others place its origins earlier. The significance of individual diplomatic events is often unclear in ancient sources. Additionally, the extent to which these elite political conflicts affected the broader populations of the Hellenistic world is uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 313 BCE is derived from later Greek historiographers (Diodorus, Plutarch, Appian) writing centuries after the events. No contemporary primary texts survive. The sources are often vague about specific dates and events within individual years. The narrative is heavily weighted toward military and political events involving the Macedonian elite. The experiences of non-Greek populations, women, and common soldiers are largely absent. Archaeological evidence is limited.

graph_edges.note: Antigonus's dominance provokes the coalition that leads to the Peace of the Diadochi.
The coalition forming against Antigonus leads to the Peace of the Diadochi.

era_context: A turning point in the Wars of the Diadochi. Seleucus I defeats Antigonus's forces at the Battle of the Tigers (or Battle of the Hydaspes), marking a major shift in the balance of power. Antigonus's dominance is challenged, and the coalition against him gains momentum. The Hellenistic world is moving toward a new equilibrium among the major powers.

events.title: Battle of Gaza (or Battle of Raphia campaign)
events.description: A major battle between Ptolemy I's forces and those of Antigonus I (commanded by his son Demetrius Poliorcetes). Ptolemy defeats Demetrius, securing Ptolemaic control over the Levantine coast and southern Syria. This victory is a significant blow to Antigonus's hegemony and marks a turning point in the Wars of the Diadochi. The exact date of this battle is debated (some sources place it in 315 or 314 BCE), but 312 BCE is the most commonly cited date in modern scholarship.
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in ancient sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Diodorus places it in 312 BCE, but some modern scholars argue for 315 or 314 BCE. The dating is complicated by the fact that ancient sources sometimes conflate or reorder events.

events.title: Seleucus I's victory at the Battle of the Tigers
events.description: Seleucus I Nicator defeats Antigonus's general Nicanor at the Battle of the Tigers (also called the Battle of the Hydaspes or the Battle of the Tigris), securing his control over Babylon and Mesopotamia. This victory is a major turning point in the Wars of the Diadochi, marking the beginning of Seleucus's rise to power and the beginning of Antigonus's decline. The victory allows Seleucus to consolidate his control over the eastern satrapies and establish the foundation of the Seleucid Empire.
events.certainty_note: This battle is well-attested in multiple ancient sources and is a major turning point in the Diadochi wars. The date of 312 BCE is widely accepted in modern scholarship.

events.title: Seleucus I founds Seleucia-on-the-Tigris
events.description: Following his victory at the Battle of the Tigers, Seleucus I founds or refounds the city of Seleucia-on-the-Tigris as a major administrative and commercial center for his growing empire. The city becomes one of the great Hellenistic cities of the Near East, rivaling Alexandria in importance. This foundation marks the beginning of Seleucus's systematic Hellenization of Mesopotamia.
events.certainty_note: The founding of Seleucia-on-the-Tigris is traditionally dated to 312 BCE, but some scholars argue for a slightly later date (c. 305 BCE). The exact chronology is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Gaza is contested. Some modern scholars argue that it occurred in 315 or 314 BCE rather than 312 BCE. Additionally, the significance of individual battles is sometimes exaggerated in ancient sources. The Battle of the Tigers is sometimes conflated with other battles in Seleucus's campaigns. The founding date of Seleucia-on-the-Tigris is also debated; some sources suggest 305 BCE rather than 312 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 312 BCE is derived from later Greek historiographers (Diodorus, Plutarch, Appian, Strabo) writing centuries after the events. No contemporary primary texts survive. The sources are often vague about specific dates and events. The narrative is heavily weighted toward military and political events involving the Macedonian elite. The experiences of non-Greek populations, women, and common soldiers are largely absent. Archaeological evidence from Seleucia-on-the-Tigris and other sites provides some corroboration, but is limited.

graph_edges.note: Ptolemy's victory at Gaza contributes to the coalition against Antigonus, leading to the Peace of the Diadochi.
Seleucus's victory at the Battle of the Tigers marks a major shift in power, contributing to the Peace of the Diadochi.
The founding of Seleucia-on-the-Tigris is part of Seleucus's broader program of Hellenization and empire-building.

era_context: The Wars of the Diadochi reach a temporary resolution with the Peace of the Diadochi. The major powers—Antigonus I, Seleucus I, Ptolemy I, Cassander, and Lysimachus—agree to a settlement that recognizes their respective spheres of influence. This peace is fragile and will be broken within a few years, but it marks a moment of stabilization in the Hellenistic world. The Hellenistic kingdoms are beginning to take their final form.

events.title: Peace of the Diadochi
events.description: The major Diadochi (Antigonus I, Seleucus I, Ptolemy I, Cassander, and Lysimachus) agree to a peace treaty that recognizes their respective spheres of influence. Antigonus I and his son Demetrius Poliorcetes are recognized as rulers of Asia Minor and Syria; Seleucus I is recognized as ruler of Babylon and the eastern satrapies; Ptolemy I is recognized as ruler of Egypt and the Levantine coast; Cassander is recognized as regent of Macedonia and Greece; Lysimachus is recognized as ruler of Thrace. The peace also includes provisions for the protection of Alexander's son, Philip Arrhidaeus, and his half-brother Heracles. This peace is a major turning point in the Diadochi wars, marking the beginning of the stabilization of the Hellenistic world.
events.certainty_note: The Peace of the Diadochi is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date of 311 BCE is widely accepted in modern scholarship.

events.title: Cassander's consolidation of Macedonia and Greece
events.description: Following the Peace of the Diadochi, Cassander is formally recognized as regent of Macedonia and Greece. He consolidates his control over the Macedonian homeland and the Greek city-states. He is also involved in the protection of Alexander's young son, Philip Arrhidaeus, and his half-brother Heracles, though his commitment to their welfare is questionable.
events.certainty_note: Cassander's consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 311 BCE are not clearly documented.

events.title: Seleucus I's consolidation of the eastern satrapies
events.description: Following the Peace of the Diadochi, Seleucus I is formally recognized as ruler of Babylon and the eastern satrapies. He consolidates his control over Mesopotamia, Persia, and the regions extending toward Central Asia and India. He is establishing the administrative and military foundations of the Seleucid Empire.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 311 BCE are not clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Peace of the Diadochi is sometimes portrayed as a definitive settlement, but it was actually quite fragile and was broken within a few years (by 307 BCE at the latest). The peace did not resolve the fundamental tensions between the major powers, particularly between Antigonus and the coalition against him. Additionally, the treatment of Alexander's heirs (Philip Arrhidaeus and Heracles) under the peace agreement is unclear, and Cassander's role in their eventual deaths is disputed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 311 BCE is derived from later Greek historiographers (Diodorus, Plutarch) writing centuries after the events. No contemporary primary texts survive. The sources are generally clear about the fact of the Peace of the Diadochi, but details about its specific terms and implementation are vague. The narrative is heavily weighted toward military and political events involving the Macedonian elite. The experiences of non-Greek populations, women, and common soldiers are largely absent. Archaeological evidence is limited.

graph_edges.note: The Peace of the Diadochi is broken by Demetrius Poliorcetes' siege of Rhodes.
Cassander's consolidation of power leads to his eventual murder of Philip Arrhidaeus.

era_context: The Roman Empire under the Tetrarchy system established by Diocletian (284–305 CE) is fragmenting. Maxentius rules Italy and North Africa; Constantine I controls Britain, Gaul, and Hispania; Licinius and Maximinus Daia contest the Eastern provinces. The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE) continues under Galerius and Maximinus. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is collapsing into the War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE), with the empire fragmenting into competing warlord states. This is a period of acute political instability across the Mediterranean and East Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road trade dynamics

events.title: Constantine I's Vision at the Bridge of Milvian (traditional dating; actual battle 312 CE)
events.description: Constantine I reports a vision of a cross in the sky, which he interprets as a divine sign to adopt Christian symbols. This event is traditionally dated to 310 CE by some sources, though the famous Battle of the Milvian Bridge where Constantine defeated Maxentius occurred in 312 CE. The vision narrative is preserved in Eusebius of Caesarea's Life of Constantine, written after Constantine's death. Whether the vision occurred in 310 or 312, or was a later hagiographic invention, remains debated. This marks the beginning of Constantine's association with Christianity, which would transform the religion's status within the empire.
key_figures: Eusebius of Caesarea (Christian historian, c. 260–340 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine (Vita Constantini), Book 1, chapters 27–32
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors (De mortibus persecutorum), chapter 44

events.certainty_note: The vision is attested only in post-Constantinian sources written decades later. No contemporary documentation exists. The date itself is uncertain—some sources place it at the Milvian Bridge (312 CE). The event may be hagiographic rather than historical.

events.title: Maxentius Proclaimed Emperor in Rome
events.description: Maxentius, son of the retired emperor Maximian, is proclaimed emperor by the Roman Senate and praetorian guard, establishing control over Italy, North Africa, and Sicily. He rules in opposition to Constantine I (who controls Gaul and Britain) and the Eastern emperors Licinius and Maximinus Daia. Maxentius's reign is marked by attempts to restore traditional pagan religion and Roman civic institutions, positioning himself as the defender of Rome against the Tetrarchic system. His rule lasts until his defeat and death at the Milvian Bridge in 312 CE.
key_figures: Maximian (retired emperor, father of Maxentius, r. 286–305 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43
Roman coins and inscriptions from the reign of Maxentius

events.certainty_note: Maxentius's proclamation and reign are well-attested in numismatic evidence, inscriptions, and multiple historical chronicles. The date 306 CE (or possibly 307 CE) is secure.

events.title: Galerius Issues Edict of Toleration for Christians
events.description: Galerius, the senior Eastern emperor, issues an edict granting toleration to Christians and ending the Great Persecution (303–311 CE). The edict is issued from Nicomedia in Bithynia, likely in late 310 or early 311 CE. Galerius had been the primary architect of the persecution under Diocletian; his reversal is attributed to illness (possibly gout or a wasting disease) and pragmatic recognition that persecution had failed to eliminate Christianity. The edict permits Christians to worship openly but does not restore confiscated property. This marks a turning point in Christian-imperial relations, though persecution continues under Maximinus Daia in the Eastern provinces.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church (Ecclesiastical History), Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: The edict is attested in multiple near-contemporary sources, but the exact date is uncertain—sources place it between late 310 and early 311 CE. The text of the edict itself does not survive; we know it only through paraphrases in Eusebius and Lactantius.

events.title: Western Jin Dynasty Collapse Accelerates; Warlord Fragmentation in China
events.description: The Western Jin Dynasty (266–316 CE), already weakened by the War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE), continues to fragment as regional warlords consolidate power. By 310 CE, the central government's authority is nominal; competing military strongmen control provinces. This period sees the beginning of large-scale migration of non-Han peoples (Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang) into North China, which will lead to the establishment of the Sixteen Kingdoms (304–439 CE). The south remains under nominal Jin control but is increasingly autonomous. This represents one of the most chaotic periods in Chinese history, with widespread warfare, famine, and displacement.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) and Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang (11th century CE)
Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled in the 7th century CE
Archaeological evidence from tomb inscriptions and ceramic remains

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Jin collapse is well-established, but precise dating of individual events within 310 CE is difficult. Chinese historical sources are detailed but were compiled centuries later and may contain legendary elements.

events.title: Shapur II Consolidates Sassanid Control in Persia
events.description: Shapur II (r. 309–379 CE), known as 'Shapur of the Shoulder' (Shapur Dhū'l-Aktāf), begins his long reign as Sassanid Shah. He is crowned while still in the womb according to tradition, and by 310 CE has consolidated power after a period of internal instability. Shapur II will become one of the greatest Sassanid rulers, known for military campaigns against Rome, the conquest of Ctesiphon, and religious reforms. His reign marks the height of Sassanid power and represents a counterbalance to Constantine I's rise in the West.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk), compiled 9th century CE
Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History, Books 14–31 (4th century CE)

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's accession and reign are well-attested in Sassanid numismatic evidence and later Persian chronicles. The date 309 CE is secure.
cross_references: 337-001 (Constantine I dies; Shapur II campaigns against Rome)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Constantine's vision is highly disputed. While traditionally placed in 310 CE, many modern scholars argue it was a later hagiographic invention or occurred at the Milvian Bridge in 312 CE. Eusebius's account, written c. 337–340 CE, is the only source and was composed after Constantine's death with clear propagandistic intent. The vision narrative may reflect Constantine's later Christian piety rather than a historical event. Additionally, the extent of Maxentius's 'paganism' versus Constantine's 'Christianity' is often overstated in older historiography; both rulers were pragmatic about religious policy, and the religious framing of their conflict is partly a later Christian narrative.
historiographic_note: The year 310 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through numismatic evidence, inscriptions, and near-contemporary chronicles (Eusebius, Lactantius), though these sources were written 20–30 years after events and carry Christian apologetic bias. For China, sources are detailed but compiled centuries later (7th–11th centuries CE) and may conflate or misdate events. For Sassanid Persia, evidence is primarily numismatic and from much later Persian chronicles. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from surviving written sources for this year. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian elites.

graph_edges.note: Constantine's vision (or its later invention) is presented as motivating his victory at Milvian Bridge
Maxentius's rule directly precipitated the conflict with Constantine
