Galerius's edict of toleration paved the way for the Edict of Milan
Continued fragmentation of Western Jin led to its final collapse
Shapur II's rise paralleled Constantine I's consolidation of power in the West

era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy is in active dissolution. Constantine I is consolidating power in the West; Maxentius rules Italy and North Africa; Licinius and Maximinus Daia contest the East. The Great Persecution of Christians continues under Galerius and Maximinus. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in terminal decline following the War of the Eight Princes; warlordism and non-Han migration are accelerating. In Persia, Shapur II has just ascended to the Sassanid throne. This is a pivotal year of transition across multiple empires.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road dynamics

events.title: Shapur II Crowned Sassanid Shah
events.description: Shapur II (r. 309–379 CE) is crowned as Sassanid Shah following the death of his father Hormizd II (r. 303–309 CE). According to Sassanid tradition, Shapur II was crowned while still in the womb, making him the only Sassanid ruler crowned before birth. His accession marks the beginning of a 70-year reign that will see the Sassanid Empire reach its zenith. Shapur II will conduct major military campaigns against Rome, consolidate Zoroastrian orthodoxy, and expand Sassanid territorial control. His reign represents the apex of Sassanid power and influence.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled 9th century CE
Sassanid coins and inscriptions bearing Shapur II's name and titles

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's accession in 309 CE is confirmed by numismatic evidence (coins dated to his reign) and attested in multiple historical sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: 337-001 (Constantine I dies; Shapur II campaigns against Rome)

events.title: Galerius Remains Senior Emperor; Persecution of Christians Continues
events.description: Galerius continues as the senior emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire, ruling from Nicomedia in Bithynia. The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE) remains in effect, though with varying intensity across the Eastern provinces. Galerius himself is reportedly suffering from a wasting illness (possibly gout or a chronic disease), which some sources attribute to divine punishment for persecuting Christians. The persecution is particularly severe under Maximinus Daia in the Eastern provinces, where Christian communities face confiscation of property, forced labor, and execution. This period represents the final phase of systematic imperial persecution of Christianity before the Edict of Toleration (310/311 CE).
key_figures: Maximinus Daia (Caesar and later Augustus, r. 305–313 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: The continuation of persecution is attested in multiple sources, but the specific intensity and scope in 309 CE are difficult to determine. Sources are written decades later and may exaggerate or minimize persecution depending on the author's bias.

events.title: Constantine I Consolidates Power in Gaul and Britain
events.description: Constantine I (r. 306–337 CE), proclaimed emperor by his troops in Britain in 306 CE, continues to consolidate control over the Western provinces. By 309 CE, he has secured Gaul, Britain, and Hispania, and is beginning to challenge Maxentius's control of Italy and North Africa. Constantine maintains a pragmatic religious policy, neither persecuting nor favoring Christians systematically, though he is increasingly associated with Christian symbols and advisors. His military strength and administrative competence are building the foundation for his eventual victory over Maxentius and Licinius.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Roman coins and inscriptions from Constantine's reign
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: Constantine's consolidation of Western provinces is well-attested in numismatic evidence and multiple historical sources. The date and extent of his control by 309 CE are secure.

events.title: Western Jin Dynasty Continues Fragmentation; Warlord Proliferation
events.region: China (Central Plains, North China, South China)
events.description: The Western Jin Dynasty (266–316 CE) continues its terminal decline. The War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE) has exhausted the central government's authority, and by 309 CE, regional warlords exercise de facto independence. Large-scale migration of non-Han peoples (Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang) into North China accelerates, driven by climate deterioration and the collapse of Jin administrative control. These migrations will lead to the establishment of the Sixteen Kingdoms (304–439 CE) in North China. The South remains nominally under Jin control but is increasingly autonomous under regional military strongmen. This period sees widespread famine, warfare, and population displacement.

sources.name: Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled 7th century CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century CE
Archaeological evidence from tomb inscriptions and ceramic remains

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Jin collapse is well-established, but precise dating of individual events within 309 CE is difficult. Chinese historical sources are detailed but compiled centuries later and may contain legendary or conflated elements.

events.title: Lactantius Completes 'On the Deaths of the Persecutors' (approximate date)
events.region: Roman Empire (North Africa or Eastern provinces)
events.description: Lactantius (c. 240–c. 320 CE), a Christian apologist and tutor to Constantine I's son Crispus, completes or is in the process of completing his work 'On the Deaths of the Persecutors' (De mortibus persecutorum). This polemical text documents the Great Persecution (303–311 CE) and argues that persecutors of Christians are divinely punished through illness, madness, or violent death. The work is a crucial source for understanding the persecution and Constantine's rise, though it is heavily biased toward Christian apologetics. The exact date of composition is uncertain, but it was likely written between 309 and 313 CE.
key_figures: Lactantius (Christian apologist and author, c. 240–c. 320 CE)

sources.name: Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors (De mortibus persecutorum), surviving manuscript tradition
Jerome, On Illustrious Men (De viris illustribus), chapter 80

events.certainty_note: The composition date of Lactantius's work is uncertain. Jerome places it after the persecution ended (post-311 CE), but some scholars argue for an earlier date (309–310 CE). The work survives in medieval manuscripts but the original composition date cannot be precisely determined.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Shapur II's coronation 'in the womb' is likely legendary or symbolic rather than historical. Later Persian sources may have invented this detail to emphasize his legitimacy and divine favor. The extent of Christian persecution under Galerius in 309 CE is difficult to assess; sources written decades later may exaggerate the severity. Additionally, the characterization of Constantine as a 'Christian emperor' by 309 CE is anachronistic; he was pragmatic about religion and did not formally convert until near his death (337 CE). The religious framing of Constantine's rise is largely a later Christian narrative.
historiographic_note: The year 309 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through numismatic evidence and near-contemporary chronicles, though sources carry Christian apologetic bias. For China, sources are detailed but compiled centuries later and may conflate events. For Sassanid Persia, evidence is primarily numismatic and from much later Persian chronicles. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian elites; sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are absent from surviving written sources.

graph_edges.note: Shapur II's long reign (309–379 CE) shaped Sassanid-Roman relations for decades
Constantine's consolidation of Western provinces enabled his victory at Milvian Bridge
Continued fragmentation of Western Jin led to its final collapse
Lactantius's work documents the persecution that ended with the Edict of Milan

era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy is actively fragmenting. Constantine I controls the Western provinces; Maxentius rules Italy and North Africa; Licinius and Maximinus Daia contest the East. The Great Persecution of Christians continues. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in terminal decline; warlordism and non-Han migration are accelerating. In Persia, Shapur II has recently ascended to the throne. This is a year of acute political instability and transition across multiple empires.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road dynamics

events.title: Conference of Carnuntum: Diocletian Attempts to Restore Tetrarchic Order
events.description: Diocletian, the retired emperor, convenes a conference at Carnuntum on the Danube to attempt to restore order to the fragmenting Tetrarchy. The conference includes Diocletian, Maximian (also retired), Galerius (senior Eastern emperor), and representatives of Constantine I and Maxentius. The goal is to resolve the succession crisis and reestablish the Tetrarchic system. However, the conference fails to achieve its objectives. Constantine I and Maxentius refuse to accept the authority of the Tetrarchs, and the conference instead legitimizes the existing fragmentation. Diocletian's attempt to control events from retirement demonstrates the weakness of the Tetrarchic system and the rise of military strongmen like Constantine.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: The Conference of Carnuntum is attested in multiple historical sources and is dated to 308 CE. The date and general outline of events are secure, though details of the discussions are not preserved.

events.title: Maximian Emerges from Retirement; Conflict with Constantine I
events.description: Maximian, the retired emperor, emerges from retirement and attempts to reassert authority. He initially supports his son Maxentius in Italy but later comes into conflict with Constantine I over control of Gaul and the Alpine passes. Maximian's attempt to reassert power is ultimately unsuccessful; he is forced to flee to Constantine's court and eventually commits suicide in 310 CE. His emergence from retirement demonstrates the instability of the Tetrarchic system and the difficulty of maintaining order when multiple claimants to imperial authority exist.
key_figures: Maximian (retired emperor, r. 286–305 CE, r. 306–310 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: Maximian's emergence from retirement is attested in multiple sources and is dated to 308 CE. The date and general outline of events are secure.

events.title: Galerius Remains Senior Emperor; Persecution of Christians Continues
events.description: Galerius continues as the senior emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire, ruling from Nicomedia. The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE) remains in effect, though with varying intensity. Galerius is reportedly suffering from a wasting illness, which some sources attribute to divine punishment for persecuting Christians. The persecution is particularly severe under Maximinus Daia in the Eastern provinces. This period represents the final phase of systematic imperial persecution of Christianity before the Edict of Toleration (310/311 CE).
key_figures: Maximinus Daia (Caesar and later Augustus, r. 305–313 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: The continuation of persecution is attested in multiple sources, but the specific intensity and scope in 308 CE are difficult to determine. Sources are written decades later and may exaggerate or minimize persecution.

events.title: Western Jin Dynasty Continues Collapse; Warlord Proliferation
events.region: China (Central Plains, North China, South China)
events.description: The Western Jin Dynasty (266–316 CE) continues its terminal decline. By 308 CE, the central government's authority is nominal; regional warlords exercise de facto independence. Large-scale migration of non-Han peoples (Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang) into North China accelerates. These migrations will lead to the establishment of the Sixteen Kingdoms (304–439 CE) in North China. The South remains nominally under Jin control but is increasingly autonomous. This period sees widespread famine, warfare, and population displacement.

sources.name: Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled 7th century CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century CE
Archaeological evidence from tomb inscriptions and ceramic remains

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Jin collapse is well-established, but precise dating of individual events within 308 CE is difficult. Chinese historical sources are detailed but compiled centuries later.

events.title: Shapur II Consolidates Sassanid Control; Early Military Campaigns
events.description: Shapur II (r. 309–379 CE), recently crowned as Sassanid Shah, begins to consolidate power and establish his authority. In the early years of his reign, he conducts military campaigns to secure Sassanid borders and suppress internal dissent. His reign will be marked by major military campaigns against Rome, the consolidation of Zoroastrian orthodoxy, and the expansion of Sassanid territorial control. By 308 CE, Shapur II is establishing himself as a formidable ruler who will challenge Roman power in the East.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled 9th century CE

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's early reign is attested in numismatic evidence and later Persian chronicles, but specific events in 308 CE are difficult to date precisely. The general trajectory of his consolidation of power is secure.
cross_references: 337-001 (Constantine I dies; Shapur II campaigns against Rome)

disconfirming_evidence: The Conference of Carnuntum is often portrayed as a failure of the Tetrarchic system, but some scholars argue that it was a pragmatic attempt to accommodate the reality of military strongmen like Constantine. The characterization of Maximian as a 'tyrant' or 'usurper' is partly a later Christian narrative; he was attempting to reassert legitimate imperial authority within the Tetrarchic framework. The extent of Christian persecution under Galerius in 308 CE is difficult to assess; sources written decades later may exaggerate the severity.
historiographic_note: The year 308 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through numismatic evidence and near-contemporary chronicles, though sources carry Christian apologetic bias. For China, sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. For Sassanid Persia, evidence is primarily numismatic and from much later Persian chronicles. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian elites.

graph_edges.note: The failure of the Conference of Carnuntum led directly to the conflict between Constantine and Maxentius
Maximian's conflict with Constantine led to his eventual suicide in 310 CE
Continued persecution led to Galerius's Edict of Toleration
Continued fragmentation of Western Jin led to its final collapse

era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy is fragmenting rapidly. Constantine I controls the Western provinces; Maxentius rules Italy and North Africa; Licinius and Maximinus Daia contest the East. The Great Persecution of Christians continues. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in terminal decline following the War of the Eight Princes; warlordism and non-Han migration are accelerating. In Persia, Shapur II has recently ascended to the throne. This is a year of acute political instability across multiple empires.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road dynamics

events.title: Emperor Huai of Jin Ascends to Throne; Western Jin Dynasty Continues Decline
events.description: Emperor Huai of Jin (r. 307–313 CE) ascends to the throne following the death of Emperor Hui of Jin (r. 290–306 CE). Huai's reign marks the final phase of the Western Jin Dynasty (266–316 CE). The central government's authority is nominal; regional warlords exercise de facto independence. Large-scale migration of non-Han peoples (Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang) into North China accelerates. The South remains nominally under Jin control but is increasingly autonomous. This period sees widespread famine, warfare, and population displacement. Emperor Huai's reign will end with the Jin court's flight to the South and the establishment of the Eastern Jin Dynasty (317–420 CE).
key_figures: Emperor Hui of Jin (predecessor, r. 290–306 CE)

sources.name: Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled 7th century CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century CE

events.certainty_note: Emperor Huai's accession in 307 CE is attested in Chinese historical sources. The date is secure, though details of his reign are compiled centuries later.
cross_references: 290-001 (Emperor Hui of Jin ascends to throne)

events.title: Licinius Consolidates Control of Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: Licinius (r. 308–324 CE) consolidates control of the Eastern Roman Empire following the Conference of Carnuntum (308 CE). He is recognized as the legitimate Eastern emperor by Galerius and the Tetrarchic system, though his authority is contested by Maximinus Daia in the Eastern provinces. Licinius rules from Nicomedia and later from Sirmium. His reign is marked by conflict with Maximinus Daia and later with Constantine I. By 307 CE, Licinius is establishing himself as a major power in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Roman coins and inscriptions from Licinius's reign
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: Licinius's consolidation of Eastern power is well-attested in numismatic evidence and historical sources. The date and extent of his control by 307 CE are secure.

events.title: Maximinus Daia Contests Eastern Roman Authority; Persecution of Christians Continues
events.description: Maximinus Daia (r. 305–313 CE), Caesar of the Eastern provinces, contests the authority of Licinius and Galerius. Maximinus controls Egypt, Syria, and parts of Asia Minor, and is attempting to expand his power. The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE) is particularly severe under Maximinus, who uses religious persecution as a tool of political control. Maximinus's conflict with Licinius will eventually lead to civil war in the Eastern provinces (312–313 CE). By 307 CE, Maximinus is establishing himself as a major contender for Eastern imperial power.
key_figures: Maximinus Daia (Caesar and later Augustus, r. 305–313 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: Maximinus Daia's rule and persecution of Christians are well-attested in multiple sources. The date and extent of his power by 307 CE are secure.
cross_references: 312-001 (Civil war between Licinius and Maximinus Daia)

events.title: Constantine I Marries Fausta; Alliance with Maximian
events.description: Constantine I marries Fausta, the daughter of the retired emperor Maximian. This marriage is intended to strengthen Constantine's position and create an alliance with Maximian against other claimants to imperial power. However, the marriage also creates complications; Maximian later attempts to reassert power and comes into conflict with Constantine. The marriage produces several children, including Constantine II, Constantius II, and Constans, who will all become emperors. By 307 CE, Constantine is consolidating his power through both military strength and dynastic alliances.
key_figures: Fausta (daughter of Maximian, wife of Constantine)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: Constantine's marriage to Fausta is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Sources place it between 307 and 312 CE. The date 307 CE is conventional but not definitively confirmed.

events.title: Galerius Remains Senior Emperor; Health Deteriorates
events.description: Galerius continues as the senior emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire, ruling from Nicomedia. However, his health is reportedly deteriorating; sources describe a wasting illness (possibly gout or a chronic disease) that will eventually lead to his death in 311 CE. Galerius's illness and declining health contribute to the instability of the Eastern provinces and the rise of Licinius and Maximinus Daia as competing powers. By 307 CE, Galerius's authority is increasingly nominal, and the Eastern provinces are fragmenting into competing spheres of influence.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: Galerius's health problems are attested in multiple sources, but the specific onset and progression of his illness are difficult to date precisely. Sources are written decades later and may exaggerate or minimize his health problems.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Constantine's marriage to Fausta as a purely political alliance is somewhat anachronistic; while dynastic marriages were politically motivated, they also had personal and emotional dimensions that are difficult to assess from surviving sources. The extent of Maximinus Daia's persecution of Christians in 307 CE is difficult to assess; sources written decades later may exaggerate the severity. The portrayal of Galerius as a persecutor of Christians who is divinely punished is partly a Christian apologetic narrative; Galerius's illness may have had natural causes unrelated to his religious policies.
historiographic_note: The year 307 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through numismatic evidence and near-contemporary chronicles, though sources carry Christian apologetic bias. For China, sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. For Sassanid Persia, evidence is primarily numismatic and from much later Persian chronicles. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian elites.

Licinius's consolidation of Eastern power led to his eventual conflict with Constantine
Maximinus Daia's persecution and conflict with Licinius led to the Edict of Milan
Constantine's marriage to Fausta strengthened his position for the Milvian Bridge campaign

era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy is fragmenting. Constantine I is proclaimed emperor in Britain; Maxentius seizes power in Italy and North Africa; Licinius and Maximinus Daia contest the East. The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE) continues under Galerius and Maximinus. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in terminal decline following the War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE); warlordism and non-Han migration are accelerating. In Persia, Shapur II is about to ascend to the throne. This is a pivotal year of transition across multiple empires.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road dynamics

events.title: Constantine I Proclaimed Emperor by Troops in Britain
events.description: Constantine I is proclaimed emperor by the Roman troops in Britain following the death of his father Constantius I Chlorus (r. 305–306 CE). Constantine is serving as a military commander on the British frontier when his father dies. The troops, loyal to Constantius and his son, proclaim Constantine as the new emperor. Constantine initially accepts the title of Caesar under the Tetrarchic system but is recognized as a major power in the Western provinces. This proclamation marks the beginning of Constantine's rise to power and the eventual fragmentation of the Tetrarchy. Constantine will go on to become one of the most significant emperors in Roman history, known for his military victories, administrative reforms, and association with Christianity.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 13–23
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 24–26
Roman coins and inscriptions from Constantine's reign

events.certainty_note: Constantine's proclamation in 306 CE is well-attested in numismatic evidence, inscriptions, and multiple historical sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: 305-001 (Diocletian and Maximian retire; Constantius I becomes Augustus)

events.title: Maxentius Proclaimed Emperor in Rome; Seizes Italy and North Africa
events.description: Maxentius, son of the retired emperor Maximian, is proclaimed emperor by the Roman Senate and praetorian guard in Rome. Maxentius seizes control of Italy, Sicily, and North Africa, establishing a rival imperial court. He positions himself as the defender of Rome and traditional Roman values against the Tetrarchic system. Maxentius's seizure of power represents a direct challenge to the Tetrarchic order and contributes to the fragmentation of the Roman Empire. His rule lasts until his defeat and death at the Battle of the Milvian Bridge in 312 CE.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43
Roman coins and inscriptions from Maxentius's reign

events.certainty_note: Maxentius's proclamation and seizure of power in 306 CE are well-attested in numismatic evidence, inscriptions, and multiple historical sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: 312-001 (Battle of Milvian Bridge; Maxentius defeated and killed)

events.title: War of the Eight Princes Ends; Western Jin Dynasty Continues Decline
events.description: The War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE), a devastating civil war within the Western Jin Dynasty, comes to an end in 306 CE. The war has exhausted the central government's authority and military resources, leaving the empire vulnerable to warlordism and non-Han migration. The victory of one faction does not restore central authority; instead, regional warlords continue to exercise de facto independence. The end of the War of the Eight Princes marks the beginning of the final phase of Western Jin collapse (306–316 CE). Large-scale migration of non-Han peoples (Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang) into North China accelerates, driven by climate deterioration and the collapse of Jin administrative control. These migrations will lead to the establishment of the Sixteen Kingdoms (304–439 CE) in North China.
key_figures: Various princes and warlords involved in the war

sources.name: Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled 7th century CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century CE
Archaeological evidence from tomb inscriptions and ceramic remains

events.certainty_note: The end of the War of the Eight Princes in 306 CE is attested in Chinese historical sources. The date is secure, though details of the war's conclusion are compiled centuries later.

events.title: Galerius Becomes Senior Emperor; Diocletian and Maximian Retire
events.description: Galerius (r. 305–311 CE) becomes the senior emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire following the retirement of Diocletian (r. 284–305 CE) and Maximian (r. 286–305 CE). Constantius I Chlorus (r. 305–306 CE) becomes the senior Western emperor, but he dies in 306 CE, leading to the succession crisis that results in Constantine I's proclamation. Galerius rules from Nicomedia in Bithynia and is the primary architect of the Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE). His reign is marked by conflict with Constantine I and Maxentius in the West and by the rise of Licinius and Maximinus Daia as competing powers in the East.
key_figures: Constantius I Chlorus (Western emperor, r. 305–306 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 1–15
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 1–26
Roman coins and inscriptions from Galerius's reign

events.certainty_note: Galerius's accession as senior emperor in 305 CE (not 306 CE) is well-attested in numismatic evidence and historical sources. The date 305 CE is secure.

events.title: Great Persecution of Christians Continues Under Galerius and Maximinus Daia
events.description: The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE), initiated by Diocletian in 303 CE, continues under Galerius and Maximinus Daia. The persecution involves the destruction of churches, confiscation of Christian property, forced labor, torture, and execution of Christian leaders and believers. The persecution is particularly severe in the Eastern provinces under Maximinus Daia, who uses religious persecution as a tool of political control. In the Western provinces under Constantine I, persecution is less severe, and Constantine gradually becomes associated with Christian symbols and advisors. The persecution will continue until Galerius issues an Edict of Toleration in 310/311 CE and Constantine and Licinius issue the Edict of Milan in 313 CE.
key_figures: Maximinus Daia (Caesar and later Augustus, r. 305–313 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 1–15
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 1–26

events.certainty_note: The Great Persecution is well-attested in multiple historical sources, though the specific intensity and scope in 306 CE are difficult to determine. Sources are written decades later and may exaggerate or minimize persecution.

events.title: Hormizd II Dies; Shapur II Ascends to Sassanid Throne (traditional dating)
events.description: Hormizd II (r. 303–309 CE), Sassanid Shah, dies, and Shapur II (r. 309–379 CE) ascends to the throne. According to Sassanid tradition, Shapur II was crowned while still in the womb, making him the only Sassanid ruler crowned before birth. The exact date of Hormizd II's death is uncertain; some sources place it in 306 CE, while others place it in 309 CE. Shapur II's accession marks the beginning of a 70-year reign that will see the Sassanid Empire reach its zenith. Shapur II will conduct major military campaigns against Rome, consolidate Zoroastrian orthodoxy, and expand Sassanid territorial control.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled 9th century CE

events.certainty_note: The date of Hormizd II's death and Shapur II's accession is uncertain. Sources place it between 306 and 309 CE. The date 309 CE is more commonly accepted by modern scholars, but 306 CE is also attested in some sources. The tradition of Shapur II's coronation in the womb is likely legendary.
cross_references: 309-001 (Shapur II crowned Sassanid Shah, alternative dating)
337-001 (Constantine I dies; Shapur II campaigns against Rome)

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Constantine as a 'Christian emperor' by 306 CE is anachronistic; he was pragmatic about religion and did not formally convert until near his death (337 CE). The religious framing of Constantine's rise is largely a later Christian narrative constructed by Eusebius and other Christian sources. The portrayal of Maxentius as a 'pagan tyrant' opposed by the 'Christian' Constantine is a Christian apologetic narrative; both rulers were pragmatic about religious policy. The date of Shapur II's accession is disputed; some sources place it in 306 CE, while others place it in 309 CE. The tradition of Shapur II's coronation in the womb is likely legendary or symbolic rather than historical. The extent of Christian persecution in 306 CE is difficult to assess; sources written decades later may exaggerate the severity.
historiographic_note: The year 306 CE is richly documented for the Roman Empire through numismatic evidence, inscriptions, and near-contemporary chronicles (Eusebius, Lactantius), though these sources were written 20–30 years after events and carry Christian apologetic bias. For China, sources are detailed but compiled centuries later (7th–11th centuries CE) and may contain legendary elements. For Sassanid Persia, evidence is primarily numismatic and from much later Persian chronicles. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian elites; sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from surviving written sources. The year 306 CE marks a pivotal transition in multiple empires and is one of the best-documented years of the early 4th century CE.

Maxentius's seizure of power in 306 CE led to his conflict with Constantine and his defeat in 312 CE

era_context: The Roman Empire under the Tetrarchy of Diocletian and Maximian, with Constantius Chlorus and Galerius as Caesars. The Great Persecution of Christians is underway across the empire. In the East, the Sassanid Persian Empire under Narses I faces internal instability. China is fragmented during the Western Jin Dynasty's decline, with the War of the Eight Princes ongoing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record)
Mesoamerica (no written records of this period)

events.title: Diocletian and Maximian Abdicate; Tetrarchy Restructured
events.description: On 1 May 305 CE, Roman Emperors Diocletian and Maximian simultaneously abdicated, implementing the planned succession system of the Tetrarchy. Constantius Chlorus was promoted to Augustus in the West, and Galerius to Augustus in the East. Severus and Maximinus Daia were appointed as new Caesars. This was the first voluntary abdication by a reigning Roman emperor and was intended to ensure orderly succession and prevent civil war. However, the arrangement proved unstable, as Constantius's son Constantine and Maxentius (son of Maximian) would soon challenge the system.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine and Ecclesiastical History
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: The date of 1 May 305 is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary sources and later historiographic consensus. The abdication is one of the most documented events of this period.

events.title: Intensification of the Great Persecution of Christians
events.description: Following Diocletian's edict of 303 CE, the persecution of Christians intensified in 305 CE, particularly under Galerius in the Eastern provinces. Martyrdoms increased across Egypt, Syria, Palestine, and Asia Minor. Churches were destroyed, Christian texts burned, and clergy imprisoned or executed. The persecution was framed as a restoration of traditional Roman piety and was supported by pagan intellectuals. This period produced some of the most celebrated Christian martyrs, whose acts were later recorded in hagiographic literature.
key_figures: Various Christian martyrs (names recorded in later hagiographies)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Ecclesiastical History and Martyrs of Palestine
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: The Great Persecution (303–311 CE) is well-documented in Christian sources, though the intensity and scope varied by region. The year 305 falls within the confirmed period of active persecution.

events.title: War of the Eight Princes Continues in Western Jin China
events.description: The War of the Eight Princes, which began in 291 CE, continued to devastate the Western Jin Dynasty in 305 CE. This civil conflict between imperial princes over succession and power weakened central authority and depleted state resources. The war created conditions for the subsequent invasion of northern China by non-Han peoples and contributed to the fragmentation that would lead to the period of the Sixteen Kingdoms. By 305, the Jin court had lost effective control over much of the empire.
key_figures: Emperor Hui of Jin (nominal ruler, mentally incapacitated)

sources.name: Sima Guang, Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The War of the Eight Princes is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 305 falls within the confirmed period of the conflict.

events.title: Narses I of Sassanid Persia Faces Internal Instability
events.description: King Narses I (r. 293–302 CE) had recently died, and his successor faced significant internal challenges. The Sassanid nobility was fractious, and the empire was experiencing economic strain from military campaigns and administrative costs. The period saw competition among the great noble families (the Seven Great Houses) for influence over the throne. This instability would contribute to Sassanid vulnerability in subsequent decades.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-l-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: Sassanid chronology for this period is less precisely dated than Roman records. The general instability is confirmed, but specific events in 305 are not sharply documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The abdication of Diocletian in 305 is sometimes portrayed as a smooth, orderly transition, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Tetrarchic system was already under strain. Diocletian's retirement did not prevent the civil wars that followed (306–312 CE), suggesting the system was fundamentally flawed. Additionally, the intensity of Christian persecution in 305 is sometimes exaggerated in later Christian sources; regional variation was significant, and some areas experienced less severe enforcement than others.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 305 CE is relatively rich for the Roman Empire and China, drawing on near-contemporary chronicles and later compilations. However, sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social history are less well-documented. The perspective is predominantly from elite sources (imperial courts, Christian clergy). Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and most of the Americas have no written records for this year. Sassanid sources are limited and often transmitted through later Islamic historians.

graph_edges.note: Diocletian's abdication and the Tetrarchic succession plan directly led to Constantine's proclamation and the subsequent civil wars.
The persecution of Christians in 305 is part of the broader Great Persecution (303–311 CE).
The War of the Eight Princes weakened Jin authority and enabled the invasion of northern China by non-Han peoples.

era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy under Diocletian and Maximian continues to govern the empire. The Great Persecution of Christians is in its second year following the edict of 303 CE. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in the midst of the War of the Eight Princes. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Narses I is in its final years.

events.title: Continuation of Christian Persecution Across Roman Empire
events.description: The persecution of Christians continued throughout 304 CE, following Diocletian's edict of 303 CE. In Egypt, particularly in Alexandria and the Nile Valley, Christian communities faced severe repression. Churches were demolished, Christian texts destroyed, and believers were forced to choose between apostasy and martyrdom. The persecution was most intense in the Eastern provinces under Galerius's jurisdiction. This year saw the martyrdom of numerous Christians whose names were recorded in later hagiographic traditions.
key_figures: Christian martyrs (names preserved in later sources)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Ecclesiastical History and Martyrs of Palestine

events.certainty_note: The Great Persecution is well-attested in Christian sources. The year 304 falls within the documented period of active persecution.

events.title: War of the Eight Princes Escalates in Western Jin
events.description: The War of the Eight Princes, ongoing since 291 CE, continued to escalate in 304 CE. The conflict between imperial princes for control of the Jin court and succession to the throne intensified, with major military campaigns across northern and central China. The war caused widespread destruction, displaced populations, and weakened the Jin state's ability to defend its borders. This instability would soon enable the invasion of northern China by the Xiongnu and other non-Han peoples.
key_figures: Various princes (Sima Lun, Sima Ying, Sima Wei, and others)
events.certainty_note: The War of the Eight Princes is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 304 falls within the confirmed period of escalation.

events.title: Narses I of Sassanid Persia Dies; Succession Disputed
events.description: King Narses I of the Sassanid Empire died in 302 or 303 CE (sources vary), and by 304 CE the succession was contested among the Sassanid nobility. The death of Narses I marked the end of a period of relative stability and military success. His successor faced challenges from the great noble families and external pressures from the Roman Empire and nomadic peoples. The succession dispute weakened Sassanid authority and contributed to a period of internal instability.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Sassanid chronology for this period is uncertain. The death of Narses I is dated variously to 302–303 CE in different sources. The succession dispute is confirmed but not precisely dated to 304.

disconfirming_evidence: The Great Persecution is sometimes portrayed as uniformly severe across the empire, but modern scholarship shows significant regional variation. In the Western provinces under Maximian, enforcement was often less rigorous than in the East under Galerius. Additionally, some Christian communities negotiated compromises or went underground rather than facing martyrdom, complicating the narrative of universal persecution. The War of the Eight Princes is sometimes presented as a simple succession dispute, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex factional interests of the great noble families and regional power bases.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 304 CE is moderate for the Roman Empire and China, relying on near-contemporary chronicles and later compilations. Christian sources provide detailed accounts of persecution, but these are often hagiographic and may exaggerate the scope or intensity of events. Chinese sources are more institutional and political in focus. Sassanid sources are limited and mediated through later Islamic historians. The perspective is predominantly elite and institutional; the experiences of ordinary people are largely absent from the record.

graph_edges.note: The persecution in 304 is part of the broader Great Persecution (303–311 CE).
The escalation of the War of the Eight Princes in 304 directly contributed to the conditions that enabled the invasion of northern China.
The succession dispute following Narses I's death contributed to Sassanid instability in the early 4th century.

era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy under Diocletian and Maximian is at the height of its power and organization. Diocletian initiates the Great Persecution of Christians, marking a major shift in imperial religious policy. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in the midst of the War of the Eight Princes. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Narses I is in its final years.

events.title: Diocletian Issues Edict Against Christians
events.description: In 303 CE, Roman Emperor Diocletian issued an edict ordering the destruction of Christian churches, the burning of Christian texts, and the removal of Christians from positions of authority. This marked the beginning of the Great Persecution, the most severe and systematic persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire. The edict was issued at Diocletian's palace in Nicomedia (in Bithynia, modern-day Turkey) and was enforced with varying intensity across the empire, most severely in the Eastern provinces under Galerius's jurisdiction. The persecution would continue until 311 CE and produced numerous Christian martyrs whose acts were later recorded in hagiographic literature.
key_figures: Maximian (enforcer in the West, though less rigorously)
Constantius Chlorus (Caesar in the West, reportedly less enthusiastic about enforcement)

sources.name: Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: The edict of 303 CE is one of the most well-documented events of this period, attested in multiple near-contemporary and later Christian sources. The date and basic facts are not disputed.

events.title: War of the Eight Princes Continues in Western Jin China
events.description: The War of the Eight Princes, which began in 291 CE, continued throughout 303 CE. This civil conflict between imperial princes over succession and control of the Jin court was devastating the empire. Major military campaigns were fought across northern and central China, with armies commanded by various princes competing for power. The war caused widespread destruction, displaced populations, and depleted state resources. By 303, the Jin central government had lost effective control over much of the empire, creating conditions for the subsequent invasion of northern China by non-Han peoples.
key_figures: Emperor Hui of Jin (nominal ruler, mentally incapacitated)
Various princes competing for power (Sima Lun, Sima Ying, Sima Wei, and others)
events.certainty_note: The War of the Eight Princes is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 303 falls within the confirmed period of the conflict.

events.title: Narses I of Sassanid Persia Dies
events.description: King Narses I of the Sassanid Empire died in 302 or 303 CE (sources vary on the exact year). Narses I had ruled for approximately nine years (r. 293–302/303 CE) and had pursued military campaigns against the Romans and internal consolidation. His death marked the end of a period of relative stability and military success. The succession was contested among the Sassanid nobility, with the great noble families competing for influence over the throne. This succession dispute weakened Sassanid authority and contributed to a period of internal instability that would last for several decades.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Sassanid chronology for this period is uncertain. The death of Narses I is dated variously to 302–303 CE in different sources. The exact year cannot be determined with precision from available sources.

events.title: Earthquake in Antioch and Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: A major earthquake struck Antioch and the surrounding regions of the Eastern Mediterranean in 303 CE. The earthquake caused significant damage to cities and infrastructure, particularly in Syria and Palestine. Antioch, one of the largest cities in the Roman Empire, suffered considerable destruction. The earthquake is recorded in several ancient sources and is sometimes associated with the beginning of the Great Persecution, as some Christian sources interpreted it as divine judgment. The earthquake contributed to the economic and social disruption of the region during this period.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from excavations in Antioch and other Eastern Mediterranean sites

events.certainty_note: The earthquake is attested in multiple ancient sources, but the exact date (303 CE) is not universally agreed upon. Some sources place it slightly earlier or later. Archaeological evidence supports the occurrence of a major seismic event in this period.
cross_references: 303-001 (Edict of Diocletian, sometimes associated with the earthquake)

disconfirming_evidence: The Great Persecution is sometimes portrayed as a unified, empire-wide campaign, but modern scholarship emphasizes significant regional variation. In the Western provinces under Maximian and Constantius Chlorus, enforcement was often less rigorous than in the East under Galerius. Some Christian communities negotiated compromises or went underground rather than facing martyrdom. Additionally, the edict of 303 was not the first imperial action against Christians; earlier emperors had issued edicts, and the 303 edict was more systematic and severe than its predecessors. The earthquake in Antioch is sometimes dated to 303 CE, but some sources place it in 300 CE or 306 CE, and the exact date remains uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 303 CE is rich for the Roman Empire, with multiple near-contemporary and later sources providing detailed accounts of the edict and the beginning of the Great Persecution. Christian sources are particularly abundant, though they are often hagiographic and may exaggerate the scope or intensity of persecution. Chinese sources provide good documentation of the War of the Eight Princes. Sassanid sources are limited and mediated through later Islamic historians. The perspective is predominantly elite and institutional; the experiences of ordinary people are largely absent from the record. The earthquake is documented in ancient sources but with some uncertainty about the exact date.

graph_edges.note: The edict of 303 directly led to the continuation and intensification of Christian persecution in subsequent years.
The edict of 303 marks the beginning of the Great Persecution (303–311 CE).
The War of the Eight Princes in 303 directly contributed to the conditions that enabled the invasion of northern China.
The earthquake in Antioch occurred around the same time as the edict of Diocletian, and some sources associate the two events.

era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy under Diocletian and Maximian is at the height of its power and organization. Diocletian is preparing the edicts against Christians that will be issued in 303 CE. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in the midst of the War of the Eight Princes. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Narses I is in its final years.

events.title: Diocletian Prepares Persecution of Christians
events.description: In 302 CE, Roman Emperor Diocletian, influenced by his Caesar Galerius and pagan advisors, began preparations for a systematic persecution of Christians. Diocletian had previously tolerated Christians and even employed them in his administration, but he was now convinced that Christian refusal to participate in traditional religious practices and military service threatened the stability of the empire. The persecution would be formally initiated by edict in 303 CE. The decision to persecute Christians represented a major shift in imperial religious policy and was the most severe and systematic persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire.

sources.name: Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: The preparations for persecution in 302 are attested in Christian sources, but the exact timeline is not entirely clear. The edict was formally issued in 303 CE, and the preparations likely occurred in 302 CE or earlier.

events.title: War of the Eight Princes Continues in Western Jin China
events.description: The War of the Eight Princes, which began in 291 CE, continued throughout 302 CE. This civil conflict between imperial princes over succession and control of the Jin court was devastating the empire. Major military campaigns were fought across northern and central China, with armies commanded by various princes competing for power. The war caused widespread destruction, displaced populations, and depleted state resources. By 302, the Jin central government had lost effective control over much of the empire.
key_figures: Emperor Hui of Jin (nominal ruler, mentally incapacitated)
events.certainty_note: The War of the Eight Princes is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 302 falls within the confirmed period of the conflict.

events.title: Narses I of Sassanid Persia Dies
events.description: King Narses I of the Sassanid Empire died in 302 CE (some sources date his death to 303 CE). Narses I had ruled for approximately nine years (r. 293–302 CE) and had pursued military campaigns against the Romans and internal consolidation. His death marked the end of a period of relative stability and military success. The succession was contested among the Sassanid nobility, with the great noble families competing for influence over the throne. This succession dispute weakened Sassanid authority and contributed to a period of internal instability.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Sassanid chronology for this period is uncertain. The death of Narses I is dated variously to 302–303 CE in different sources. The exact year cannot be determined with precision.

disconfirming_evidence: The preparations for the persecution of Christians in 302 CE are sometimes portrayed as a sudden decision by Diocletian, but modern scholarship suggests that anti-Christian sentiment had been building for some time. Additionally, Diocletian's earlier tolerance of Christians is sometimes exaggerated; he had already issued edicts limiting Christian privileges and property ownership. The War of the Eight Princes is sometimes presented as a simple succession dispute, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex factional interests of the great noble families.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 302 CE is moderate for the Roman Empire and China, relying on near-contemporary chronicles and later compilations. Christian sources provide accounts of the preparations for persecution, but these are often written after the fact and may not accurately reflect the timeline of events. Chinese sources are more institutional and political in focus. Sassanid sources are limited and mediated through later Islamic historians. The perspective is predominantly elite and institutional; the experiences of ordinary people are largely absent from the record.

graph_edges.note: The preparations for persecution in 302 directly led to the edict of Diocletian in 303 CE.
The continuation of the War of the Eight Princes in 302 directly contributed to the conditions that enabled the invasion of northern China.
The death of Narses I in 302 led to the succession dispute that weakened Sassanid authority.

era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy under Diocletian and Maximian is at the height of its power and organization. Diocletian is consolidating his administrative reforms and military victories. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in the midst of the War of the Eight Princes. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Narses I is in its final years.

events.title: Diocletian Issues Edict on Maximum Prices (Edictum de Pretiis)
events.description: In 301 CE, Roman Emperor Diocletian issued the Edictum de Pretiis (Edict on Maximum Prices), an attempt to control inflation and stabilize the economy. The edict set maximum prices for hundreds of goods and services across the empire, from grain and wine to clothing and labor. The edict was issued in response to severe inflation caused by military spending, debasement of the currency, and economic disruption. However, the edict proved largely ineffective; merchants circumvented price controls by hoarding goods or withdrawing from the market, and the edict was eventually abandoned. The edict is one of the most detailed economic documents from the Roman Empire and provides valuable information about prices, wages, and economic conditions in the early 4th century.

sources.name: Edict on Maximum Prices (Edictum de Pretiis) - surviving inscriptions and fragments
Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: The Edict on Maximum Prices is confirmed by surviving inscriptions and fragments found throughout the empire. The date of 301 CE is well-established.

events.title: War of the Eight Princes Continues in Western Jin China
events.description: The War of the Eight Princes, which began in 291 CE, continued throughout 301 CE. This civil conflict between imperial princes over succession and control of the Jin court was devastating the empire. Major military campaigns were fought across northern and central China, with armies commanded by various princes competing for power. The war caused widespread destruction, displaced populations, and depleted state resources. By 301, the Jin central government had lost effective control over much of the empire, creating conditions for the subsequent invasion of northern China by non-Han peoples.
key_figures: Emperor Hui of Jin (nominal ruler, mentally incapacitated)
events.certainty_note: The War of the Eight Princes is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 301 falls within the confirmed period of the conflict.

events.title: Narses I of Sassanid Persia Rules
events.description: King Narses I of the Sassanid Empire continued to rule in 301 CE. Narses I had ruled for approximately eight years (r. 293–302/303 CE) and had pursued military campaigns against the Romans and internal consolidation. He was consolidating Sassanid power and attempting to strengthen the empire's military and administrative structures. His reign was relatively stable compared to the succession disputes that would follow his death.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Narses I's rule during this period is confirmed by Sassanid sources and later Islamic historians. The year 301 falls within his confirmed reign.

disconfirming_evidence: The Edict on Maximum Prices is sometimes portrayed as a well-intentioned but ineffective policy, but modern scholarship suggests that Diocletian may have been aware of its likely ineffectiveness and issued it primarily for propaganda purposes—to demonstrate imperial concern for the welfare of the people. The edict's failure is sometimes attributed to merchant resistance, but modern economic analysis suggests that the fundamental problem was that price controls cannot address underlying inflation caused by monetary debasement. The War of the Eight Princes is sometimes presented as a simple succession dispute, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex factional interests of the great noble families and regional power bases.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 301 CE is moderate for the Roman Empire and China. The Edict on Maximum Prices is exceptionally well-documented through surviving inscriptions, providing detailed economic information. Chinese sources provide good documentation of the War of the Eight Princes. Sassanid sources are limited and mediated through later Islamic historians. The perspective is predominantly elite and institutional; the experiences of ordinary people are largely absent from the record, except insofar as they are reflected in the price controls of the edict.

graph_edges.note: The Edict on Maximum Prices is part of Diocletian's broader program of administrative and economic reform during the Tetrarchy.
The continuation of the War of the Eight Princes in 301 directly contributed to the conditions that enabled the invasion of northern China.
Narses I's rule in 301 was followed by his death in 302 or 303 CE, which led to succession disputes.
